192. While the calculated pollutant concentrations are within the allowable limits of Decision
No. 3733/2002/QD-BYT, prolonged exposure can cause adverse health effects.
193. Comments: Dust and air emissions during construction will primarily arise from
demolition, transportation of materials by boat, soil excavation and filling, concrete mixing,
and welding. Emission loads are generally low to moderate, air pollutant contents would
remain within QCVN 05:2023, except NOₓ may exceed the WHO 2021 guideline values
intermittently at some specific locations on the waterway. The groups exposed the most to dust
and air emissions are construction workers and a small number of residents near the
construction sites, but exposures are short-term and localized. Overall, the impacts are
assessed as low and can be effectively controlled by mitigation measures in Chapter 5.

e) Noise and Vibration

e.1). Noise
194. Noise urging construction is usually generated by (i) vehicles; and (ii) construction
equipment and tools. However, the noise level gradually decreases with distance and can be
determined by the following formula:
                                ΔL = 20lg (r2/r1)1 + a (dB)
Where:
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                            Page            100
                      ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

        ∆L: reduction of noise at distance r2 compared with source r1; distance for noise source normally considered
        as r1= 8 m;
        a: noise absorption of the area
195. Transportation of construction materials: Although boats of 200 tons for construction
material transportation generate noise levels of 80–100 dBA, attenuation reduces this to
approximately 60.5 dBA at 50 m and 52.5 dBA at 20m. The rivers, and canals for transportation
include Rach Goc river, Bay Hap river, Xang-Cau Sat canal, Ngang canal, etc. As the boats
move, the duration of noise impact on an object is very short, only around 1-2 minutes, and
only a few people are living along the transportation routes, the noise impact from material
transportation is considered low.
196. Port Upgrading: As mentioned in Table 6, at least 18 construction plants will be used
for the port. The maximum noise level at different distances due to their operation is presented
in Table 55.

197. Noise from some operating equipment, such as pile drivers, drilling machines, and
grinding machines likely to exceed the QCVN 26:2025/BTNMT at nighttime (55 dBA limit) at
distances of 20–50m; noise from truck movement would slightly exceed the limit at closer
ranges. Compared with the IFC guideline (55 dBA daytime for residential areas), many


10
     QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT – National technical regulation on noise.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                             Page          101
                       ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

machines, such as pile drivers, drills, grinders, trucks, cranes, vibrating compactors, and air
compressors, also surpass the standard. According to the WHO (2011) guidelines, most
equipment generates noise well above the recommended thresholds for sleep and learning,
indicating a risk of disturbance and potential health impacts if operations occur near residential
areas. Noise levels at construction sites are typically generated by at least two types of
machinery operating at the same time. The calculated noise levels during the construction phase
due to concrete mixing and pile driving are shown in Figure 31.
                                                             Noise (dB)
      Noise (dB)




                   Distance from noise source (m)                         Distance from noise source (m)



               (a) Concrete mixer                                  (b) Pile Driving
                                       Figure 31: Noise Attenuation over Distance
198. The largest noise source is pile driving. Driving of each pile may take about 1 hour,
depending on the pile length. As shown in the diagram above, the noise level at distances of 10,
20, and 50 m from the pile driving site would be 105, 100, and 85 dBA, respectively. At 100
m, noise levels are below the national daytime standard but still exceed the national nighttime
limit as well as the IFC and WHO guideline values. People who are exposed to 85 dBA of
noise for an extended amount of time (4 hours per day over 1 month of pile driving) will
experience exhaustion and negative feelings, and if the noise exceeds 90 dBA, their hearing
will be damaged.
Noise Impacts
Noise-induced hearing loss can be caused by exposure to loud noises (either a single experience or over
time) and is associated with a permanent shift in thresholds.
Tinnitus can be caused by exposure to excessive noise and is characterized as a temporary or permanent
ringing or buzzing in the ear. It causes a disorder of the perception of sound.
Hypertension and increased risk of coronary artery disease can be caused by noise. Noise has been
shown to increase blood pressure in individuals and can even result in elevated adrenaline levels.
Exposure limit value: An employer must not expose employees to noise above the exposure limit value
(87dB(A) LEP,d). This limit takes account of the protection provided by hearing protection. If the
employer does exceed this limit, exposure must be stopped.
199. Rach Goc Port. Affected objects of the noise generated by port construction machinery
and tools are on-site workers of the project (20 people), other people working and trading at the
port (27 people), and nearby people (Figure 32). The impacts are not avoidable, at a moderate
level, intermittent, and can be controlled through the mitigation measures applicable to the
detailed design/ construction specified in Chapter 5.

203. Dredging can take place at daytime only. Dregers can generate high noise levels, reaching
about 101 dBA at 1.5 m and 70.7 dBA at 50 m, decreasing to 64.7 dBA at 100 m and 51.1 dBA
at 150 m. Daytime limits (70 dBA) are exceeded within 50m. WHO guidelines (30–40 dBA)
are exceeded at a distance of 150m. The most affected are workers and residents within 50 m,
with impacts dropping sharply beyond 100 m. However, noise impacts are small, intermittent,
and short-term as dredging lasts only 7–20 days per canal segment, and the impacts can be
reduced by implementing the mitigation measures outlined in Chapter 5.

e.2). Vibration
205. Most vibrations on construction sites come from heavy machinery like pile drivers,
bulldozers, excavators, rollers, other hand-held equipment, etc., used for tasks like leveling,
compacting, etc., affecting the people who operate the machines or are present nearby.
Vibrations may also cause damage and disruption to existing structures near construction sites.
According to the Transit Noise and Vibration Impact Assessment study (US, Federal Transit
Administration, 2006), which details the vibration levels of vehicles, machinery, and
equipment, the following formula is used to predict the amount of vibration decreasing with
distance:
                             L = L0– 10lg (r/r0) – 8.7a (r – r0) (dB)
Where:
 − L: vibration in dB at a distance “r” meters away from the source;
 − Lo: vibration in dB measured at a distance “r0” meters from the source. Vibration at a distance of r 0=10 m is
   generally acknowledged as the source vibration.
 − a: the intrinsic vibration reduction coefficient on clay ground of about 0.5.

206. Table 59 summarizes the estimated vibration levels of heavy equipment and machinery.
                       Table 59: Vibration by Distance of Heavy Construction Plants
     N            Equipment           At Source                                     Distance
     o                                 ro=10m
                                                       r=12m             r=14m             r=16m              r=18m

                                    dB      mm/s    dB      mm/s      dB       mm/s      dB     mm/s     dB         mm/s
     1   Excavator                   80      1.72   70.5     0.58     61.1      0.2      51.9    0.07    42.6       0.02
     2   Bulldozer                   79      1.53   69.5     0.51     60.1     0.17      50.9    0.06    41.6       0.02
     3   Pile driver                104     16.35   93.5     5.13     83.1     2.35      74.9    0.86    65.6       0.30
     4   Roller                      84      2.17   72.5     0.73     63.1     0.25      53.9    0.08    44.6       0.03

         QCVN 27: 2025/BNNMT11                                               75.0
Source: U.S. Environmental Protection Committee Vibration from construction equipment and construction
machinery, NJID, 300.1, 31 – 12 – 197
207. Upgradation of the port: At Rach Goc fishing port site, pile drivings are expected to
generate vibration levels with Laeq at 104 dB and Lveq at 16.35mm/s exceeding the permissible
limits, particularly within 15m of the source. Other equipment, such as the excavator, bulldozer,
roller, and air compressor, generates vibration levels near or below the 75 dB limit at distances



11
     QCVN 27:2025/BTNMT – National technical regulation on vibration.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                          Page          105
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

of 12m or more. Affected objects of the vibration are nearby structures and people (workers,
port staff, shop and boat owners, totaling 27).
  − Human health impacts: Exceeding construction vibrations can affect human health, causing
    fatigue, headaches, vestibular imbalance, low back pain, musculoskeletal disorders, and
    hand-arm vibration syndrome (e.g., finger blanching and numbness) with frequent or
    prolonged exposure. The risks depend on vibration intensity, frequency content, daily
    exposure duration, and whether the exposure is whole-body or hand-arm. Long-term
    exposure may lead to spinal and postural disorders, circulatory and sensorineural problems,
    and sleep disturbance or stress in nearby residents. However, vibration-generating
    machinery operates intermittently, typically 2–3 hours per day over the 12-month
    construction period. Therefore, the impacts are considered moderate, as they are intermittent
    but repeatable during construction and end upon project completion. These impacts are
    managed through mitigation measures outlined in Chapter 5.
  − Structural impacts: The impacts and risks are discussed in detail in the type/site-specific
    impacts and risks (section 4.4.2.1.c)
208. Embankment Upgrading: The main vibration sources include excavators, pull dozers,
etc. Affected objects of the vibration are the nearby structure and people:
  − Human health impacts: Based on Table 59, at a distance of 12 meters from the source, the
    vibration level meets the permissible standard on human health, comfort, and perception.
    Therefore, impacts on human health and comfort are considered small, causing only
    temporary disturbance such as noise annoyance without long-term health effects.
  − Impacts on structures: The level of impacts of vibration on the stability of the existing
    structures depends on the intensity, frequency of vibration, and the existing condition of the
    structure, the ground foundation, etc. The embankment will be upgraded using the rolling
    method, with each 100-meter section completed in approximately 7 days, and only pulling
    dozers, excavators cause vibration. In addition, within a 12-meter radius from the
    embankment centerline, there are 352 houses, 47 facilities (coffee shops, stationery shops,
    small shrimp collection shops, karaoke club, etc.), and 2 schools. Survey results indicate that
    the local houses include thatched structures, houses with corrugated iron walls and roofs,
    brick-walled houses with corrugated iron roofs, and reinforced concrete houses also roofed
    with corrugated iron (Figure 20). Vibration levels below 75 dB would not cause structural
    damage but could pose a small risk of minor cosmetic cracks in older or brittle constructions.
    Therefore, the impacts are small, short-term, and can be mitigated by the measures
    (including controlling construction methods and intensity) during construction.
209. Canal dredging: The equipment used for canal dredging mainly includes barges and
dredgers. Based on Table 59, vibration levels within a 12-m distance meet acceptable standards.
It can be observed that there are no houses within 12m of the dredging location. Therefore, the
impact of vibration from dredging activities is low, as each canal section is dredged in a short
period (around 7–20 days), and the dredging will proceed along the canal alignment, resulting
in a short-term impact at any given location. Moreover, these canals are regularly dredged every
3–5 years using similar equipment, and no recorded impacts due to vibration on these structures
have been reported in the past.
210. Comments: Considering the intermittent nature of the work, the distance of structures
from the source, and the limited number of nearby structures and people, the overall vibration
impacts and risks are considered small to moderate. These are manageable through the
mitigation measures presented in Chapter 5.

215. Domestic wastewater contains suspended solids, oil and grease, organic matter (BOD₅
and COD), nutrients (nitrogen and phosphorus), and microorganisms. The pollutant
concentrations in domestic wastewater are significantly higher than the discharge standards
permitted by QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT (Column B). Elevated nitrogen and phosphorus levels
in wastewater can trigger eutrophication, fostering excessive algae growth in water bodies.
Additionally, wastewater contains high concentrations of pathogens and microorganisms,
increasing the risk of gastrointestinal diseases such as diarrhea, dysentery, food poisoning, and
jaundice. Therefore, wastewater should not be discharged directly into the environment without
any prior treatment.

216. Port Upgrading: As mentioned above, the Rach Goc Port is situated at the confluence
of the Rach Goc River and the Mot Canal. The Rach Goc river section at the Port is from 80 to
150 meters wide, and 4 to 8 meters deep. The Mot Canal is 30–50 meters wide and 4 to 5 meters
deep. Currently, water quality in Rach Goc river and Mot Canal is quite good, meeting the
requirements of QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (Column B1) for irrigation purposes. Parameters
such as pH, DO, BOD, TSS, total nitrogen, total phosphorus, and Coliform are all within the
permissible limits. The port workforce consists of 20 workers. The Port Authority informed
that there was the Port has a vacant building with adequate sanitation facilities, and contractors
can hire some rooms to use as an office and accommodation (Figure 34). The volume of
domestic wastewater generated from the workers’ camp is relatively small, approximately
0.96m³/day, lasting for 12 months of the construction period. Besides, the impacts of
wastewater can be effectively mitigated by the measures presented in Chapter 5.




                               Figure 34: Vacant building at the port
217. Embankment Upgrading: The amount of wastewater generated from a campsite is only
0.38m3/day, very small. Mitigation measures will be implemented to minimize impacts of
wastewater generation, as detailed in Chapter 5.
218. Canal dredging: the canals to be dredged are from 10–15 meters wide, 4-5 meters deep.
At present, the water quality in the canals complies with irrigation standards (QCVN
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          108
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

08:2023/BTNMT (Column B1)), with pH, DO, BOD, TSS, total nitrogen, total phosphorus,
and Coliform levels all within the allowable limits. Each dredging site will involve 6 workers,
including both local and migrant laborers, with migrant workers renting local houses equipped
with septic tanks. The amount of wastewater generated each day from each campsite would be
0.29 m3/day. These impacts can be effectively mitigated through the measures in Chapter 5.

b) Rainwater Runoff

219. According to WHO, the concentrations of pollutants in rainwater are N: 0.5–1.5 mg/L; P:
0.004–0.03 mg/L; COD: 10–20 mg/L; and TSS: 10–20 mg/L. Runoff passing construction sites
may cause increased turbidity and the concentration of other pollutants in the water bodies at
the receptors. The amount of runoff from a construction site is calculated via the following
formula:
                                      W0 = S x M/1000
Where: - W0: runoff water volume (m )
                                   3

           − S: surface area (m2). Most of these are material warehouses and construction yards, and only about
             20% are vacant land.
           − M: Maximum rainfall amount (mm), for the project area M=50mm

220. The project areas have potential acid sulfate soils (as shown in the map under section
2.1.5.1, although soil/sediment samples did not show low pH (likely the soil surface already
washed by rainwater). Given the substantial volume of earthworks at embankment and port
upgradation, the risk of acid release from disturbed soil is considered likely, and spread by
rainwater, negatively impacting water quality in nearby aquatic environments. While
earthworks should be carried out in the dry season, the potential impacts and risks related to
acid sulphate soil are manageable through the measures presented in Chapter 5.
221. Port Upgrading: The port's construction site spans approximately 12,456m2. All
activities underwater will happen in the coffer dams and boats with very small surface areas. In
addition, an inland construction area of approximately 10,000 m2 will be set up, as a dredged
material storage area of 8,763 m2, and other activities. The dredged material storage site of the
port has an inward slope, and rainwater runoff runs within the storage area before overflowing
outside.
222. The port would experience a 500m3/day overflow of runoff when the daily rainfall
reaches its maximum of 50 mm (Table 64).
                  Table 64: Estimation of Rainwater Runoff During Construction

223. Soils, discarded stone, sand, and oil and grease from construction plants may follow
rainwater to enter surface runoff. It should be noted that the existing wastewater treatment
station at the port has not been in operation, thus it will not be able to treat the polluted overflow
water, surface runoff will flow directly into the Rach Goc river and the Mot Canal (located on
the left of the Port), causing an increase in turbidity and pollutant contents of the river and
canal. The objects of the impact are water and aquatic life of Rach Goc river, Mot Canal, and
people working in the port and residents nearby. The impacts and risks related to rainwater
runoff at the Rach Goc Port would be limited as construction would last for 12 months, of which
6 months are dry season, and there are no rare/endangered species in the Rach Goc River. The

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                       Page            109
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

measures presented in Chapter 5 and the Dredging and Material Management Plan (Appendix
2) would help to mitigate, control the impacts of surface water runoff at the port site.
224. Embankment Upgrading: The embankment width ranges from 3-4.5m, and each
segment is 100m long. Therefore, the amount of rainwater overflowing for each 1000m section
is from 60-135m3 for a rainfall of 50mm. If there is granular/sand and oil on the construction
site surface, rainwater would wash these materials into shrimp ponds, gardens, and houses,
causing sedimentation and pollution in water bodies. As aquaculture ponds are popular while
potential acid sulphate soils exist in the project area, if rainwater runoff passes the disturbed
acid sulphate soils before entering the aquaculture ponds, it may cause death of shrimps and
plants along the embankments in the 5 communes of Tan Thuan, Ta An Khuong, Cai Nuoc,
Phu Tan, and Phu My. Increased water turbidity and/or acidity or other pollutants in water
bodies would affect freshwater ecosystems, causing harm to the fish and other aquatic life, such
as invertebrates, benthic fauna, and flora. The impact is assessed as medium level, short-term
term and mitigable. To prevent this, contractors will be required to carry out mitigation
measures provided in Chapter 5.
225. Dredging canals: Although dredging vehicles occupy a limited area, grease and oil from
their surfaces or leaking containers can be carried away by rainwater, potentially polluting
water and adversely affecting the aquatic ecosystem. However, the impact can be mitigated
through effective fuel storage management measures on the dredging equipment and other
measures in Chapter 5.

c) Construction Wastewater

226. Construction wastewater comprises wastewater generated from washing materials and
equipment, and machinery, including dredgers. Cleaning the concrete mixer after each working
shift generates wastewater containing cement residues and sand from the rotating drum. Each
cleaning requires approximately 30–50 liters of water per mixer per shift, resulting in 30–50
liters of wastewater per day per construction site (one shift per day). If discharged directly into
surrounding canals, this wastewater could have a small impact on the aquatic ecosystems of
affected waterways. Wastewater from the cleaning of construction equipment contains organic
compounds, oil, grease, and suspended particles, and varies in volume based on construction
equipment operations, as detailed in Table 65.
227. The direct impact of construction wastewater is considered low due to: (i) its small and
sporadically generated volume; (ii) its occurrence being limited to the construction period (3 to
24 months); and (iii) its generation being confined to construction sites across six communes.
Wastewater from routine machinery cleaning that is not contaminated with oil can be treated in
sedimentation tanks to remove solids such as soil and sand before being discharged into the
environment. Oil-contaminated wastewater from equipment maintenance will be treated using
an oil and grease separator prior to discharge, with the collected oil and grease stored in
appropriate containers and managed as hazardous waste. As the wastewater treatment facility
at the port is not operating, the Contractors will be required to collect and treat the wastewater
as presented in Chapter 5.

4.4.1.3. Solid Waste Generation

228. Solid waste is generated during construction, which includes (i) domestic waste from
workers' camps, (ii) construction waste, and (iii) hazardous waste.

a) Domestic wastes from Workers’ camps

229. Domestic garbage from camps comprises styrofoam boxes, plastic bags, vegetables,
plastic tubers, fruits, food leftovers, etc. 60% of this waste is organic and easily biodegradable.
Each worker generates 0.4kg/day (MONRE, 2019). With 6-20 workers at each site, the daily
2.4 to 8 kg of domestic waste would be generated at each site. The total amount of domestic
garbage generated daily from all construction site groups is around 34.4 kg (Table 66).
230. If not collected and disposed of properly, domestic waste could cause localized nuisance,
attract vectors of disease, and generate leachate that may affect nearby soil and water quality.
231. As mentioned above, currently, waste from the Rach Goc fishing port is collected and
transferred to the waste treatment plant in An Xuyen ward by the designated company. The
contractors should be required to contract a licensed service provider for the collection and
transportation of domestic waste from camps. In the construction area of embankments and
dredged canals, contractors should separate domestic waste at source (organic, recyclables, and
other waste) and arrange proper collection and disposal in compliance with regulations as
presented in Chapter 5.
232. Therefore, the health and environmental impacts of domestic waste generation are low,
short-term, and can be mitigated by the implementation of the measures in Chapter 5.

235. Other types of construction waste: In addition to demolition waste, construction solid
waste generated consists of scattered soil, sand, cement bags, and discarded formworks, as
follows:
  − Cement bags: According to Table 7, the project will use approximately 49,363.4 tons of
    cement, equivalent to 987,268 cement bags released into the environment. These bags could
    take decades to break down, and if improperly discarded into canals or production areas,
    they may negatively impact nearby water bodies and shrimp ponds. Cement bags are
    reusable (e.g., for seedlings at nurseries) thus would be collected by dealers.
  − Discarded formworks: At the moment, most builders are using iron or plastic formworks,
    and very few are using wooden ones. All iron or plastic formworks are collected because
    they can be reused or sold.

c) Hazardous Waste Generation

236. Hazardous waste generated during construction phase includes wasted oil, oily rags,
chemical containers (diesel oil, gasoline, etc.), and welding rods. Repair of construction
machinery and equipment might result in waste oil, empty oil containers, oil-contaminated rags,
etc. The volume of hazardous waste generated depends on the types and quantity of equipment
and machinery, the volume of materials/fuels consumed, the frequency of lubricant changes,
and machinery maintenance. According to the study conducted by the Military Science and

Technology Research Center-Ministry of National Defense, the amount of wasted oil
discharged from a change of oil is 7 liters per item. Therefore, the amount of waste oil and
grease of each project component is estimated as follows (Table 69):

    the waste will be collected and transported by a designated hazardous waste unit in
    compliance with current regulations.

    light bulbs and batteries, estimated at 2-5 kg per month per site. Without proper management,
    this waste can adversely impact soil, water, and air. Additionally, residual grease in
    containers may leak into the soil, causing pollution.
237. Improperly managed hazardous waste may cause two main impacts. First, small amounts
of oil and grease could be released from construction activities, potentially affecting nearby
aquatic species and hindering the growth of surface-dwelling aquatic plants and animals in the
immediate vicinity. Second, heavy metals in the waste could enter the food chain, posing
limited risks to human health. However, given that the quantities of oil and grease generated by
the project are low, any environmental release would be highly localized with small potential
for spreading. Therefore, the impact is considered low, localized, and manageable through
appropriate mitigation measures in Chapter 5.
238. Comments: The amount of hazardous waste generated at each site is small, localized,
and limited to the construction phase. If not managed properly, this waste may cause serious
environmental and health issues. This direct impact is assessed as moderate and can be
minimised with measures outlined in Chapter 5.

4.4.1.4. Excavated and Dredged materials

239. Approximately 569,416 m³ of dredged and excavated materials will be generated (Table
70). Based on sediment sample analysis from the port (Appendix 4), concentrations of heavy
metals are within the permissible limits under QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT – National Technical
Regulation on Sediment Quality. Therefore, the material is suitable for reuse as backfill.
32,593m³ of dredged materials from Rach Goc Fishing Port will require temporary storage for
later reuse. The remaining material will be reused immediately for embankment upgradation,
including 37,911 m³ to be transported off-site and 498,912 m³ to be reused on-site. Although
all materials are intended for reuse, potential impacts and risks may arise during the
transportation and interim storage period. These are assessed in detail in Section 4.4.2.1 and the
mitigation measures are presented in Chapter 5 and the Dredging and Material Management
Plan (Appendix 2) which complies with government Decree No. 57/2024/ND-CP issuing
regulations on dredging at sea ports and inland waterways and dredged material disposal and
relevant requirements specified in the WB’s ESS3 (Resource Efficiency and Pollution Control).
