4.4.1.5. Water Quality Reduction

240. Dredging activities will disturb sediments in the river and canal bed, leading to increased
turbidity. The causes include:
  − Some dredged material may fall from the bucket and disperse in the water as it moves from
    the canal bed to the surface.
  − Bottom mud is stirred, spread, and suspended from the canal bed into the water column.

  − Turbid water leaking from the dredged material storage areas may flow back into the canals.

241. Additionally, other pollutants such as oil and wastewater from dredging equipment may
enter and adversely affect surface water quality. Water quality monitoring data presented in the
baseline chapter and Appendix 4 show that TSS in the project canals and rivers varied between
16 to 89 mg/L, below QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (100mg/L for irrigation purposes). The
assessments presented below will consider the extent to of dredging would affect water quality
in rivers and canals. The dispersion of TSS in the water environment resulting from dredging
is influenced by the dredging method, machinery, the river morphology, and the flow rates in
the canal and river. In theory, to calculate the spread distance of SS from dredging, the formula
from the study “Optimising the Location and Structure of Sediment and Silt Control Works for
Water Intake on Rivers” by Phạm Đức Thắng - Institute of Water Resources Science, Hanoi -
2002, can be used.
                                                    H .K .v
                                                 L = Uo
In which:
  − L: Length of suspended sediment dispersion

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          114
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

 − H: Dredging depth (H = 1 to 2 m, the highest value chosen for calculation is 2 m).
 − K: Empirical coefficient accounting for the effect of flow hindrance on particle settling speed (K = 1.3).
 − v: Flow velocity (m/s), v=0,1 – 0,5 m/s
 − Uo: Hydraulic size of the particle = 0.00512 m/s (corresponding to particle size of clay silt in the project canals,
   d = 0.005 mm).
242. The length of dispersion of suspended solids during dredging is L = 0.00512(2×1.3×0.5)
=254 meters. Thus, the impact distance of suspended solids (SS) to the surrounding areas from
the dredging point is 254m. On the other hand, the experience from other dredging projects in
the Mekong Delta shows that the maximum increase in SS concentration at a distance of 50
meters from the dredging point (ΔCmax) ranges from 100 to 300 mg/L, with an average of 200
mg/L. The flow rate in the project canal system varies between 0.1 and 0.5 m/s. At a distance
of 200m from the dredging location, the dilution rate is 1/5, while at a distance of 500m, the
dilution rate is 1/10. Therefore, an increase in SS concentration at 200 meters from the dredging
point: ΔCmax=(1/5)×200=40 mg/L. This results in a total concentration ranging from 56 to
289mg/L. Therefore, the maximum TSS concentration may exceed the Class B limit by
approximately 2.89 times under worst-case conditions. This exceedance is temporary and
typically persists for 3–5 hours during dredging operations before being reduced through
natural dilution and tidal flushing, returning to baseline conditions within one tidal cycle (6–
12 hours).
243. Based on the calculated dispersion distance (approximately 254m), the canal width of
10m, and the dredging depth of 2-2.5m, together with flow velocities ranging from 0.1 to 0.5
m/s, suspended solids generated during dredging are expected to disperse and dilute rapidly.
Empirical evidence from similar dredging activities in the Mekong Delta shows that, once
dredging stops, TSS concentrations in areas influenced by semi-diurnal tides typically return to
baseline levels within 6-12 hours if the dredging is stopped. This means that the total duration
of impacts on water quality from the canal dredging activities will include the dredging duration
plus an additional 6–12 hours. However, as the canal water is regularly exchanged by tidal
flows, water quality is expected to return to normal within 6–12 hours after dredging activities
cease. Increased water turbidity from dredging may affect aquatic life, especially shrimp ponds,
which are popular in the project area. More details are discussed below.
244. Impacts of Increased Water Turbidity on Shrimp Farming. There are 200 hectares of
shrimp ponds belonging to 170 households in Cai Nuoc, Tan Thuan, and Phu Tan communes.
Water is taken into the ponds through simple culverts, in 2–5 days each month, mainly during
high tides. As presented above, dredging may cause turbidity to increase up to 300 mg/L at 50
m and 40 mg/L at a distance of 200m from the dredging point. According to QCVN
08:2023/BTNMT – National Water Quality Standard for Aquatic lives, the allowable limit for
TSS is 100 mg/L and DO is at least 4 mg/L. Baseline data showed that TSS in the project areas
in the dry season (the time that dreging will be carried out) varies from 16 to 89 mg/L, and DO
ranges from 6 to 7 mg/L. Therefore, dredging would potentially make the TSS in the river/canal
exceed allowable limits 4 times along the section from the dredging location to 254 m
downstream. The impact is not avoidable but manageable, as the farmers only take water
intermittently, 2-5 days each month, as mentioned earlier. Dredging along each section only
takes several days. The potential impacts of dredging on shrimp farming would further be
mitigated through the measures presented in Chapter 5 and the Dredging and Material
Management Plan (Appendix 2).
245. Impacts of Increased Water Turbidity on Aquatic Life: Only a few construction
machines (1-2 items) will be used for dredging. Dredging a 100-meter canal takes a short time
(2-3 days) and can be scheduled during periods of low water demand for shrimp farming. Only
common native species such as carp, snails, and shrimp are present; no known rare/endangered
species in the project area. While the impacts on benthic and aquatic communities are
unavoidable, they can recover within 12 months. Therefore, the potential impacts of dredging
along 6 canal routes and the waterway at Rach Goc port would be temporary.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                              Page            115
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

4.4.1.6. Biological Impacts and Risks

246. Site clearance, transportation of construction and dredged materials, operation of
machinery for dredging, and associated construction activities are expected to have adverse
impacts on both local terrestrial and aquatic biological resources, as detailed below:
247. The project's impact on the terrestrial biological resources is mainly due to the
construction of the embankment. According to the RP, 9,361 trees and 0.876ha of shrubs will
be affected by the project. Consultations with the DAE and local communities have confirmed
the absence of endangered or protected species at project sites. The impact on terrestrial
ecology is deemed low. In addition, the project owner will apply measures in Chapter 5 to
minimize the impact.
248. The main sources of impacts on aquatic ecosystems during the construction phase include
discharge of wastewater and solid waste from various project sites, as presented in Sections
4.4.1.1 to 4.4.1.4 and 4.4.1.12a. These activities pose small to moderate risks to aquatic
ecosystems.

4.4.1.7. Impacts on Local Traffic

a) Impact on Waterway Transportation

249. The sources of impacts on waterway traffic during the construction phase of the project
include: (i) the increased waterway traffic density with additional barges transporting
construction materials for the project sites, and (ii) the occupation of water surfaces for the
construction (especially at the port), the deployment of construction equipment and machinery.
These activities may lead to (i) disturbance to water way traffic, longer travel times for residents
compared to normal conditions; (ii) disruptions to port access, maritine procedures, and fish
trading activities of boat owners at the port; and (iii) an increased risk of waterway accidents
due to collisions between local vessels, or between local vessels and project vessels, equipment,
or completed structures—especially at night or during extreme weather events such as tropical
depressions and storms. In the event of a collision, impacts may include damage to local
property, threats to the safety and lives of local people and project workers, damage to project
assets, and delays in construction progress. These impacts are assessed in detail below.
250. Construction material delivery: On average, approximately 4–5 trips per day (4.28
trips/day) will be conducted to deliver materials to 7 construction sites spread across 6
communes, meaning that each transportation route accommodates only about one trip per day.
The waterways utilized—such as Rach Goc River, Moi–Dong Tren Canal, Ba Lai Canal, Nam
Long Canal, and Xang–Cau Sat Canal—are wide and navigable, with no recorded incidents of
congestion or significant waterway traffic accidents. Material transport will occur during
daytime hours only, with each trip taking approximately 3–4 hours. Additionally, the
construction sites are geographically dispersed, further reducing the risk of localized traffic
concentration. Consequently, the impact on waterway traffic is considered small.
251. In the construction area of the dredged canals: The dredging activities on the six canals
may affect waterway traffic primarily due to the presence of dredging vessels and barges
stationed while waiting to transport dredged materials to the sites used for embankment
upgrading. This could make navigation more difficult for local boats passing through these
sections, and may potentially lead to collisions between local vessels and project equipment
(e.g., dredgers or barges), or between local boats themselves—especially during peak hours, at
night, or in extreme weather conditions such as tropical depressions, strong winds, or storms.
If such incidents occur, they could result in damage to property, pose safety risks to residents,
and endanger the lives of construction workers operating in the affected waterway sections.
However, these impacts are assessed to be small due to the following factors: (i) dredging is
carried out sequentially (rolling manner), and the dredging duration for each canal is relatively
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         116
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

short (approximately 7–20 days); (ii) the occupied area for dredging equipment at each site is
small (approximately 5 x 20 meters); and (iii) the traffic density on these canals is low,
consisting mostly of small boats, and the remaining width of the canals after equipment
placement is still sufficient to allow local vessels to pass easily.
252. At construction area of the port: Materials will be transported along the Moi-Dong Tren
Canal, Ba Lai Canal, Nam Long Canal, and Xang-Cau Sat Canal, etc. Waterway traffic at these
waterways and the Rach Goc river section passing the fishing port may be affected by the
presence of dredging vessels and construction equipment used for building the wharf. However,
the Rach Goc River at the construction site is approximately 150 meters wide, while the area
occupied by project equipment is only about 27 meters—roughly 20% of the river’s width.
Most vessels operating on the Rach Goc River are relatively narrow, with widths of 4–5 meters,
and typically travel one at a time rather than side by side in groups of four or five. As such, the
remaining river width is sufficient to allow vessels to navigate safely and smoothly. However,
there remains a potential risk of collisions between local boats and project equipment,
particularly at night or during extreme weather conditions when visibility is reduced, and at the
intersections between the Rach Goc river and the two canals located at the sides of the Port.
253. This impact is assessed as moderate, moderate-term, and can be effectively mitigated
through the implementation of the measures proposed in Chapter 5. In addition, national laws
also require the contractor to prepare and Waterway Traffic Management Plan and submit it
for review and approval by the local authority before construction commencement.

b) Impacts on Local Road Traffic

254. Port upgradation: Traffic density along the narrow access roads and two bridges (8-ton
load limit) is very low, consisting mainly of small trucks under 8 tons and vehicles used by
residents. Meanwhile, the transportation of construction materials for the port upgrade is
primarily carried out by boats. Furthermore, the dredged material is pumped from the barge to
the designated storage area. Therefore, the impact of the port upgrading activities on roadway
traffic during the construction phase is low. Some rules would be set for the drivers to follow
in case the roadway is used for the transportation of a small amount of materials during
construction phase.
255. Embankment upgrading: Embankment routes will be upgraded along existing narrow,
degraded paths (which may be dirt roads, concrete roads, etc.) (from 1 to 3 m wide). Currently,
these are the main access routes for residents, but traffic density is low as the houses are
distributed sparsely. The embankment upgrading will be carried out in a rolling manner, with
each 100-meter section typically taking about 25-30 days to complete. Road works will make
travel more difficult for residents, especially school students, whose mobility is already limited.
It may even increase the risk of traffic accidents, leading to injury, fatalities, or property
damage, especially in sections with high traffic density—such as areas near schools during
student dismissal times—or during heavy or prolonged rains that cause the roads to become
muddy, as well as in low-visibility conditions like fog or nighttime. In addition, dredged
material taking activities near 3 low-quality bridges may cause damage to these structures. If
such incidents occur, there is a risk that people or vehicles could fall into the canals while
passing by, resulting in property loss, injury, or even drowning. The impact is assessed as
moderate due to: (i) generally low traffic density along the embankment routes; (ii) students are
traveling to and from schools along the routes.
256. Canal dredging: Dredging activities will primarily be conducted on the water surface,
and the dredged sediment will be transported for use in reinforcing embankments. However, as
mentioned above, there are three low-quality canal crossing bridges. Dredging, if implemented
improperly near these structures, may cause damage or even bridge collapses and lead to traffic
interruptions and/or accidents with property damage, injury, or even drowning. It could also
cause travel disruptions. This impact is at a moderate level and manageable.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         117
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

257. Comments: The impact on local mobility is assessed as low to moderate, localized, and
happens in a short time. The project owner will implement the necessary management and
mitigation measures presented in Chapter 5 and the Dredging and Material Management Plan.

4.4.1.8. Risks of Localised Flooding

258. The excavation, earthwork, and leveling activities have the potential to impede drainage,
causing localised flooding, especially in rainy weather.
259. Port upgradation: Port upgrading activities will mainly occur on the water surface;
construction of the sorting facility is not likely to cause localised flooding. However, if wet
dredging materials are pumped to the 8,763 m² of the storage site, localised flooding may
happen if leakage is not managed properly. This is controllable by engineering design of the
disposal site and the existing stormwater drainage system at the port. As previously noted, the
disposal site is designed with inward-sloping embankments, enabling effective containment and
management of nearly all rainwater runoff and leakage water. Therefore, overall localised
flooding risks are considered low at the Port upgrading site.
260. Embankment Upgrading: Localised flooding commonly caused by rainwater in the
project area. It could be worse during embankment upgradation when the ground is disturbed,
existing drainage path is altered. Inundation can negatively impact the health and productivity
of workers, as well as affect nearby residents. It may also cause damage to construction works.
Deep, prolonged local flooding could (i) directly and indirectly affect the living conditions,
production activities, and mobility of both workers and local communities; (ii) degrade the
landscape, reduce environmental sanitation, and increase the risk of communicable diseases;
(iii) damage ongoing construction works; (iv) reduced productivity of aquaculture farms if
shirmps escape from the ponds during high water level.
261. Canal Dredging and Dredged Materials: The dredging materials are either loaded on
the barges or stored temporarily along the canals, where embankments will be upgraded. This
area spans approximately 20.8 ha. According to the FS report, each storage site is divided into
compartments, each no more than 5 m long, approximately 6 m wide, and up to 1.2 m high (see
Figure 4). The ground elevation at the top of embankments (after upgrading) will be from 1.8
to 2.5m, higher than the top of dredging containments. Effectively preventing dredged material
from spilling into local gardens, houses, and shrimp ponds (see Figure 3). Additionally, every
200m, a drainage ditch 0.5m wide will be constructed to discharge water back into the canal,
ensuring timely drainage during rainfall and preventing overflow into gardens or shrimp ponds
located in front of the dredged material compartments. Consequently, the maximum rainwater
runoff from each compartment during heavy rainfall is estimated at 1.5m³/day (calculated for a
rain of 50mm/day).
262. In the event of an embankment breach, a mixture of water, dredged materials, and other
existing substances on the ground surface may overflow into the household land located along
the affected section. Many objects, such as 940 water intake culverts, 1,070 gardens/yards and
houses, and 47 business establishments such as cafes, grocery stores, and shrimp collection
points, etc. (Table 34), followed by shrimp ponds, may be affected by flooding. However, the
likelihood of all compartments failing simultaneously is very low. If one compartment
breaches, it would affect only about 2–3 households. Therefore, the impact is assessed as small
due to: (i) the limited number of affected households per breach (around 2–3); and (ii) the small
size of the compartments, allowing for rapid repairs—typically within a few hours.
263. However, the impact mainly occurs during the rainy season (May to November). The
impact is assessed as low because (i) the majority of the road construction will take place during
the dry season, and (ii) rainwater can drain quickly due to the embankments being located near
canals. Additionally, the impact can be mitigated by measures, as proposed in Chapter 5.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          118
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

4.4.1.9. Risks of Soil Subsidence and Embankment Erosion During Heavy Rains

264. Port upgradation: There are soil subsidence and erosion risks at the 10,446 m² of dredge
material storage area.
265. Canal dredging: When dredging canals to a depth of 2.0–2.5m and a width of 10m, there
is a risk of canal bank erosion at the dredging sites, especially under the impacts of tides with
waves, or waves due to the movement of the project’s waterway transportation means. The area
along the dredging path primarily includes yards, some fruit gardens, approximately 100
houses, and several aquaculture ponds. These may be vulnerable to erosion/soil subsidence
during canal dredging construction. To minimise canal erosion risks, the canal cross-sections
are designed with a low slope ratio of 1.5 to 2.0. Therefore, erosion and soil subsidence risk is
considered low, and manageable due to the following reasons: (i) the dredging will be carried
out using a rolling method; (ii) each canal section is expected to be completed within 5–20
days; and (iii) no households are located within 10 meters of the canal banks.
266. Embankment upgradation: Erosions may happen at granular material loads (soil,
sand), while soil subsidence may happen at the slopes created during construction. The nearby
households may be affected by dust, soil, and water encroachment. The risk is low as
construction will be carried out in sequence, each 100-meter segment. Only 5–10 houses lie
within 100 meters of the work zone
267. Comments: The risk for soil subsidence and embankment erosion is low and temporary.
It can be effectively minimized through the application of consequential construction methods
and other measures detailed in Chapter 5. Especially, during the detailed design, further
surveys would be necessary to determine if there are any structures at subsidence/safety risks
from canal dredging. Mitigation measures will be proposed accordingly if relocation is not
required.

4.4.1.10. Social Impacts and Risks

a) Impacts and Risks Related to Labor Influx

268. Risks of conflicts with the local community. During the peak construction period,
approximately 10 teams are expected to be mobilized, each consisting of 6 to 20 workers.
Workers involved in embankment upgrading and canal dredging will rent accommodations
from residents, which are equipped with septic tanks. Meanwhile, workers assigned to port
improvement activities will stay in vacant rooms available at the port during the construction
phase. Given the small and dispersed workforce, most potential pressures, such as local
inflation, increased rents, or overloading of public services (water, electricity, waste collection,
health care) are expected to be negligible and can be readily managed with existing
infrastructure. The main potential social risk relates to interactions between workers and the
local community, particularly ethnic minorities and children. In some communes (Cai Nuoc
and Tan Thuan), ethnic minority households live close to construction areas and may have
limited experience in interacting with outside workers. There, the presence of labor influx at
project sites, especially where ethnic minority households are present, could lead to the risk
of misunderstandings, discrimination, and social tension. Children and women may also be
more vulnerable to sexual exploitation, harassment, or abuse (SEA/SH), or violence (physical
or by words).
269. Risks of social conflicts, GBV/SEA/SH, HIV/AIDS, and communicable diseases. With 6-
20 workers (approximately 75% men and 25% women), social conflicts may arise within the
labour force due to the differences in customs, lifestyles, and income levels. In addition, there
are also risks of Gender-based violence (GBV), Sexual Abuse (SEA), or Sexual Harassment
(SH). This risk is considered to be low in the ongoing projects in Viet Nam. On the other hand,
if the workers are engaged in “social evils” such as gambling, drug use, or prostitution, they
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         119
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

may be infected with communicable diseases such as HIV/AIDS and syphilis, which can have
an impact on the next generation. These risks are at a low level and are manageable.
270. Risk of Child Labor, Forced Labor usage. Some unskilled labor will be hired. The
contractors are required to comply with Viet Nam’s regulations, under which the working age
is from 15 years old. There were no issues regarding the use of child labour or forced labour in
the past WB-financed or government-funded projects in Ca Mau province. Therefore, the risk
of child labour, forced labour is low.
271. In summary, the potential impacts and risks related to Labour Influx are anticipated to
be low and localized because (i) workforce numbers are small, (ii) Ca Mau Province has
experience managing migrant labor, and (iii) local authorities are accustomed to and capable of
supervising temporary workers. The risks can be mitigated by the measures introduced in the
Labour Management Procedures (LMP), the Workers’ Codes of Conduct (CoC), the Grievance
Redress Mechanism (GRM), and the mitigation measures presented in Chapter 5.

b) Access disturbance or disruptions

272. The embankment upgrading will use sections of existing narrow and degraded roads as
work areas, which may limit access to approximately 1,070 households, 47 household
businesses, and their clients (3-20 people per facility), and pose safety risks to them. However,
the impacts are considered moderate due to: (i) the rolling construction method being applied;
and (ii) the short duration of disturbance (about 25–30 days per household). The construction
is also widely supported by residents, as it will enhance mobility after completion. Mitigation
measures outlined in Chapter 5 will be implemented to reduce impacts.

c) Impacts on Fishing Activities

273. Surveys and community consultations indicate a small number or even no aquaculture or
fishing activity along the Rach Goc River section passing the Port. Fishing activity is very
limited along the canal to be dredged, as the farmers are doing aquaculture largely. Thus, the
project would not affect local fishing activities.

4.4.1.11. Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) Risks

a) General risks

274. Construction activities present general health and safety risks to workers, including:
(i). Health risks due to exposure to dust, noise, vibration, and chemical (oil, hazardous
     materials)/physical (dusts) /biological (insects) agents during site clearance, demolition,
     excavation, leveling, transportation, and construction; Workers may also experience
     musculoskeletal injuries from handling heavy materials, repetitive movements, or
     overexertion. Physical and mental sickness may come from excessive workloads, tensions
     between the workers. Inadequate sanitation facilities may also lead to water-borne diseases,
     lack of access to adequate health care services may lead to epidemic outbreaks, etc.
            ▪ Dust and airborne particles are a major health hazard, particularly from materials such
              as concrete, brick, and wood. Inhaling fine dust can lead to respiratory issues or lung
              diseases.
            ▪ Exposure to high noise levels (from heavy construction plants, power generators, pile
              drivers, etc.) may lead to hearing loss and deaf. Loud, repetitive, and excessive noise
              causes long-term hearing problems. Noise can also be a dangerous distraction and may
              distract the worker from the task at hand, which can cause accidents.
(ii). Safety risks due to exposure to unsafe working conditions, such as structural collapse,
      working above water surface, working from height (especially from scaffold, ladders, or
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  Page          120
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

      roofs), working in confined spaces, extreme weather conditions while working (storm,
      waves and tides, exceeding sunlight, etc.). Operations of equipment and heavy construction
      plants (especially trucks, cranes, hammers, etc.), driving or moving within construction
      sites and along the transportation routes, etc. Slips, trips, and falls risks may also lead to
      injuries, even fatalities.
(iii).Accident risks related to electrical usage while welding, cutting, and soldering, including
      shocks, burns, injuries, or even fatalities
(iv). Risks of exposure to fire and explosion hazards sourced from flammable materials.

b) Specific risks

b.1). Specific OHS risks from the demolition during site clearance
275. The most significant hazards include structural instability leading to uncontrolled
collapse of walls, roofs, or entire buildings, and falling debris such as bricks, concrete, or steel,
which can cause severe injuries or fatalities to workers and bystanders. Dust and airborne
particles from construction materials pose respiratory issues, especially in cases where asbestos
(suspected to be a cancer-causing agent) is present in the old structures before the use of them
was abandoned. Noise from machinery and falling debris can impair hearing, which may
contribute to an accident happening. Incidents involving heavy machinery (excavators,
bulldozers, and cranes, either due to equipment failure, operator error, or poor visibility) and
hand tools (sledge hammers and drillers) increase the likelihood of lacerations, blunt force
injuries, slips, trips, and falls on uneven or debris-laden terrain. Utility-related risks, such as
damage to underground water lines or live electrical cables, heighten the danger of
electrocution. Working at height amplifies the risk of falls and musculoskeletal injuries,
especially when scaffolding and ladders lack adequate safeguards. Demolition near public roads
or populated areas also raises concerns regarding traffic safety and exposure of bystanders to
debris or machinery, with adverse weather conditions further exacerbating these risks.
b.2). Specific OHS during the Rach Goc Fishing Port Upgrading
276. Upgradation of the Rach Goc fishing port presents a range of specific OHS risks across
different work listed below:
 − Roof construction (onshore): The workers and port visitors are exposed to these risks

     ▪ Injuries or even fatality from falling from heights when working on rooftops or scaffolds.
     ▪ Heatstroke, exhaustion, or being sick from exposure to high temperatures.
     ▪ Injuries from lifting or handling heavy construction materials, from moving objects from
       the ground to the rooftop and vice versa, or while moving between the loads of
       construction materials.
     ▪ Electrical hazards from equipment or temporary wiring systems can cause injuries or even
       fatality.
     ▪ Risk of slips, trips, or scaffold collapse, especially during adverse weather (heavy rain,
       strong winds).
 − Dredging (on water and onshore). The workers and drivers of other transportation means
   passing the construction areas, port officers, fishermen from fishing boats are exposed to
   risks from dredging activities, and the community passing by the disposal area behind the
   backwall of the port, if that site is used by the project.
     ▪ Drowning when working near or over water, especially during strong waves, high tides,
       excessive sunlight, or other extreme weather conditions such as heavy rains, strong winds
     ▪ Injuries or even fatality from collision hazards between the operating machinery, barges,
       or trucks, or between project construction plants with other transportation means passing

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page          121
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

      or parking nearby. The hazard may lead to secondary incidents such as boat fires,
      explosions, or ship shrinks.
    ▪ Health impacts, fatigue, or reduced alertness from prolonged exposure to vibration and
      high noise levels, especially during pile driving or from exposure to hazardous materials
      such as oil and gas used for dredging equipment.
    ▪ Injuries from falling into and getting trapped in the wet sediments stored at the dredged
      material storage area.
    ▪ Fire and explosion risks may cause injuries or fatalities.
 − Wharf expansion (mostly over water): Wharf expansion, mostly over water, involves fall
   hazards at edges or over open water, instability or collapse of equipment and working
   platforms due to wave impact or overloading, heightened electric shock risks in wet
   environments, limited evacuation routes in the event of fire, explosion, or other emergencies,
   and cumulative health effects from continuous exposure to intense sunlight, wind, and tidal
   variations.
    ▪ Hearing impairments caused by loud noise during pile driving.
    ▪ Falling into water hazards, being injured, or drowning from working at edges or over
      open water.
    ▪ Injuries from equipment, scaffolding, working platforms, instability, or collapse,
      especially under the impacts of strong water currents or waves.
    ▪ Wave impact may destabilize.
    ▪ High risk of electric shock due to wet or humid environments.
    ▪ Limited evacuation routes in case of fire, explosion, or other emergencies.
    ▪ Musculoskeletal pains from using hand tools, especially concrete bibrators
    ▪ Health risks from exposure to hot works (welding), dusts (especially from metal cutting
      and cement dusts)
b.3). Specific OHS Risks from Embankment Upgrading
 − Exposure to heavy machinery overturning incidents, especially when operating on soft,
   unstable soils in Ca Mau, can result in injuries or fatalities.
 − Slips, trips, falls, and drowning.

 − Health issues (pains, irritations, hearing impairments, etc.) from exposure to harmful agents
   (such as bees, snakes, fire ants, etc.) during site clearance, or exposure to loud noise or
   pollutants such as cement, dust, fuel, and lubricants. Poor sanitation and waste management
   can further increase the spread of infectious diseases.
 − Safety risks while working in confined spaces, especially when working in culverts or
   drainage systems.
 − Musculoskeletal strain and fatigue from lifting or moving heavy loads.

 − Exposure to vector-borne diseases such as dengue fever. Electrical, fire, and explosion
   hazards are also significant due to wet working conditions, exposed wiring, and the use of
   flammable materials. Combined with hot and humid weather, long hours, and demanding
   labor conditions, these risks can lead to heat stress, dehydration, and mental fatigue.
 − Electrical hazards, especially prevalent in the rainy season. Exposed wiring, improper
   grounding of equipment, and the presence of overhead power lines can lead to electric
   shocks, electrocution, or fires. The use of welding and cutting tools in wet conditions further
   increases these risks.
 − The risk of fire and explosions arises from the storage and handling of flammable materials,
   such as fuel and welding gases. Sparks from welding or cutting metal components can
   ignite dry vegetation or stored construction materials, leading to serious fire hazards.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          122
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

 − Heat stress is a major concern due to the province’s hot and humid climate, increasing the
   risk of dehydration, heatstroke, and fatigue. Long working hours and demanding labor
   conditions can contribute to mental stress, exhaustion, and reduced productivity.
b.4). Specific OHS Risks from Canal Dredging
277. Workers face a high risk of drowning when operating on barges or near water with strong
currents and fluctuating levels. Slippery, muddy surfaces increase slips and falls, while heavy
dredging machinery can cause mechanical failures, rollovers, or collisions. Prolonged exposure
to loud noise, vibration, and contaminated sediments containing hazardous substances may
affect workers’ health, as do extreme weather conditions such as heat, humidity, sudden rainfall,
or flooding that destabilize work areas. Canal bank excavation also risks collapses and striking
submerged debris or utilities, causing injuries or fatalities to the workers. In addition, the project
area may host snakes, mosquitoes, and other insects, increasing the chance of bites or stings
during dredging and vegetation clearance.
278. The OHS risks are substantial. Contractors will be required to implement mitigation
measures presented in Chapter 5, which should take into account the comprehensive technical
regulations on Safety in Construction described in national standards QCVN 18:2021/BXD.
Risk assessment should be done during the construction phase so that the OHS plans can be
further detailed for implementation.

4.4.1.12. Community Health and Safety Risks

279. Construction activities may cause risks to the health and safety of communities in the
project area, including:
 − At the Port upgrading site:

    ▪ Waterway navigation hazards to boats passing by, berthing, or moving within the
      construction area due to the presence of barges, cranes, and pile-driving equipment.
    ▪ Traffic safety risks along the port access road, particularly at intersections and residential
      areas, despite the majority of construction materials being transported via waterways.
    ▪ Safety concerns at the temporary disposal area located behind the port wall (if activated),
      including risks of slope instability, accidental entry, or improper containment of dredged
      materials.
    ▪ Structural safety risks to buildings and infrastructure located within a 50-meter radius of
      the port due to vibration and ground movement from pile driving.
 − At the embankment upgrading site:

    ▪ Open excavations and trenches at construction sites: These can pose fall hazards to local
      residents, especially children, if left unfenced or improperly marked.
    ▪ Stockpiles of construction materials: Sand, gravel, cement, and other materials stored near
      roadways or public areas can obstruct visibility and movement, creating tripping or
      collision risks.
    ▪ Heavy machinery movement: The presence and movement of trucks, excavators, and
      other construction equipment in or near populated areas increases the likelihood of traffic-
      related incidents.
    ▪ Waste and debris accumulation: Construction waste temporarily dumped or stored near
      communities may contain sharp objects or hazardous substances, posing injury or
      contamination risks.
    ▪ Dust and noise pollution: High levels of dust and continuous noise can affect the
      respiratory health and mental well-being of residents, particularly vulnerable groups like
      the elderly or children.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                Page          123
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

     ▪ Blocked access: Construction zones that encroach on sidewalks, driveways, or alleys may
       restrict access to homes, schools, shops, and emergency services, leading to community
       disruption.
     ▪ Poor lighting at construction sites: Sites without proper lighting during early mornings or
       evenings increase the chance of accidents for both workers and passersby.
     ▪ Standing water: Areas of stagnant water left after excavation or rain can become breeding
       grounds for mosquitoes, increasing the risk of disease transmission in surrounding
       communities.
     ▪ There is a risk of unauthorized individuals entering construction sites: residents may
       illegally enter to play or collect materials, creating safety hazards and potentially
       endangering lives (especially those of children) and causing the loss of project materials.
       To mitigate this, construction sites will be enclosed, and warning signs will be posted to
       prevent unauthorized access.
  − At the canal dredging site:

     ▪ The failure of embankments at the dredging canal section or the material storage area
       could impact nearby houses and pose safety risks to surrounding residents.
     ▪ Waterway traffic accidents resulted from the parking or operations of the dredging
       machinery on the waterway.
     ▪ Accidents if existing infrastructures are damaged by dredging (canal-crossing electrical
       wires and bridges, if any)
     ▪ There is a risk of unauthorized individuals entering construction sites: residents may
       illegally enter to play or collect materials, creating safety hazards and potentially
       endangering lives (especially those of children) and causing the loss of project materials.
       To mitigate this, construction sites will be enclosed, and warning signs will be posted to
       prevent unauthorized access.
280. The impact level is evaluated as small to moderate based on: (i) impacts and risks
localised; (ii) a moderate construction duration of 3 to 24 months; (iii) a small workforce (6–
20 workers per site), prioritizing local hires; and can be mitigated by measures in Chapter 5,
such as provision of training for workers on occupational safety, traffic safety, and sanitation;
installation of warning signs and fencing at construction sites to protect public safety;
Consultation and advance notification of the construction plan should be provided at least 15
days prior to commencement, allowing residents to proactively adjust their travel, daily
activities, and production plans, and (vii) effective mitigation through the application of
Environmental and Social Codes of Practice (ESCOPs).

a) Risk of incidents, fire, explosion, and pollution due to Leakage or spill

281. Waterway Incident Risks. As most construction materials will be transported by water,
there is a risk of boat collisions during transit to the construction sites. Such accidents could
significantly impact the environment, particularly water quality, by increasing turbidity from
bed disturbance or causing oil spills from engine damage, and may result in injuries or fatalities
to boat operators and locals. These incidents are expected to have a moderate negative impact
due to their low probability. Should an accident occur, it could have a substantial, long-term
impact on habitats, people, and property. Thus, a robust rapid-response plan is essential to
effectively manage such emergencies in Chapter 5.
282. Fire and explosion risks. Fire and explosion emergencies can occur due to fuel storage
and the improper use of electrical equipment, potentially leading to severe outcomes, such as
injury, disability, or death. Major risk factors include: (i) insufficient or poorly maintained
firefighting systems at fuel storage locations on construction sites; (ii) electrical hazards caused
by generators powering machinery and equipment; and (iii) the use of heating equipment, which

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         124
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

may cause fires or occupational accidents like burns. The risk of fire and explosion poses a
moderate to significant negative impact, with potential long-term consequences if incidents
occur. Given their unpredictable nature, each construction site must have a dedicated
Emergency Preparedness and Response Plan. Additionally, adequate fire prevention and
response equipment, as detailed in Chapter 5, shall be readily available to reduce the likelihood
and severity of such events.

b) Risk of Security Forces

283. Project contractors may assign security personnel to protect staff and assets during
construction. Experience has shown that security-related risks to on-site workers and the nearby
community are deemed low and confined to the construction phase. To address these risks,
contractors must adhere to proper procedures for recruiting, training, and managing security
personnel at the site.

4.4.1.13. Impacts on Physical Cultural Resources (PCR)s

284. There are 8 shrines of the goddess located within 10-30m of the project’s construction
areas (Table 35). Although no land acquisition at these sites is required, construction activities
may impact these shrines through dust, noise, and vibration, as well as by disrupting access,
creating visual disturbances, or causing nuisance if construction materials and waste are not
properly managed. A loud noise could also interfere with religious ceremonies. Furthermore,
as the shrines are quite old and have simple structures, construction activities could potentially
compromise their structural integrity. Detailed impact assessment on the shrines are showed in
Section 4.4.2.2.d.
285. Apart from the shrines, no significant historical or cultural sites were identified at the
project construction sites during surveys and consultations with local authorities and residents.
However, due to the extensive excavation and dredging involved, there is a possibility that
artifacts may be exposed during construction.
286. Comments: The impact on physical cultural resources is expected to be low and localized.
In addition, mitigation measures to address these impacts are presented in the Appendix.
Furthermore, a chance-find procedure, as outlined in Chapter 5, will be established to guide
the management of any unexpected discovery of historical or cultural artifacts during
construction.

4.4.1.14. Impacts and Risks of the Installation of Water Monitoring Stations

287. At the FS stage, the exact scope and locations of the proposed monitoring stations have
not yet been determined. This impact assessment is therefore based on comparable facilities
constructed under the previous MD-ICRSL project, which typically consisted of small brick or
reinforced concrete houses to host monitoring equipment, combined with short access
walkways built on pile foundations along riverbanks (Figure 35).
288. The main potential impacts relate to construction activities on and over water. Pile-
driving, installation of concrete structures, and building access paths may temporarily disturb
riverbanks and aquatic habitats. There is also a risk of accidental spills of construction materials
(e.g., cement, lubricants, fuel) into the river, potentially affecting water quality and nearby
aquatic organisms. Noise and vibration from pile-driving could disturb local fish and other
aquatic life during construction. Workers will also face occupational health and safety risks
associated with working over water, including risks of falling, drowning, and exposure to
adverse weather conditions. Because the stations will be located in brackish or saline waters,
there is a potential risk of corrosion of reinforced concrete structures and monitoring equipment
due to saline intrusion, which may shorten the lifespan of construction materials and devices if

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         125
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

appropriate anti-corrosion design and maintenance measures are not applied. In addition,
locating stations in areas with dense vessel traffic could interfere with waterway transportation
and increase the risk of accidents. Stations placed near discharge points, oil leaks, or other
pollutants from vessels may also face safety risks and reduced accuracy or reliability of
monitoring data. The impacts and risks are assessed as low, short-term, and can be mitigated
by measures in Chapter 5.




             Figure 35: A water monitoring station built in the MD-ICRSL project

4.4.2. Type and Site-Specific Impacts

4.4.2.1. Site-specific impacts during the upgrade of the Rach Goc Fishing Port

289. The upgrading of the Rach Goc Fishing Port will include (i) expanding the existing wharf
by 150 meters through two sections of 75 meters each, 14 meters in width, with a top elevation
of +2.50 meters and a bottom elevation of –5.00 meters; (ii) dredging a 10,446 m² water area
in front of the port to a width of 25 meters, length of 270 meters, and depth of 5 meters;
(iii) installing two new roofing structures at the fish sorting areas of each new wharf section (29
meters long, 9 meters wide, 7.2 meters high, with a 4.5-meter clearance); and (iv) constructing
a 150-meter drainage ditch (40 cm x 40 cm) to improve stormwater management.
290. Based on the location, investment scale, and socio-environmental characteristics of the
project area described in Chapter 2, the specific impacts and risks associated with the port
upgrading are identified as follows: (i) Impacts on existing operations of the port; (ii) impacts
on waterway traffic along the Rach Goc River within the project area; (iii) deterioration of water
quality and effects on aquatic life due to dredging activities; (iv) environmental pollution risks
at temporary storage areas of the dredged materials; (v) potential structural damage to nearby
houses from pile-driving activities; and (vi) impacts on local businesses.
291. The details of these impacts and associated risks are presented in the following sections.

a) Disturbance to the operation of the existing Rach Goc fishing port

292. The upgrading of the fishing port will be carried out on the existing port facilities, which
will affect ongoing activities at the site, including: (i) boat arrivals for declaration procedures;
(ii) trading of fish products; and (iii) other businesses.
293. Impacts on declaration procedures and cargo unloading activities: As mentioned
earlier, fishing vessels returning from offshore operations via the Rach Goc estuary are required
to dock at the port to complete declaration procedures.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         126
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

294. Therefore, the port upgrading activities—particularly the dredging of the water area in
front of the port and the expansion of anchorage wharves—may partially reduce the available
space or temporarily restrict access to certain docking areas. This could lead to delays in
completing procedures due to slower vessel movement, the need to dock elsewhere before
returning for declaration, or increased risk of collisions between local vessels and construction
vessels or equipment, especially during extreme weather events. Such impacts could pose risks
to the safety of project workers, boat crew, the properties of boat owners, and project assets,
and potentially delay construction progress.
295. Based on field surveys, the number of boats reaching at the port ranges from 20 to 25,
including 15-20 fishing boats, 3-5 boats to supply ices for the fishing boats. The Rach Goc
River section in front of the port has a natural width of about 120–150 meters and an average
navigable depth of 4.5–5.0 meters during the dry season. This cross-section provides sufficient
maneuvering space for two-way movement of medium-sized fishing vessels (20–25 m long,
draft <2.5 m). Boat arrivals at the port are concentrated during two main time windows each
day—between 7:00–8:00 a.m. and 2:00–3:00 p.m.—when about 3–4 boats typically arrive
simultaneously.
296. However, these impacts are assessed to be small and mitigable due to the following
reasons:
  − Construction will be implemented by segments to ensure that at least one operational berth
    area—75 to 150 meters wide—remains open at all times. This width allows simultaneous
    docking for 3–4 vessels of 20–25m length, adequately accommodating average daily traffic
    even during peak hours.
  − Construction will occur short term (about 12 months). Dredging and wharf extension will be
    conducted only during the dry season. Despite seasonal low water levels, the remaining
    navigable channel (120–150 m wide and 4.5–5.0 m deep) will be maintained for safe vessel
    passage throughout the construction activities.
  − The duration of a boat’s stay at the port is relatively short—approximately 15–20 minutes
    for boats that only complete declaration procedures, and about 40–45 minutes for those
    conducting both declaration procedures and fish trading.
  − The contractor and port authority will coordinate to avoid construction during peak vessel
    traffic hours (7:00–8:00 AM and 2:00–3:00 PM), maintain temporary navigation buoys, and
    issue regular public notices. These measures will minimize collision risk and ensure safe and
    timely completion of declaration procedures.
297. Impacts on fish trading activities: After completing the declaration procedures, some
vessels may conduct fish trading directly at the port, including unloading fish from boats to the
shore, preliminary sorting, icing, weighing, and loading the fish onto trucks (Figure 36).
Onshore construction activities may temporarily reduce the available trading space and pose
safety risks, such as falling heavy objects or collapsing scaffolding. However, these impacts are
assessed as low due to: (i) the trading area being very limited, approximately 4–5 m²; (ii) the
small number of participants, typically around 10 per transaction; and (iii) the short duration of
each transaction, generally 45–60 minutes.




                                 Figure 36: Fish trading at the port
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          127
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

b) Impacts on the waterway along Rach Goc River

298. Waterway traffic at the fishing port may be affected by the presence of dredging vessels
and construction plants used for building the anchorage facility. However, the Rach Goc River
at the construction site is approximately 150 meters wide, while the area occupied by project
construction plants is only about 27 meters, roughly 20% of the river’s width. Most vessels
operating on the Rach Goc River are relatively narrow, with widths of 4–5 meters, and typically
travel one at a time rather than side by side in groups of four or five. As such, the remaining
river width is sufficient to allow vessels to navigate safely and smoothly. However, there
remains a potential risk of collisions between local boats and project construction plants,
particularly at night or during extreme weather conditions when visibility is reduced. This
impact is assessed as moderate, short-term, and can be effectively mitigated through the
implementation of the measures proposed in Chapter 5, in which the Port contractor would be
required to prepare and submit a Waterway Traffic Management Plan (see Appendix 6) for
approval by the local authority before construction commencement.

c) Deterioration of water quality and effects on aquatic life due to dredging activities

299. Dredging activities will disturb sediments in the river and canal bed, leading to increased
turbidity. The causes include:
  − Some dredged material may fall from the bucket and disperse in the water as it moves from
    the canal bed to the surface.
  − Bottom mud is stirred, spread, and suspended from the canal bed into the water column.

  − The turbid water leaking from the dredged material storage areas may flow back into the
    canals.
300. Additionally, other pollutants such as oil and wastewater from dredging equipment may
enter and adversely affect surface water quality. The dispersion of TSS in the water
environment resulting from dredging is influenced by the dredging method, machinery, the
river morphology, and the flow rates in the river. In theory, to calculate the spread distance of
SS from dredging, the formula from the study “Optimising the Location and Structure of
Sediment and Silt Control Works for Water Intake on Rivers” by Phạm Đức Thắng - Institute
of Water Resources Science, Hanoi - 2002, can be used.
                                                       H .K .v
                                                    L = Uo
In which:
  − L: Length of suspended sediment dispersion
  − H: Dredging depth (H = 1 to 2 m, the highest value chosen for calculation is 2 m).
  − K: Empirical coefficient accounting for the effect of flow hindrance on particle settling speed (K = 1.3).
  − v: Flow velocity (m/s), v=0,1 – 0,5 m/s
  − Uo: Hydraulic size of the particle = 0.00512 m/s (corresponding to particle size of clay silt in the project canals,
    d = 0.005 mm).
301. The length of dispersion of suspended solids during dredging is L = 0.00512(2×1.3×0.5)
=254 meters. Thus, the impact distance of suspended solids (SS) to the surrounding areas from
the dredging point is 254m. On the other hand, the experience from other dredging projects in
the Mekong Delta shows that the maximum increase in SS concentration at a distance of 50
meters from the dredging point (ΔCmax) ranges from 100 to 300 mg/L, with an average of 200
mg/L. The flow rate in the Rach Goc river varies between 0.1 and 0.5 m/s. At a distance of
200m from the dredging location, the dilution rate is 1/5, while at a distance of 500m, the
dilution rate is 1/10. Therefore, an increase in SS concentration at 200 meters from the dredging
point: ΔCmax=(1/5)×200=40 mg/L. Based on QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (Class B – allowable
TSS ≤100 mg/L), the background TSS in Rach Goc river is 89 mg/L, which is within the
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                              Page             128
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

permissible limit. During dredging, the maximum increase of suspended solids at 200m from
the dredging point is estimated at 40 mg/L, resulting in a total concentration of TSS in the river,
ranging from 129-289mg/L. Therefore, the maximum TSS concentration may exceed the Class
B limit by approximately 2.89 times in the worst case. This exceedance is temporary and
typically persists for 3–5 hours during dredging operations before being reduced through
natural dilution and tidal flushing, returning to baseline conditions within one tidal cycle (6–
12 hours).
302. Empirical evidence from similar dredging activities in the Mekong Delta shows that, once
dredging stops, TSS concentrations in the area influenced by semi-diurnal tides typically return
to baseline levels within 6-12 hours if the dredging is stopped. This means that the total duration
of impacts on water quality from the dredging activity will include the dredging duration plus
an additional 6–12 hours. However, as the Rach Goc river water is regularly exchanged by tidal
flows, water quality is expected to return to normal within 6–12 hours after dredging activities
cease. Increased water turbidity from dredging may affect aquatic life.
303. Impacts of increased water turbidity on aquatic life: The dredging activity at the port
will involve one to two machines operating for approximately 30–45 days along a 270 m section
of the Rach Goc river. It alters water quality and flow dynamics, and impact aquatic flora and
fauna in Rach Goc river in various ways (Figure 37):
  − Impacts on phytoplankton. The increase in turbidity and suspended solids in the water
    resulting from dredging prevents sunlight from penetrating deeply into the water, thereby
    limiting the growth and productivity of primary nutrient producers in the aquatic ecosystem
    and impeding the photosynthesis capacity of floating plants. Some phytoplanktons are
    removed from the water with the dredged material or are damaged by the dredging
    equipment. However, the impact on phytoplankton is temporary and localized - as the mud
    normally settles around 6 to 12 hours after dredging stops and the affeted areas are within a
    diameter of 254 meters radius. After dredging is complete, the remaining phytoplankton
    continues to bloom and spread, allowing the ecosystem to recover rather quickly. A total of
    83 phytoplankton species recorded in Rach Goc River may be susceptible to potential
    impacts. However, these species are widely distributed rather than locally endemic. Many
    species in this diverse ecosystem can regain their photosynthetic and respiratory ability as
    pollution concentrations fall.
  − Impacts on zooplankton and zoobenthos. Dredging removes some benthic organisms along
    with their habitat. The others will die if their habitat is damaged by dredging. In total, 35
    species of zooplankton and 22 species of zoobenthos were identified in the project area; all
    are common species not listed under national or international protection frameworks. These
    communities typically recover naturally within one year after sediment conditions stabilize
    (Prussian et al, 1999)12.
  − Impacts on fish. Increased water turbidity and suspended solids can impede their respiration.
    Also, disturbance of the bottom sediments can release CH4 and H2S to adhere to their gill,
    limiting their respiratory function. When their habitat is disturbed, fish and shrimp tend to
    swim away as soon as the dredging begins. No endemic or rare fish species requiring
    protection were found in the Rach Goc River; the aquatic environment is dominated by
    typical native species such as carp, snails, and shrimp. However, if oil and grease are spilled
    into the water during dredging, they cause acute toxicity to aquatic species and deposits in
    aquatic food chain, ranging from low-level flora (algae, phytoplankton) to high-level aquatic
    fauna (fish, etc.).




12
  U.S Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA)'s monitoring of recovery of dredging in a river in Alaska
showed that substantial recovery of the diversity of macro-invertebrate occurred after one year.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     Page           129
                     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




      Figure 37: Characteristics of Aquatic Life in the Project Area and Impacts of Dredging
304. In practice, the designated dredging area is typically maintained on a five-year cycle
(subject to budget availability) and was previously dredged during the construction of the port,
indicating that the current dredging works are unlikely to result in species loss.
305. Given the limited scale and duration, the phased, section-by-section dredging sequencing,
and the absence of rare or endangered aquatic species, and the natural resilience of the
ecosystem, the impacts are assessed to be low, temporary, reversible within 12 months,
consistent with observations from other dredging areas (Table 71), and manageable. To mitigate
the impact, the project owner will develop and implement a Dredging and Material
Management Plan (DMMP), as in Appendix 2 and apply monitoring measures as specified in
Chapter 5.
                  Table 71: Recovery Period Observed in Various Dredging Areas13
          Location                                  Habitat type            Recovery Time
          Coos Bay, Oregon                          Disturbed mud           4 weeks
          Cagaliari Bay, Sardinia                   Channel muds            6 months
          Mobile Bay, Alabama                       Channel muds            6 months
          Goose Creek, Long Island                  Lagoon muds             >11 months
          Klaver Bank, North Sea                    Sand-gravels            1-2 year

d) Environmental pollution risks at temporary storage areas of the dredged materials

306. As noted, all dredged material will be reused in various ways; however, before reuse, it
may cause several environmental impacts, including: (i) potential acid sulfate soil activation;


13
     Page 83, EIA for Corridor 1, The Northern Delta Transport Development Project, 2007
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                           Page   130
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

(ii) risks of overflow and leakage of dredged materials to the surroundings; and (iii) Malodor
generated from dredging, as follows:
d.1). Potential Acid Sulfate Soil Activation
307. Sediment sample analysis from the project site indicates no presence of acid sulfate soils
(ASS) (see Appendix 4), however, data collected from the project communes and stakeholder
consultations revealed that several locations in the broader project area are affected potential
ASS (PAAS). Although dredged material volume is moderate (around 32,593 m³), the risk of
acid release may still occur, if potential acid sulfate soil is present.
308. After dredging, the material will be directly pumped from the barge to the disposal site
through pipelines. As the pumping process uses a large amount of water, PASS activation is
unlikely to occur during pumping. Instead, the risk of acidification may arise during the drying
and consolidation phase at the disposal site, especially during the first rains when the material
becomes exposed. This could affect the soil and vegetation in the port area, as well as the water
quality and aquatic life in Rach Goc River and the Mot Canal if containment fails
309. These risks would be controlled through the design of disposal sites having a containment
area with inward-sloping embankments, which helps confine any potential acid sulfate runoff
within the site, primarily during the first seasonal rains. Additionally, the entire surface of the
fishing port has been concreted, and the port is enclosed by a perimeter wall that isolates it from
the surrounding areas. The port area also contains limited vegetation cover. Furthermore, the
material consists of recently deposited material, as the port is dredged every 5 years, indicating
a low PASS activation potential. Notably, Rach Goc River and Mot Canal do not host any
endangered or protected aquatic species. Furthermore, the local residents and contractors are
experienced in neutralizing soil acidity by the application of power lime, and the material is
available in the local market. Therefore, the potential environmental impact from ASS
activation associated with the material is considered low, localized, and readily manageable
through standard mitigation measures.
d.2). Risks of Overflow/Spreading of Dredged Materials to the Surroundings
310. The dredged material storage site is designed with a dump height of 2.0–2.5 meters, a
crest width of 20 cm, and a containment area of 8,763 m². However, if the material is deposited
at the edge or on top of the pile, there is a risk that the material could overflow beyond the
designated boundary. Additionally, the formed slopes may collapse under the weight of the
material, potentially causing spillage that could cover nearby vegetation, affect existing
infrastructure, or even pose safety risks to people. Affected elements may include four (04)
existing structures within the port, three (03) nearby residential houses, 27 workers at the port,
and surrounding vegetation and households, and quality and aquatic life in Rach Goc River and
Mot Canal. Nevertheless, the entire disposal area is enclosed by a 3-meter-high wall, that
separates it from the surrounding environment. Therefore, even in the event of a collapse, the
overall impact is considered small, confined within the port area, short, and manageable through
mitigation measures outlined in Chapter 5.
d.3). Malodor Generated from Dredging
311. According to the sediment quality analysis in the project area, heavy metal concentrations
(Cd, Pb, Cu) are within the allowable limits set in the QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT standards
(Appendix 4). Besides, the sediment exhibits a pH range of 6.68 to 8.92, indicating slight
alkalinity. However, it contains high levels of biodegradable organic substances that, under
anoxic conditions, release odorous compounds such as inorganic gases, mercaptans, organic
acids, phenol, and para-cresol, causing unpleasant odors.
312. Based on comparable projects in the Mekong Delta, the odor dispersion from dredged
material in canals ranges from 5.42 to 8.42 Ou/m³. Additionally, the dredged material may


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         131
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

produce a fishy smell due to organic compounds and clay, or emit NH3 and CH4 gases from
anaerobic digestion in the water environment.
313. Using the Screen View model to assess odor and air emission dispersion from dredging,
the findings in Figure 38 reveal that: (i) the odor impact from the construction site is considered
LOW; and (ii) emissions from dredged materials are low, remaining under the 200 µg/m³
threshold set by QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT - National Technical Regulation on Air Quality.

                Odour dispersion                                      NH3 dispersion
