e) Potential structural damage to nearby houses from pile-driving activities

314. Vibrations from construction can affect the stability of nearby structures. Repetitive or
intermittent vibrations, even at low levels, may induce material fatigue, gradually reducing
load-bearing capacity, particularly at joints, door corners, or areas with abrupt stiffness changes.
Over time, microcracks can form, initially appearing as cosmetic damage but potentially
developing into structural issues if unmanaged. Structures made of brittle materials such as
brick, mortar, or old concrete are especially vulnerable. Vibrations can also compact foundation
soils, causing differential settlement and additional cracking. Resonance may occur if vibration
frequencies match a building’s natural frequency, amplifying effects and potentially causing
severe damage.
315. Pile driving will be carried out for the construction of the new wharves at the Rach Goc
Fishing Port. The construction will be implemented from the shoreline toward the sea using a
barge-mounted vibratory hammer, following a sequence as shown in Section 1.3.3.1. Only one
pile-driving machine will operate at a time, and each active work front will cover a short section
of about 10 to 15 meters. The vibratory hammer to be used weighs less than 25 tons and operates
at adjustable frequencies of 30–40 Hz to avoid resonance with nearby structures. Each pile is
approximately 12 to 18 meters long and will be driven to a depth of 15 to 20 meters through
soft alluvial clay typical of the province. The construction sequence will be carried out
gradually and section by section, which helps minimize cumulative vibration effects. The port
construction area is located on weak soil, which can alter vibration propagation and amplitude.
Fine-grained or saturated soils near the source may amplify certain frequencies or cause uneven
settlement, increasing the risk of cracking in brick walls and lightweight masonry structures.
316. Based on similar Mekong Delta projects, and considering the weak soil conditions at Rach
Goc, vibration intensity is expected to decrease quickly with distance. Within a 10-meter radius
of the piling point, vibration levels may reach 3–5 mm/s, potentially causing minor, cosmetic
cracks on plaster or at wall corners in lightweight buildings. Between 10 and 25 meters,
vibration intensity will range from 1–2 mm/s, remaining below the allowable 5 mm/s threshold

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         132
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

specified in TCVN 6962:2001, and thus is not expected to cause structural damage. Beyond
40–50 meters, vibration becomes negligible (less than 0.5 mm/s).
317. Within these ranges, the nearest household—approximately 18–20 meters from the piling
site—has a brick wall and metal roof structure and may experience small, non-structural
hairline cracks at door corners or mortar joints. Other structures (port office, water-supply
station, toilet block, and one local house) are 30 - 45 meters away, where vibration impacts are
insignificant. No schools, pagodas, fuel depots, or sensitive structures are within 50-m radius
of the piling area (see Figure 39).
318. To prevent and control vibration impacts, the contractor will apply a controlled piling
method using a hydraulic vibratory hammer with frequency adjustment and short operation
periods per pile. Piling will be conducted sequentially, ensuring only one piling point is active
at any time while adjacent sections remain undisturbed. The contractor will carry out pre- and
post-construction surveys with photographic documentation to record any cracks or
deformations in surrounding buildings. If any structural damage attributable to the piling
activity is confirmed, the contractor will be responsible for repairs or compensation.
319. Given the moderate hammer weight, soft alluvial soils that dampen vibration, short
construction duration of about 30 to 45 days, and phased construction approach, the risk of
significant structural damage is low. Impacts are expected to be limited to minor, cosmetic
cracking in the nearest lightweight house, which will be reversible and easily repairable.
Therefore, no relocation or permanent compensation is required.




                      Figure 39: Structures potentially affected by vibration

f) Impacts and Risks on nearby local businesses

320. Local businesses near the port upgrade area include a coffee shop and a grocery store.
The potential impacts on these businesses are as follows:
 − The coffee shop is located approximately 50 meters from the construction site and operates
   daily from 6:00 to 18:00, serving around 7–10 customers per day, mainly port staff.
   Customers typically visit during peak hours between 8:00–9:00 and 11:30–13:30, and each
   visit lasts between 30 minutes and 1 hour. During construction, construction activities may
   generate dust, noise, vibration, and localized runoff. However, the impact level is considered
   low due to the short exposure duration of customers, the relatively large distance from the
   construction area, and the impacts can be mitigated by measures in Chapter 5.
 − Similarly, the grocery store, located about 100 meters from the site and physically separated
   from the construction area by a wall, serves only 3–5 customers per day, with each customer
   typically staying for 5–7 minutes. Given the very limited number of customers, brief
   exposure duration, and greater distance from construction activities, the potential impacts
   from dust, noise, vibration, and localized flooding are assessed as small, short-term, highly


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                Page      133
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   localized, and fully mitigable by measures for generic impacts during construction as in
   Chapter 5.

4.4.2.2. Site-specific impacts and risks during upgrading of the Embankments

321. The embankment upgrades will be implemented along existing narrow roads (1.2 to 3
meters wide) that are currently in poor condition. These roads have been severely degraded
over time due to localized flooding caused by heavy rainfall and tidal intrusion. The proposed
upgrade area traverses aquaculture zones across five communes, where transportation
infrastructure is limited and primarily designed for motorbike access only. Accordingly, the
key site-specific impacts associated with the embankment upgrading include: (i) impacts on
aquaculture activities; (ii) impacts on business and livelihood activities; (iii) impacts on public
infrastructure such as bridges and power poles; and (iv) impacts on sensitive receptors.

a) Impacts on Aquaculture

322. The impacts of the embankment upgrading on shrimp farming activities are mainly
associated with: (i) stormwater runoff in areas where the embankment alignment intersects
shrimp ponds; and (ii) potential damage or obstruction of water intake sluices essential for
shrimp cultivation.
323. Impacts on aquaculture due to stormwater runoff and water pollution: The
embankment upgrade will require excavation of soil along the existing alignment and
placement of the excavated materials onto the new embankment slopes. At locations where the
embankment intersects shrimp ponds, these activities may temporarily create exposed surfaces
and temporary stockpiles of excavated materials. If such works occur during rainfall,
stormwater may wash loose or uncompacted materials into adjacent ponds or surrounding
waterways, potentially degrading water quality and causing shrimp mortality. The impacts are
limited, localized, and expected to last 25-30 days for a 100-meter section. Approximately 10-
20 ponds would be affected. Based on experience from similar works (especially under the WB
9 – Mekong Delta Integrated Climate Resilience and Sustainable Livelihood (MD-ICRSL))
several measures can help minimize these impacts. The contractors could negotiate with
farmers to schedule construction to take place between crops, after harvesting and before
seeding the next batch. The new sections passing shrimp ponds should be first isolated with
bamboo sheets placed in between Melaleuca piles to reduce the dispersion of the filling
materials into water. In addition, prior consultation and agreements with pond owners are
essential to ensure smooth implementation.
324. Impacts on aquaculture due to damage/obstruction of water intake culverts for
shrimp ponds: Field survey results show that within the embankment area, there are
approximately 940 culverts used by local households to supply water for their shrimp ponds
under the existing embankments. These culverts vary in size and condition, with some in good
and others in poor condition. Currently, shrimp farming is the main livelihood of local residents.
Any disruption to the water intake culverts would significantly impact household income and
economic stability. Based on experience from previous embankment-upgrade projects in the
area, excavation and filling works along the embankment surface often damage water-intake
culverts, thereby affecting shrimp farming activities. Therefore, shrimp farmers have raised
concerns about potential impacts on their culverts during numerous consultation meetings about
the project.
325. In response to these concerns, the project will include the construction of reinforced
crossing slabs at points where the embankment intersects existing culverts without carrying out
any excavation or filling in these areas. This measure is designed to prevent structural damage,
obstruction, and ensure the culverts remain fully functional during embankment upgrading.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         134
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

326. These reinforced slabs are designed to accommodate the existing culverts, which have
elevations ranging from +1.0m to +1.6m, while the proposed elevation of the crossing slabs or
the embankment is +2.5m. The slab configuration provides a clearance space of approximately
1–1.5m in height, and 3–4m in width, allowing farmers to access and undertake necessary
maintenance or repairs when required. Nevertheless, installing these crossing slabs will make
culvert maintenance more challenging if damage occurs; however, repairs will still be possible.
To minimize future difficulties, local authorities have advised households with degraded
culverts to repair them before construction begin, as maintenance will be more difficult once
the embankment is completed. Most affected households have confirmed their intention to
complete culvert repairs prior to construction activities.
327. The potential impact on aquaculture from damage or obstruction of water intake culverts
is assessed as moderate and mitigable. With the project’s design considerations and proactive
actions by local households, the risk of long-term disruption to shrimp farming is significantly
reduced. Further guidance on implementation and monitoring is provided in Chapter 5 to ensure
continued functionality and access for maintenance.

b) Impacts on Business Activities

328. As previously mentioned, there are approximately 47 business households along the
76.72 km of embankment route, including cafes, grocery stores, shrimp purchasing stations,
billiard halls, and karaoke bars (see Section 2.6.1). Embankment upgradation activities along
the existing roads will temporarily block the entrances to these businesses, making it difficult
for customers to access them. However, the impact is assessed as low, temporary, and localized,
due to the following reasons: (i) each business has a relatively small customer base, and the
area is fairly isolated, so customers have limited alternative options for goods and services;
(ii) the disruption at each location will last only about 25–30 days. Mitigation measures will
include requiring contractors to provide temporary access roads, maintain clear entryways to
businesses, and strictly adhere to the construction schedule to avoid unnecessary delays (see
details in Chapter 5).

c) Impacts on Existing Infrastructure

329. Risk of bridges damage and access disruption: Field investigations identified 70 canal-
crossing bridges within the embankment upgrade area (see Appendix 8), including three
structurally weak bridges - one wooden and two deteriorated concrete bridges with visible
surface cracks -located approximately 10 m from the construction works. The embankment
upgrade will not involve transporting materials across these bridges, and all works will be
carried out at least 10 m away from them. No piling or vibration-intensive activities are planned;
works will be limited to small-scale excavation along the canal bank, slope shaping, placement
of geotextile, sand filling, and concrete surfacing.
330. Given the physical separation and low-intensity construction methods, the potential
impact on the three structurally weak bridges and others is assessed as low, with only minor
and localized risks anticipated. However, if damage or failure of any of the bridges occurs, -
for example, due to vibrations from the movement of heavy construction plants, - access
through the bridges may be disturbed or even interrupted. there are alternative bridges of good
quality located within 3–5 km that affected households can use if needed. On the other hand,
damage caused to the existing infrastructures could pose safety risks, including injuries or
fatalities for pedestrians or vehicle users. Only about 5–6 households live in the immediate
vicinity and use these bridges. These households have no school-aged children and are not
classified as vulnerable.
331. Considering these factors, the overall potential social impact associated with bridge
damaged and safery is assessed as low and localized. However, enhanced monitoring and

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          135
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

proactive communication with affected households are recommended to prevent, detect, and
manage any unforeseen incidents promptly. In addition, the project owner will require
contractors to implement immediate repairs or provide appropriate compensation if any bridge
damage occurs during construction.

d) Impacts and Risks on Sensitive Receptors

332. According to Table 36, schools, commune cultural houses, Ethnic Minorities and shrines
have been identified as sensitive receptors potentially affected by the embankment upgrading.
The potential impacts and risks associated with the remaining sensitive receptors are detailed
below.
333. PCRs: There are 8 shrines of the goddess located within 10-30m of the embankment
construction areas (Table 36). Although no land acquisition at these sites is required,
construction activities may impact these shrines through dust, noise, and vibration, as well as
by disrupting access, creating visual disturbances, or causing nuisance if construction materials
and waste are not properly managed. A loud noise could also interfere with religious
ceremonies. Furthermore, as the shrines are quite old and have simple structures, construction
activities could potentially compromise their structural integrity. Nevertheless, the impacts and
risks are considered low, short-term, and manageable due to: (i) the very limited operation
period and small number of visitors (10–30 people) and activities occur only three times a
year—from January to March in the lunar calendar—for about 3–4 hours each day; (ii) the short
duration of construction activities in front of the shrines (25–30 days), with construction being
suspended during worship events; and (iii) contractors will document the existing conditions of
the shrines with photographs prior to construction and will be responsible for compensation
should any damage occur.
334. Schools: Two primary school branches located near the embankment upgrading routes
may be affected: Nha Thinh branch of Dong Thoi 3 Primary School (along Lung Dau Dua
canal) and Khanh Tu branch of Dong Thoi 2 Primary School (along Ngang canal). These
schools operate Monday to Friday and accommodate approximately 150–200 students and
teachers. Being only 10 meters away, the classrooms faces considerable risk and impac nearby
e construction area. Key impacts include dust, noise, and vibration; increased risk of inundation;
restricted and unsafe access to school gates; risk of traffic accidents; and distraction of students
during class hours. These impacts mainly affect students, teachers, and parents. Construction
activities will occur during school hours (6:30–12:00 and 13:00–17:00), with each affected
section lasting approximately 7 to 30 days. Overall, impacts are assessed as moderate, localized,
and short-term, and can be effectively mitigated through phased construction methods and site
management measures.
335. Hamlet cultural houses: Three hamlet cultural houses (located in My Dien hamlet of
Cai Nuoc commune, Nha Thinh B hamlet of Cai Nuoc commune, and Tan An A hamlet of Ta
An Khuong commune) are situated approximately 20–30 meters from the embankment. These
facilities host cultural events, community meetings, and public gatherings around three times
per month, mainly during evenings and weekends, accommodating 30–70 people per event.
During the embankment upgrading, potential impacts include the risk of inundation, temporary
difficulty in accessing the facilities, and increased risk of traffic accidents near the construction
route. The impacts are expected to last for approximately 7–30 days per facility. Overall, the
impacts are assessed as moderate, localized, short-term, and mitigable by applying sequential
construction approach.
336. Ethnic Minorities (EMs) households are present in the implementation area of the
embankments. While EMs stand to gain long-term benefits from the project’s components,
especially from the project embankments, they may also face adverse effects the potential
negative impacts as follows:

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page          136
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

  −  Exposure to public safety and health risks: The mobilization of workers to the embankment
     construction sites may cause disruptions to the economic, social, and cultural life of the
     EM communities in the area.
  − Exposure to common construction-related impacts and risks: These include increased
     levels of dust, gas emissions, noise, vibration, loss of vegetation cover and trees, waste and
     wastewater generation, water quality reduction affecting aquatic lives, soil erosion and
     sedimentation, and localized flooding risks.
  − Social disturbance related to the presence of migrant workers: During construction time,
     many workers will come and stay in the project area. This may cause social difficulties,
     affecting local security and health, especially young EM people.
337. The scale and magnitude of the impacts on EM are higher than those of the Kinh people.
These impacts include social disruption, health risks, and exposure to construction-related
disturbances. The presence of a large workforce may also lead to temporary social and health
risks, such as disruption of social order, increased pressure on local services, and heightened
exposure to communicable diseases. These risks are assessed as moderate, short-term, and
localized, and are considered manageable and mitigable through measures outlined in Chapter
5 and the Ethnic Minority Development Plan (EMDP).

4.4.2.3. Site-specific impacts during canal dredging

338. A total of six canals, with a combined length of 14.81 km, will be dredged to achieve a
designed bottom width of 10 m and a bottom depth ranging from –2.0 to –2.5 m. Based on the
project’s location, scale of investment, and the socio-environmental conditions of the area
described in Chapter 2, the key potential impacts and risks associated with canal dredging
include: (i) impacts on aquaculture activities; (ii) deterioration of water quality and effects on
aquatic organisms due to dredging operations; (iii) environmental pollution risks at the
temporary storage areas of dredged materials; (iv) impacts on existing infrastructure; and
(v) impacts on waterway navigation.

a) Impacts on Aquaculture

339. The 6 canals to be dredged currently serve as the primary water supply for aquaculture.
They are from 10 to 15m wide and from 2 to 2.5m deep. They have been sedimented, thus
narrowed down, flow obstructed, and water transmission capacity reduced. The dredging may
increase water turbidity in the 6 canals, potentially affecting the water quality and impacting
the water source for aquaculture ponds in the 3 communes of Tan Thuan, Phu Tan, and Phu
My.
340. There are 200 hectares of shrimp ponds belonging to 170 households in Cai Nuoc, Tan
Thuan, and Phu Tan communes. Water is taken into the ponds through simple culverts forn 2–
5 days each month, mainly during high tides. As presented above, dredging may cause turbidity
to increase up to 300 mg/L at 50 m and 40 mg/L at 200m from the dredging point. According
to QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT – National Water Quality Standard for Aquatic lives, the allowable
limit for TSS is 100 mg/L and DO is at least 4 mg/L. Baseline data showed that TSS in the
project areas in the dry season (the time that dreging will be carried out) varies from 16 to 89
mg/L, and DO ranges from 6 to 7 mg/L. Therefore, dredging would potentially make the TSS
in the canal exceed allowable limits 4 times along the section from the dredging point to 254 m
downstream. Although this impact cannot be completely avoided, it is manageable as dredging
at each section lasts just a few days. The potential impacts of dredging on shrimp farming would
further be mitigated through the measures presented in Chapter 5 and the Dredging and Material
Management Plan (see Appendix 2).
341. The impact is considered moderate, localized, and manageable due to the following
factors: (i) duckbill excavators will be used to minimize turbidity increases in the canals;
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         137
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

(ii) dredging activities for each canal will last only 7–20 days; (iii) turbidity increases will be
confined within 254-meter radius from the dredging point, (see Section 4.4.2.5.b); (iv) the
construction plan will be consulted with and notified to residents at least 15 days prior to
commencement, this helps locals to properly arrange their production plan; and (v) the farmers
only open the water intake a few days each month. The contractors will implement measures in
Chapter 5 to maintain a clean construction site and ensure the dredged canals are left in good
condition.

b) Deterioration of Water Quality and Effects on Aquatic Life Due to Dredging Activities

342. Water Quality Reduction: Dredging activities in the canals will result in reduced water
quality and disturbance to aquatic ecosystems across six affected canals. Similar to dredging at
Rach Goc fishing port, the dispersion range of SS from the dredging locations is estimated to
extend up to 254 meters into the surrounding areas. Water quality monitoring data presented in
Chapter 2 and Appendix 4 show that TSS in the project canals varied between 16 to 48 mg/L,
below QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (100mg/L for irrigation purposes). With an increase in SS
concentration at 200 meters from the dredging point: ΔCmax=(1/5)×200=40 mg/L, total TSS
concentration in the dredging point will range 56 to 248 mg/L. Therefore, the maximum TSS
concentration may exceed the Class B limit by approximately 2.48 times under worst-case
conditions. This exceedance is temporary and typically persists for 3–5 hours during dredging
operations before being reduced through natural dilution and tidal flushing, returning to
baseline conditions within one tidal cycle (6–12 hours).
343. Based on the calculated dispersion distance (approximately 254m), the canal width of
10m, and the dredging depth of 2-2.5m, together with flow velocities ranging from 0.1 to 0.5
m/s, suspended solids generated during dredging are expected to disperse and dilute rapidly.
Empirical evidence from similar dredging activities in the Mekong Delta shows that, once
dredging stops, TSS concentrations in areas influenced by semi-diurnal tides typically return to
baseline levels within 6-12 hours if the dredging is stopped. This means that the total duration
of impacts on water quality from the canal dredging activities will include the dredging duration
plus an additional 6–12 hours. However, as the canal water is regularly exchanged by tidal
flows, water quality is expected to return to normal within 6–12 hours after dredging activities
cease.
344. Impacts on aquatic lives: The dredging works along six canal sections, each ranging
from 1.28 to 3.80 km in length will be carried out using duckbill dredgers operating sequentially
over a short duration of 7–20 days per canal. These activities will temporarily increase water
turbidity and suspended solids, altering local flow dynamics and light penetration, affecting
aquatic flora and fauna in various ways:
  − Impacts on phytoplakton: The increase in turbidity may temporarily reduce sunlight
     penetration, limiting photosynthesis and productivity of phytoplankton and other aquatic
     plants. A total of 83 phytoplankton species previously recorded in these canals are
     common, widely distributed, and not endemic. Once the dredging ceases and suspended
     sediments settle, phytoplankton communities are expected to recover quickly through
     natural blooming and dispersion.
  − Zooplankton and zoobenthos may be directly affected by sediment disturbance and habitat
     removal in the dredging corridor. In total, 35 zooplankton species and 22 zoobenthos
     species have been identified in the canals, all of which are common and not listed as rare
     or protected. These benthic and planktonic communities typically recover within several
     months to one year once sediment conditions stabilize.
  − Fish and shrimp species inhabiting the canals are mainly small native species, such as carp,
     snakehead, goby, and freshwater shrimp. During dredging, they will tend to move away


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         138
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

     from disturbed areas due to noise and increased turbidity. No rare, endemic, or
     economically threatened fish species were found in the canals.
345. In practice, the canals identified for dredging are maintained on a five-year cycle,
suggesting that the project’s dredging activities are unlikely to cause species loss. The
associated impacts are temporary and reversible, as the habitat and benthic ecosystems typically
recover within 12 months (see Table 71). In addition, the use of duckbill dredgers, particularly
suitable for canals of this type will help minimize turbidity and reduce impacts on
phytoplankton and fish populations. As a result, the overall dredging impacts are considered
moderate and controllable. Additionally, the project owner will prepare a Dredging and
Material Management Plan (DMMP) as in Appendix 2 and implement monitoring measures as
outlined in Chapter 5 to mitigate the impacts.

c) Environmental pollution risks at temporary storage areas of the dredged materials

346. As mentioned in Section 1.3.5.3, all dredged materials from the canal dredging activities
will be transported to the designated embankment upgrading sites for reuse as filling materials
for the embankments. However, prior to reuse, the temporary storage of these dredged materials
may pose several potential environmental impacts, including: (i) activation of acid sulfate soils;
(ii) leakage and surface runoff; (iii) risks of overflow or dispersion of dredged materials into
surrounding areas; and (iv) generation of unpleasant odors, as detailed below:
c.1). Potential Acid Sulfate Soil Activation
347. As mentioned in the section describing dredging activities at Rach Goc Port, dredging
may expose acid sulfate soil (ASS) layers in the dark gray clay sediments and causing
acidification of soils and water in the project area. After dredging, the material will be spread
along the entire 76.72 km of embankment for drying over approximately 7–10 days before
being reused for embankment reinforcement. Rainwater can leach the acid into surrounding
soils or nearby canals, degrading water quality, soil fertility, vegetation, and aquatic
ecosystems.
348. Although the canals are regularly dredged on a 3–5-year cycle—meaning the sediments
are relatively recent and less likely to contain high levels of ASS—and most vegetation along
the embankment has limited ecological or economic value, the risk remains notable for
aquaculture households. Approximately 170 households rely on canal water for aquaculture.
Acid leaching during the 8–10 days each month when water is abstracted for shrimp farming
could have severe consequences. If leaching occurs outside of this period, the impact is
expected to be low. Overall, the impact of acid sulfate soil activation is assessed as small if it
occurs outside the water abstraction period, and major if it coincides with water abstraction
activities. However, local communities and authorities are well experienced in managing such
risks due to the routine nature of dredging in the area, and lime application is a common and
effective mitigation measure. The project owner will ensure that contractors implement all
mitigation measures as proposed in Chapter 5 and the Dredging and Material Management Plan
(Appendix 2) to manage and minimize this impact.
c.2). Risks of Overflow/Spreading of Dredged Materials to the Surroundings
349. According to the FS report, the temporary material storage sites are divided into
compartments, each no more than 5m long, about 6m wide, and up to 1.2m high. Embankments
will be built along the edge of residential gardens and yards, with a crest width of 0.5 m and a
height reaching up to +2.5 m, effectively preventing dredged material from spilling into local
gardens, houses, and shrimp ponds. Additionally, every 200 m, a drainage 0.5 m wide drainge
ditch will be constructed to discharge water back into the canal, ensuring timely drainage during
rainfall and preventing overflow into gardens or shrimp ponds located in front of the material


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          139
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

compartments. Consequently, the maximum rainwater runoff from each compartment during
heavy rainfall is estimated at 1.5m³/day (calculated for 50mm/day of rain).
350. In the event of an embankment breach, a mixture of water, dredged materials, and other
existing substances on the ground surface may overflow into the household land located along
the affected section. Field surveys show that along the embankment routes, the front side
includes 940 water intake culverts (see Appendix 8), 1,070 gardens/yards and houses, and 47
business establishments such as cafes, grocery stores, and shrimp collection points (Table 72),
followed by shrimp ponds that may be affected by flooding.
                   Table 72: Business Facilities Along the Project Embankments
                                                       Affected businesses (facilities)
  No.          Communes                                      Others (shrimp collection points,
                                     Coffee        Grocery
                                                              karaoke clubs, billiard clubs,      Total
                                     shops          stores
                                                                          etc.)
   1     Ta An Khuong                         2          2                                   2          6
   2     Tan Thuan                            8          9                                   4         21

351. The risk of simultaneous failure across all compartments is very low. In the event of a
breach, it would likely be confined to a single compartment, potentially affecting only 2–3
nearby households. However, the economic value of shrimp farming is high, and contamination
or flooding of a shrimp pond could result in substantial financial losses for an entire production
cycle. Therefore, the risk is considered low in terms of probability but potentially substantial in
economic consequence, which may warrant risk rating of Moderate overall.
c.3). Malodor Generated from Dredging
352. According to the sediment quality analysis in the project area, it contains high levels of
biodegradable organic substances that, under anoxic conditions, release odorous compounds
such as inorganic gases, mercaptans, organic acids, phenol, and para-cresol, causing unpleasant
odors. Based on comparable projects in the Mekong Delta, the odor dispersion from dredged
material in canals ranges from 5.42 to 8.42 Ou/m³. Additionally, the dredged material may
produce a fishy smell due to organic compounds and clay, or emit NH3 and CH4 gases from
anaerobic digestion in the water environment. Using the Screen View model to assess odor and
air emission dispersion from dredging, the findings in Figure 38 reveal that: (i) the odor impact
from the construction site is considered LOW; and (ii) emissions from dredged materials are
low, remaining under the 200 µg/m³ threshold set by QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT - National
Technical Regulation on Air Quality.

d) Damage Risks on Existing Infrastructure

353. Survey findings indicate that 3 reinforced concrete bridges located along the dredging
alignment are currently in good condition. Dredging activities will take place on the water
surface using duckbill buckets and barges, without piling or blasting, and with a limited
operational footprint at each location. Consequently, the likelihood of significant structural
damage is low.




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   Page          140
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

e) Impacts on the Waterway

354. The dredging activities on the six canals may affect waterway traffic primarily due to the
presence of dredging vessels and barges stationed while waiting to transport dredged materials
to the sites used for embankment upgrading. This could make navigation more difficult for local
boats passing through these sections, and may potentially lead to collisions between local
vessels and project equipment (e.g., dredgers or barges), or between local boats themselves—
especially during peak hours, at night, or in extreme weather conditions such as tropical
depressions, strong winds, or storms. If such incidents occur, they could result in damage to
property, pose safety risks to residents, and endanger the lives of construction workers operating
in the affected waterway sections. However, these impacts are assessed to be small due to the
following factors: (i) dredging is carried out sequentially (rolling manner), and the dredging
duration for each canal is relatively short (approximately 7–20 days); (ii) the occupied area for
dredging equipment at each site is small (approximately 5 x 20 meters) compared to the canal
width of 10 to 15m; and (iii) the traffic density on these canals is low (about 2-3 boats per day),
consisting mostly of small boats of 5 tons, and the remaining canal width of 5 to 10m after
equipment placement is still sufficient to allow local vessels to pass easily.

4.4.2.4. Site-specific Impacts due to the Installation of Water Monitoring Stations

355. A preliminary assessment, based on similar facilities constructed under the previous WB9
project, indicates that the stations will likely consist of small brick or reinforced concrete
structures housing monitoring equipment, with short access walkways on pile foundations
along riverbanks. During construction, the installation of water level monitoring stations in
waterways with dense vessel traffic may cause temporary, localized obstructions to navigation
channels.
356. At the feasibility study (FS) stage, however, the precise scope and locations of the
proposed monitoring stations have not yet been determined. As a result, site-specific impacts
cannot be fully assessed at this time; these will be addressed during the detailed design phase.

4.5. IMPACTS AND RISKS IN THE OPERATION PHASE

4.5.1. Impacts and Risk during Operation of the Rach Goc Port
357. During the operation phase of the port, E&S impacts and risks are expected to arise from
the following sources: (i) the operation of ships, boats, and cargo trucks, as well as fish and
seafood loading and unloading activities; (ii) the daily activities of port operators, fishermen,
ship and boat drivers, and visitors; (iii) the continuous operation of vessels transporting aquatic
products in and out of the port; (iv) the operation of functional areas within the port, such as
receiving and sorting zones; and (v) the operation of technical infrastructure systems, including
the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), waste storage facilities, and public toilets.
358. Therefore, the following environmental and social impacts and risks may occur: (i) Dust,
and air emissions; (ii) Noise; (iii) Wastewater generation; (iv) Solid waste generation; (v)
Deterioration of the quality of water resources and aquatic life; (vi) Traffic risks and incidents;
(vii) Potential occupational risks and (viii) Potential environmental risks and incidents.
Followings are the detailed assessment of the impacts and risks:

4.5.1.1. Dust and Air Emissions

359. After the upgraded fishing port becomes operational, the number of boats docking at the
port and the number of vehicles delivering fishing outputs to consumption sites will increase,
especially with the improvement of the road connecting the port to the Ho Chi Minh Highway.
These vehicles use gasoline and diesel (DO), leading to a rise in air pollutants such as dust,
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         141
                     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

SO₂, NOₓ, and COx. However, the level of pollution will still depend on the number of vessels
operating at the port and the volume of fish caught.
360. Dust and air emissions from trucks: According to the project’s FS report, when the port
becomes operational, it will handle a cargo volume of 18,000 tons per year. The volume will
generate around 3–6 refrigerated truck trips per day, with a carrying capacity of 8–10 tons per
vehicle. Using the WHO (2003) rapid emission factors for diesel trucks operating in suburban
areas, the average emission factors are 0.9 kg dust, 1.44 kg NOₓ, 4.15S kg SO₂ (with S = 0.05%
sulfur), 2.9 kg CO, and 0.8 kg HC per 1,000 km of vehicle operation. Applying the standard
emission calculation formula:
                                                𝑒×𝑛
                                         𝐸=
                                             13,600 × 𝑡
(where E is the emission load in mg/m·s, e is the emission factor, n is the number of trips per
day, and t is the daily operating time, approximately three hours).
361. The total pollutant load generated from fish transport trucks is estimated at 0.012–0.018
mg/m·s for dust, 0.020–0.030 mg/m·s for NOₓ, and 0.001–0.002 mg/m·s for SO₂, while CO
and HC emissions are much lower. These calculated loads are significantly below the thresholds
of QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT, indicating minimal ambient air impacts (Table 73). In practice,
trucks operate intermittently and keep their engines off while waiting for loading, resulting in
very limited continuous emissions. The operational period for these vehicles is short, and the
surrounding area is an open, low-density rural environment with strong air dispersion. Only a
small number of workers (7 port officials and 20 fish-handling workers) and a few households
along the port access road are occasionally exposed to short-term exhaust emissions.
Futhermore, boat owners will be incentivized to sell their items to merchants utilizing registered
transport vehicles. Consequently, the impact is evaluated as low, long term, and controllable by
measures outlined in Chapter 5.

      interim target for 24h
362. Dust and emissions from boats: Once the fishing port operates at a stable capacity, the
number of vessels docking at the port will increase by 100 arrivals per day. During the docking
process, short-term emissions of exhaust gases and fine dust will be generated from vessel
engines operating at low power while maneuvering and during cargo handling. Based on the
WHO (1992) emission factors for medium-size diesel fishing vessels, each docking turn
generates small quantities of pollutants such as dust, SOₓ, NOₓ, CO, and VOC. When
aggregated for all vessels operating within a day, the total daily emission load is estimated at
around 2.5–3.0 mg/s of NOₓ, 0.2–0.3 mg/s of SOₓ, and 0.1 mg/s of dust, which remain well
below the Vietnamese ambient air quality limits (QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT).
363. At peak periods, up to five to six boats may dock simultaneously at the wharf. Even under
this cumulative condition, the overall air quality impact remains localized and temporary, as
the Rach Goc River area is open and well-ventilated, allowing for rapid dispersion of exhaust
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    Page             142
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

gases. Each boat typically remains at the wharf for 20–30 minutes to complete offloading and
clearance procedures, after which engines are switched off. Consequently, total daily operation
time of engines of all boats at the port is short—estimated at about 2–3 hours in total—and
emissions occur intermittently rather than continuously. The main receptors exposed to exhaust
emissions include approximately 7 port officials and 20 fish-handling workers who work within
the port. No residential area lies immediately adjacent to the docking zone. Given the small
number of vessels, short docking duration, and good natural ventilation, air emissions from
fishing vessels are assessed as low in magnitude, short-term (2–3 hours), cease once the ships
complete their unloading and clearance procedures, and manageable by measures in Chapter 5.
364. Fishy smell arises from the fishing boats when they arrive at the port: During the
operation of Rach Goc fishing port, fishy odors may arise when fishing vessels arrive and
unload seafood. These odors primarily originate from NH₃ and hydrocarbons emitted from
refrigeration systems and the natural decomposition of organic matter in seafood such as fish,
shrimp, and crabs. Other odor-causing gases include CO₂, H₂S, CO, CH₄, and mercaptans, with
NH₃, H₂S, and mercaptans being the main contributors to unpleasant smells affecting fishermen,
traders, and buyers at the port.
365. The intensity of fishy odors is assessed as small, as seafood arrivals are intermittent and
occur mainly during specific times of the day, between 7:00-8:00 a.m. and 2:00-3:00 p.m., and
the port is located in an area with good air quality and natural ventilation, which helps disperse
odors and reduces their persistence and overall impact.

4.5.1.2. Noise

366. The main source of noise at the port originates from boats anchoring, with noise levels
ranging from 80 to 85 dBA. The affected population includes 27 port workers and 3 nearby
households. However, the impact of this noise is considered low and long-term due to the port's
spacious and well-ventilated layout, which significantly reduces noise levels—even near the
wharves.

4.5.1.3. Wastewater Generation

367. The sources of wastewater generated from the operation of Rach Goc fishing port include:
(i) domestic and fish sorting wastewater; (ii) rainwater runoff and (iii) ballast water. The details
are presented below:

a) Wastewater from port workers and fish sorting

368. During the operation, wastewater at the Port primarily originates from domestic activities
of port staff and traders, fish sorting and cleaning operations, floor washing, and ice-making
processes. According to the Port Management Board, the total wastewater generation is
estimated at approximately 98.33 m³/day, including 2.68 m³/day from domestic use, about
70m³/day from fish sorting and washing, 15.6 m³/day from floor washing, and 10 m³/day from
ice-making (see Table 74).
                      Table 74: Wastewater volume from the port operation
  No.         Type of wastewater               Calculation      Quantity        Wastewater
                                                  rate                        volume (m³/day)
   1     Domestic activities                 25L/person/day        107                       2.68
   2     Fish sorting                              1 L/kg      70,000 kg                       70
   3     Floor washing                             5 L/m²       3,130 m²                    15.65

     4      Ice-making                       10,000 L/facility   1 facility                  10
            Total                                                                         98.33
369. Domestic wastewater mainly contains suspended solids, BOD₅, COD, nutrients (TN, TP),
and coliforms, while wastewater from fish sorting and cleaning is rich in organic matter, fish
residues, oil, and blood, with BOD concentrations ranging from 1,000 to 10,000 mg/L,
according to the Port Management Board. If these wastewater streams are discharged directly
into the environment, they could cause eutrophication, oxygen depletion, unpleasant odors, and
degradation of water quality in the Rach Goc River and Mot Canal.
370. At present, the port is equipped with adequate restroom facilities and a centralized
wastewater treatment system designed with a capacity of 250 m³/day, sufficient to treat the
combined volume of domestic, fish-washing, and floor-cleaning wastewater. Wastewater from
septic tanks, fish sorting areas, and cleaning zones is collected and conveyed to this treatment
system. However, the system has been inactive for an extended period, raising concerns
regarding its operational readiness after the port’s upgrade. To address this, the Port
Management Board will check and fix (if necessary) it to ensure full compliance with national
discharge standards before any effluent is released. In addition, clean production practices and
water-saving measures will be implemented to minimize water use and wastewater generation.
With these provisions, the impact of wastewater discharge is assessed as negligible, localized,
and manageable, not requiring additional mitigation measures.

b) Rainwater Runoff

371. Stormwater runoff is generally considered a clean type of wastewater. However, during
operation of the sorting facilities, this runoff may carry pollutants—primarily suspended
solids—from surface areas into the drainage system. Although the existing fishing port is
equipped with a stormwater drainage system, it is not adequately designed or fully integrated.
As a result, during rainfall, surface runoff may cause blockages, impairing the system’s
performance and potentially leading to localized flooding. Such flooding could disrupt
transportation, customs clearance procedures, and overall port operations.
372. Since the port is located at the confluence of the Rach Goc River and Mot Canal, rainwater
runoff can transport pollutants directly into these water bodies, negatively impacting water
quality and the local aquatic ecosystem. To address this, the current rainwater drainage system
will be upgraded as part of the port improvement design to ensure effective stormwater
management during rainfall events. Therefore, this impact is assessed as moderate, long-term
during operation phase, and can be mitigated through proper design and management measures.

c)       Ballast Water

373. When a ship is operating without cargo, it carries a certain amount of ballast water. which
is pumped into ballast tanks at the port of departure to maintain stability. Before the ship docks
to load cargo, the entire volume of ballast water is discharged. The ballast water can be
seawater, brackish water or fresh water, depending on the geographical location where the ship
performs the water refill operation. At riverside fishing ports, especially in areas near river
estuaries, ballast water is usually brackish water or seawater due to the impact of tides and
saltwater intrusion.
374. During the process of ballast water refilling, various species such as plankton,
microorganisms, and larvae can be sucked into the water tank and move along the ship to other
water bodies. The discharge of untreated ballast water can cause the spread of alien organisms,
affecting the indigenous ecosystem and the water quality of the Rach Goc River and its
intersecting Mot Canal.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         144
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

375. The upgraded fishing port is expected to handle 100 vessels per day, each under 30m in
length. For small and medium-sized fishing boats, the amount of ballast water ranges from 10
to 50 m³. The ballast water is used by fishing boats to ensure offshore operation and when
harvesting seafood is often reused. With a scale of about 100 boats of the Port, each replacement
will generate about 1,000 m³ to 5,000 m³ of ballast water. However, the ballast water does not
need to be changed too frequently, and the inspection and maintenance of the ballast water tank
is normally carried out periodically from 6 months to 1 year.
376. Domestic fishing boats in Viet Nam are small and medium in capacity, and do not operate
in international waters, so they are not yet subject to the full mandatory provisions of the
International Ballast Water Management Convention (BWM Convention). However, the
discharge of ballast water still needs to comply with existing national regulations such as
Decree No. 58/2017/ND-CP. According to regulations, the ballast water shall be discharged at
least 200 nautical miles from the nearest shore and in waters with a depth of at least 200 m. In
case the boat is unable to meet this condition, the boat may carry out a ballast water exchange
at least 50 nautical miles from the nearest shore and at a depth of at least 200 m (quite far from
the shore). In fact, fishing boats often discharge ballast water individually and not
simultaneously or at a domestic location, which helps minimize the risk of localized
environmental impacts. In general, as being operated not-concentratedly, individually and
primarily in domestic water areas, the impact of the ballast water on the environment is assessed
as medium, long-term and can be controlled through appropriate management measures in
Chapter 5.

4.5.1.4. Solid Waste Generation

377. Sources of solid waste generated during the operation of the Port include: (i) domestic
solid waste of workers at the Port; (ii) solid waste from boats; (iii) byproducts from fish sorting;
and (iv) hazardous waste. Details are as follows:

a) Domestic Waste

378. The main sources of domestic solid waste at the port come from the activities of 27 port
staff and traders operating at the port with waste generation per capita ranges from 0.1 – 0.3
kg/day (as people do not reside at the port). The average amount of solid waste generated at the
ports is approximately 2.7-8.1 kg/day (see Table 75).
379. Domestic solid waste includes (i) Organic fraction: food scraps, leftover vegetables, and
fruits. This type of waste decomposes quickly, producing foul odors, harboring pathogenic
microorganisms, and potentially affecting human health; and (ii) Inorganic fraction: packaging
materials, ceramics, glass, polymers, etc. This type of waste decomposes slowly, and if not
properly collected and treated, it can negatively impact the urban aesthetic and contribute to
environmental pollution at the fishing port. If not properly collected and treated, this waste
could pollute the soil, water, air, and landscape and impact workers in the port.
380. The port will be equipped with waste bins, and waste segregation will be carried out
within it. Furthermore, waste will be gathered and transferred to a designated treatment plant.
Therefore, the impact is small, localized, yet long-term, and can be mitigated by measures
described in Chapter 5.

b) Solid Waste from Boats

381. Once the upgraded port becomes operational, about 100 boats will dock at the port,
leading to the generation of solid waste from these boats. Solid waste generated from fishing
vessels originates mainly from daily activities of crew members during offshore operations.
According to data and discussions with the Rach Goc Port Management Board, each vessel

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         145
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

typically carries 4–6 crew members and operates offshore for 10–15 days per trip before
returning to port. Based on national marine waste management guidelines, the average domestic
waste generation rate for fishing crews is 0.3 kg/person/day. Accordingly, each vessel produces
about 1.2–1.8 kg of domestic waste per day of operation, equivalent to 12–27 kg per trip. With
100 vessels docking daily, the total domestic waste generated at the port is estimated at 120–
180 kg/day (see Table 75). The waste composition mainly includes:
  − Plastic waste: The most common type, including plastic bottles, food wrappers, nylon bags,
     and expanded polystyrene materials (foam buoys, fish boxes, and containers). These are
     lightweight, difficult to degrade, and can easily enter aquatic environments.
  − Fishing gear waste (“ghost gear”): Discarded or damaged fishing nets, lines, ropes, and
     traps made from synthetic fibers. These materials can drift or sink, entangling marine
     organisms and degrading marine habitats.
  − Domestic waste: leftover food, spoiled bait, vegetables, packaging materials, and personal-
     use items.
  − Cargo residues: Includes small quantities of spilled or spoiled fish, scales, blood, and other
     organic materials during unloading and sorting operations. These can decompose rapidly,
     creating odor and attracting pests.
  − Metal and glass waste: Empty cans, bottle caps, glass fragments, and metal scrap from
     maintenance or onboard use.
382. If not collected and treated according to regulations, boat-generated waste could
negatively impact water quality, the ecosystem of not only the Rach Goc River, Mot Canal,
and the landscape of the fishing port but also the sea, particularly when crews dispose of waste
directly into the sea during offshore fishing trips. However, the magnitude of this impact is
assessed as moderate, depending on vessel compliance with waste collection regulations. To
minimize risks, all boat owners are required to collect and store waste onboard, and offload it
at designated collection points upon return to port, where it will be transferred to licensed
treatment service providers under the supervision of the Port Management Board. The port also
enforces awareness campaigns, signage, and regular inspections to prevent illegal disposal at
sea.

c) Byproducts from Fish Sorting

383. When fishing vessels dock at the Rach Goc Port, fishermen mainly conduct sorting,
cleaning, and temporary preservation of fish before shipment to processing facilities outside the
port. These activities generate a small amount of byproducts such as damaged fish, scales, fins,
and organic residues. Because no fish-processing or filleting operations take place within the
port area, the quantity of byproducts is relatively low. According to the project FS, all vessels
bring in about 18,000 tons of fish per year and the byproduct rate from fish sorting is 1%14, the
total amount of byproducts generated daily by 100 vessels is estimated to be around 700 kg per
day (see Table 75).
384. If not promptly collected, these residues can decompose and cause odor, attract flies, and
locally degrade water quality in the unloading and sorting areas. However, the Port
Management Board implements daily cleaning of sorting sites, provides collection bins and
covered sorting tables, and assigns sanitation staff to remove organic waste immediately after
each unloading period. Fishermen are instructed to separate residues from clean fish to reuse
for fishmeal or fish compost, and avoid discharging waste into drainage channels. With these



14
  According to studies on raw material using efficiency in the seafood processing industry, as well as research
on by-product management, findings are often published in specialized scientific journals on fisheries in Viet
Nam
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                          Page           146
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

management measures, the environmental impact of fish-sorting byproducts is considered low,
localized, and fully manageable during port operation.

d) Hazardous waste

385. Hazardous waste at the Rach Goc Fishing Port is mainly generated from the operation of
fishing boats, and port facilities. Key types of hazardous waste include used oil, lubricants, oily
rags, fluorescent lamps, and batteries. These wastes contain hydrocarbons and heavy metals
that can pose risks of soil and water contamination if not properly collected and stored.
386. According to the Port Management Board’s operational data and reference from similar
fishing ports in the Mekong Delta, each boat produces an average of 1.0–1.5 liters of used oil
per trip of 15 days, and other hazardous residues (rags, filters, paint containers) equivalent to
0.1–0.2 kg/trip. With approximately 100 vessels docking per day, the estimated generation rate
of hazardous waste is about 70 kg/day (Table 75).
387. The magnitude of the potential impact is moderate, as these wastes are toxic, persistent,
and may cause localized contamination if spilled. However, the extent is to the port area and
the sea, particularly when crews dispose of hazardous waste directly into the sea during offshore
fishing trips and is fully manageable with proper collection and storage systems.
