5.1.3.2. Workers’ Codes of Conduct

457. The Workers’ Code of Conduct shall address, but not be limited to, the following
measures:
  − Compliance with laws and regulations

    • All workers must comply with the laws and regulations of the Socialist Republic of Viet
      Nam.
    • The list of workers must be registered with the local authorities in accordance with current
      regulations.
    • Children under 18 years old are not permitted to work under the Project.
  − Civilized behavior and respect for the community

    • Behave properly when interacting with the local community, respecting local customs,
      practices, and traditions.
    • Do not cause disturbance, disorder, or engage in fighting (physical or verbal) within or
      near the community.
    • Workers residing in temporary camps, when visiting local communities, must conduct
      themselves in a manner consistent with the Code of Conduct.
  − Prohibited behaviors and restricted items

    • Prohibit the use or possession of illegal substances, weapons, and firearms (except for
      authorized security personnel).
    • Prohibit pornography, gambling, and alcohol consumption during working hours.
    • Prohibit hunting, fishing with explosives/chemicals, trading wildlife, or keeping animals
      (particularly birds).
    • Prohibit cutting trees, collecting firewood, or harvesting plants beyond approved areas.
    • Prohibit disturbing architectural/historical sites, burning waste, or clearing vegetation
      without authorization.
    • There shall be no acts of violence or threats of violence in any form.
    • Respect the rights and dignity of all individuals, regardless of gender.
    • Do not engage in or support any form of sexual harassment, including verbal abuse,
      actions, or threats.
    • Do not coerce or create pressure for others to engage in non-consensual sexual acts.
  − Hygiene, safety, and environmental protection:

    • Maintain personal hygiene and living areas to appropriate standards.
    • Smoking is only permitted in designated areas.
    • Do not   wash clothes, clean vehicles, or maintain equipment in rivers/streams or
      unauthorized locations.
    • Do not dispose of garbage or construction waste indiscriminately; prevent the spread of
      pollutants such as oil or grease.
    • Toilets must be used only in designated places.
    • Do not work without personal protective equipment such as boots and helmets.
  − Disciplinary measures:

    • Any violation of the Code shall be subject to disciplinary action, ranging from a warning
      to termination of the labor contract, depending on the severity of the violation.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          190
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

      • Any construction worker, office staff, contractor’s employee, or any other person
       involved in the project found violating prohibitions shall be subject to disciplinary
       measures, which may range from a simple reprimand to dismissal, depending on the
       severity of the violation.

5.1.3.3. Labor Management Procedures

458. A Labor Management Procedure (LMP) was developed as a stand-alone document for
addressing the impacts on and of labor and working conditions of the project. The LMP
identifies the main labor requirements and risks associated with the project and helps the PMU
and Contractor to determine the resources necessary to address project labor issues. The LMP
will enable different project-related parties, for example, staff of the PMU, contractors and sub-
contractors, and project workers, to have a clear understanding of what is required on a specific
labor issue. The LMP is a living document, which is initiated early in project preparation, and
is reviewed and updated throughout the development and implementation of the project. The
LMP has 12 chapters. Chapter 1 served as an Introduction. An overview of labor use in the
project is presented in Chapter 2. Key potential labor risks are listed in Chapter 3. The
Legislative Framework governing labor employment in Viet Nam is discussed in Chapters 4
and 5. Implementation Arrangements, Age Requirement, Policies and Procedures, and Timing
of labor requirements follow in the subsequent chapters. Grievance Redress Mechanism and
Contractor Management are presented in the last two chapters 10 and 11, respectively, while
Chapter 12 relates to primary supply workers. Details are presented in the Project’s LMP.

5.1.3.4. Site-specific mitigation measures during construction phase of Component 1:
         Upgrading Rach Goc Fishing Port

459. The specific impacts and risks associated with the port upgrading are identified in Chapter
4 as follows:
   (i) Impacts on existing operations of the port;
   (ii) Impacts on waterway traffic along the Rach Goc River within the project area;
   (iii) Deterioration of water quality and effects on aquatic life due to dredging activities;
   (iv) Environmental pollution risks at the dredged material storage site.
   (v) Potential structural damage to nearby houses from pile-driving activities; and
   (vi) Impacts on sensitive receptors located in proximity to the construction area.
460. Mitigation measures for the impacts and risks are showed in Table 83.
 Table 83: Mitigation Measures for Specific Impacts and Risks of the Rach Goc Fishing Port
                                        Upgrading
  1     Mitigation measures for impacts on existing operations of the port
        − Avoidance
         ▪ Coordinate with waterway police, port management board, fisheries division, and local
           communities to plan construction and maintain safe port operations..
         ▪ Provide advance notice (at least 15 days), and post construction timelines and grievance
           mechanisms; broadcast weather updates via loudspeakers.
         ▪ Carry out phased construction and dredging of the port, prioritizing dredging on both sides
           of the expanded wharf area first and dredging in front of the existing wharf last, to maintain
           a navigable water surface for vessel movement.
         ▪ Prioritize dredging and construction activities during the dry season to avoid peak rainfall
           and flood periods that could exacerbate navigation risks.



Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    Page          191
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

        ▪ Avoiding dredging and construction during hours with peak vessel traffic (from 7:00–8:00
          a.m. and 2:00–3:00 p.m) to reduce waterway traffic conflicts and collision risks
      − Minimization
        ▪ Register   transportation routes, equipment, and construction timelines with relevant
          authorities to provide a basis for inspection and monitoring.
        ▪ Implement the approved Waterway Traffic Control Plan (see Appendix 6), including
          installing buoys, signal lights on barges, and warning signs along riverbanks to guide safe
          navigation.
        ▪ Proper placement of materials and equipment to avoid obstructing internal port roads and
          logistics facilities.
        ▪ Control access to construction zones, especially areas adjacent to berthing and cargo
          handling operations. Prohibiting non-compliant vessels or organizations from entering
          construction zones
      − Mitigation
        ▪ Establish on-site emergency response teams and develop the Contractor’s Emergency
          Preparedness Plan to address accidents and extreme weather events.
        ▪ Prepare and implement a detailed Dredging and Material Management Plan to regulate
          operations and minimize disruption to port activities.
        ▪ Install and maintain construction lights, warning signals, and traffic guidance boards at all
          active sites ensure visibility and safety..
      − Restoration & Remediation
        ▪ Clean and reinste completed areas before moving to the next phase
        ▪ Reinstating internal roads and yards affected during construction.
        ▪ Clear construction sites and relocate equipment to safe areas in the event of adverse weather
          conditions.
        ▪ Immediately remove temporary fences and warning signs after construction completion to
          fully restore navigable waterways.
  2   Mitigation measures for impacts on waterway traffic
      − Avoidance
        ▪ Consult and prepare safety and emergency response plans before construction:
             ➢ Develop and obtain approval for a   Waterway Traffic Management Plan (see Appendix
               6) and submit it to the competent authorities
             ➢ Coordinate in advance with local healthcare facilities to ensure emergency response
               readiness
             ➢ Establish an early warning system: Connect with the meteorological station and local
               authorities to update weather conditions and proactively adjust construction during
               storms, squalls, or rough seas.
             ➢ Assign clear responsibilities and conduct regular training for workers on emergency
               response procedures for waterway traffic incidents.
        ▪ Publicly announce construction information and safety warnings through mass media.
        ▪ Avoid construction outside peak waterway traffic hours (7:00–8:00 AM and 2:00–3:00 PM).
        ▪ Suspend construction during adverse weather e.g. thunderstorms, squalls, fog, or rough seas
          to ensure safety.
      − Minimization
        ▪ Ensure compliance with regulations on construction vessels and inland waterway traffic:
             ➢ Vessels must be registered, have license plates, and valid operation permits in
               accordance with regulations.
             ➢ Operators must hold the legally required navigation certificates.
        ▪ Install a warning system and delimit the construction area:


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  Page          192
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

             ➢ Provide    buoys, signal lights, and warning signs at construction, dredging, and
               navigation channel areas.
             ➢ Ensure vessels and construction equipment operate strictly within designated
               boundaries
             ➢ Do not place equipment or materials outside the project boundary or in areas at risk of
               obstructing waterway traffic.
             ➢ Apply a phased construction approach to minimize river surface occupation.
        ▪ Equip safety and communication devices:
             ➢ Fully equip all construction vessels with safety and communication devices (signal
               lights, lifeboats, lifebuoys, and fire-fighting equipment, VHS radios) and inspect
               regularly
        ▪ Compliance with construction and safety plans:
             ➢ Regularly check communication devices to ensure uninterrupted operation.
             ➢ Assign traffic sentries throughout the construction process.
             ➢ Relocate vessels and machinery to safe positions during adverse weather conditions or
               storms.
      − Restoration
        ▪ Clean up and reinstate the site: After each construction phase, dismantle equipment and clear
          the channel.
        ▪ Update the signaling system: Reinstall buoys and warning signs to ensure safe waterway
          traffic after construction.
  3   Mitigation measures for deterioration of water quality and effects on aquatic life due to
      dredging activities
      − Planning and approval
        ▪ The Contractor shall prepare a detailed Contractor’s Dredging and Material Management
          Plan (C-DMMP), based on the project’s detailed design and DMMP (Appendix 2). The C-
          DMMP must include dredged volumes; physical, biological, and chemical characteristics of
          dredged material; dredging methods and schedules; transportation, disposal, and pollution
          control processes; and risk mitigation measures at disposal sites. This plan must be approved
          by the Project Owner, updated as necessary, and regularly supervised by the CSC throughout
          implementation.
        ▪ Prepare construction schedules for each location, taking into account water levels, currents,
          weather conditions, community recommendations, and schedules of related packages.
      − Advoidance
        ▪ Strictly follow the approved dredging design, including depth, volume, and side slopes, to
          avoid creating deep pits that alter water flow and oxygen dynamics, harmful to aquatic
          organisms.
        ▪ Avoid construction during floods, high tides, or adverse weather, especially when vessels
          need to dock for storm shelter.
      − Mitigation measures
        ▪ Clearly demarcate dredging corridors when moving equipment to different river sections.
        ▪ Limit dredging during high tides to reduce the spread of sediments and pollutants.
        ▪ Do not use suction dredging methods that stir up the riverbed, due to associated
          environmental and social risks.
        ▪ Do not overload dredged materials on barges or transport vessels to reduce spillage risk into
          water bodies.
  4   Mitigation measures for environmental pollution risks at the dredged material storage site
      − Planning and approval




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  Page          193
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

        ▪ The Contractor shall prepare detailed Contractor’s Dredging and Material Management Plan
          (C-DMMP), based on the project’s detailed design and DMMP (Appendix 2). This plan
          must be approved by the Project Owner, updated as necessary, and regularly supervised by
          the CSC throughout implementation.
        ▪ Prepare construction schedules for each location, taking into account water levels, currents,
          weather conditions, community recommendations, and schedules of related packages; and
          avoid adverse conditions (floods, high tides, storms).
      − Dredged material disposal site management
        ▪ Install full mitigation measures such as embankments, outlets, drainage channels, warning
          signs, and containment dikes before transporting sediments to disposal sites.
        ▪ The disposal area is designed with three compartments surrounded by embankments of +3m,
          including drainage channels to collect water and discharge it into the Rach Goc River after
          settling. Retaining walls, interception ditches, or settling ponds must be constructed in
          advance to prevent direct runoff.




        ▪ Comply with monitoring, transportation, storage, and disposal procedures for dredged
          materials as specified in the C-DMMP and construction methods.
        ▪ If pipelines are used for slurry transport, warning signs must be installed along the pipeline.
        ▪ Conduct regular monitoring at disposal sites. Any leakage or overflow incidents must be
          promptly addressed before continuing storage.
        ▪ Maintain an operation logbook for the disposal site, recording volumes, time, and condition
          of dredged materials.
        ▪ If the dredged material is contaiminated with acid sulfate soil (pH < 3.5), lime powder will
          be used to to raise the pH to above 5: (i) pH is below 4.5: apply 1 kg of lime powder per 5
          m²; (ii) pH ranges from 4.6 to 5.5: apply 1 kg of lime per 10 m²; and (iii) pH ranges from
          5.5 to 6.5: apply 1 kg of lime per 20 m².
        ▪ All rainwater runoff must be collected and treated to remove sediments before being
          discharged into the Mot canal or Rach Goc river. To maintain the structural stability of the
          disposal site, mortar rubble masonry pavement and grouted rubble toe protection will be
          implemented to prevent erosion.
        ▪ Use lime powder to limit malodor dispersion, if necessary.
      − Disposal site incident control
        ▪ Disposal site design must take into account risks such as erosion, flooding, subsidence, and
          natural disasters.
        ▪ Establish an overall incident control plan for the project, covering dredging, transportation,
          and disposal incidents.
        ▪ Form an emergency response team of at least three members.
        ▪ Provide a hotline / accessible communication channel for communities to report incidents
          quickly.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   Page           194
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

 5    Mitigation measures for potential structural damage to nearby houses from pile-driving
      activities
      − Avoidance
        ▪ Conduct detailed geological surveys and Standard Penetration Test (SPT) to understand soil
          conditions and proximity to the house.
        ▪ Conduct a detailed structural survey of nearby houses (including foundations and wall
          integrity) before work begins.
        ▪ Use vibratory or hydraulic hammers instead of diesel hammers to reduce vibration.
        ▪ Use vibratory or hydraulic hammers instead of diesel hammers to reduce vibration.
        ▪ Select a suitable hammer size with appropriate load capacity based on site-specific
          geological conditions; and avoid large hammers near houses.
        ▪ Phase construction activities and schedule pile driving during daylight or off-peak hours to
          minimize impact.
        ▪ Ensure the detailed design and contractor’s construction method account for risks to the
          house.
        ▪ Secure construction insurance prior to commencement to cover potential damages to the
          house.
        ▪ Limit the speed and load of construction vehicles when operating near the house.
      − Minimization (Mitigation)
        ▪ Drive piles in sequentially in small clusters and avoid simultaneous driving of adjacent piles.
        ▪ Erect safety fences, barriers, and warning signs around the construction area
        ▪ Monitor vibration levels continuously and stop work immediately if thresholds are
          exceeded.
        ▪ Use modern equipment to reduce vibration and noise and follow approved construction
          methods; avoid nighttime construction near the house.
        ▪ Conduct regular equipment inspections and provide safety training for workers on safety
          and vibration control. Apply controlled piling techniques: short operation periods per pile
          and adjust hammer frequency to reduce vibration amplitude.
      − Remediation
        ▪ Reinforce vulnerable structures using bracing, retaining walls, or damping layers.
      ▪   Establish an emergency response protocol: halt piling immediately if cracks or settlement
          signs appear, inspect, and reinforce before resuming work.
      ▪   Formalize a post-construction remediation process for repairing cosmetic cracks promptly
          and documenting completion with homeowner agreement.
      − Offset
        ▪ Before starting construction, particularly in the port upgrading area, the contractor must
          survey the current conditions of the surrounding houses (including taking photos of the
          houses) and obtain the homeowners’ signatures for confirmation.
        ▪ Provide a hotline or accessible communication channel for households to report issues
          during construction.
        ▪ Notify affected households at least two weeks in advance and obtain their consent.

  6   Mitigation measures for impacts on sensitive receptors: Affected receptors associated with
      the port upgradation include a coffee shop, a grocery store
      − Avoidance
        ▪ The contractor must notify the store owner at least 2 weeks before construction, allowing
          them time to adjust production and operations accordingly.
        ▪ Schedule construction activities during the daytime only (6:00 am – 6:00 pm), avoiding
          nighttime work unless officially approved by the PMU and CSC.

  o   impacts on sensitive receptors.

        ▪ Restore the surrounding areas, including bridge embankments, access roads, and canal

5.1.3.6. Site-specific mitigation measures during construction phase of Component 2.2:
         Canal dredging

463. The specific impacts and risks associated with the canal dredging are identified in Chapter
4 as follows:
    (i). Impacts on aquaculture
    (ii). Deterioration of water quality and effects on aquatic life due to dredging activities

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    Page          201

   (iii). Impacts and risks due to the temporary storage of the dredged materials
   (iv). Impacts on existing infrastructure
   (v). Impacts on the waterway
464. Mitigation measures for the specific impacts and risks are proposed in Table 85.
     Table 85: Mitigation measures for the specific impacts and risks of the canal dredging
 1      Mitigation measures for impacts on aquaculture
        − Avoidance:
          ▪ Avoid dredging activities during shrimp pond water intake periods (2–5 days per month at
            high tide);
          ▪ Avoid dredging simultaneously on adjacent canal sections to reduce cumulative turbidity
            impacts.
          ▪ Exclude dredging near aquaculture water intakes.
          ▪ Develop contingency arrangements for alternative water supply during unavoidable intake
            periods (e.g., temporary storage or coordination with farmers).
        − Minimization:
          ▪ Use duckbill dredgers to limit sediment dispersion.
          ▪ Limit dredging depth and bucket lifting speed to minimize sediment resuspension.
          ▪ Implement temporary barriers near sensitive receptors (e.g., aquaculture water intake
            points) where technically feasible;
          ▪ Maintain dredging equipment regularly to prevent oil or fuel leaks.
        − Mitigation:
          ▪ Schedule dredging in short sections (100 m at a time) and complete each section within 2–
            3 days to minimize localized impacts.
          ▪ Arrange water quality sampling at representative monitoring stations upstream and
            downstream of dredging sites, following the parameters in QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT;
          ▪ Implement emergency spill response procedures for accidental fuel or lubricant leakage
            from dredging equipment;
          ▪ Provide training for operators on environmentally responsible dredging and fuel handling
            practices.
          ▪ Include emergency water treatment options for aquaculture ponds if intake occurs during
            high turbidity periods (e.g., sedimentation tanks or filtration).
        − Compensation:
          ▪ Implement a communication plan to inform communities and shrimp farmers in advance
            about dredging schedules and expected turbidity fluctuations.
 2      Mitigation measures for deterioration of water quality and impacts on aquatic life due to
        dredging activities
        − Avoidance
          ▪ The Contractor shall prepare a detailed Contractor’s Dredging and Material Management
            Plan (C-DMMP), based on the project’s detailed design and DMMP (Appendix 2). The C-
            DMMP must include dredged volumes; physical, biological, and chemical characteristics
            of dredged materials; dredging methods and schedules; transportation, disposal, and
            pollution control processes; and risk mitigation measures at disposal sites. This plan must
            be approved by the Project Owner, updated as necessary, and regularly supervised by the
            CSC throughout implementation.
          ▪ Prepare construction schedules for each location, taking into account water levels, currents,
            weather conditions, community recommendations, and schedules of related packages.
          ▪ Avoid dredging during the peak breeding seasons of aquatic organisms.
        − Minimization


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    Page          202
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

         ▪ Immediately load dredged materials onto barges after lifting and transport to the designated
            storage areas.
         ▪ Do not overload barges or transport vessels, ensuring safety and avoiding spillage.
         ▪ Do not store oil/lubricants on barges or dredging equipment; oil-contaminated materials
            must be collected and handled by licensed entities.
         ▪ Dredging operators must be trained and certified, under supervision of CSC.
         ▪ Emergency Preparedness Plan: The emergency response plan must address incident
           scenarios such as vessel collisions, engine failures, capsizing, or oil spills, with objectives
           that include reducing potential impacts on water quality and aquatic life, alongside ensuring
           safety and operational continuity. Key measures should include:
            ➢ Procedures for immediate containment and cleanup of oil or material spills to prevent
                contamination.
            ➢ Coordination with relevant authorities for rapid incident response to minimize
                environmental and safety risks.
            ➢ Communication protocols between vessels, the contractor, and project owner to ensure
                timely action.
       − Restoration
         ▪ Collect and clean up spilled materials and waste after each construction stage, avoiding
           long-term impacts on water quality.
       Restore water quality by adjusting construction schedules if signs of prolonged degradation
       appear.
 3     Mitigation measures for impacts and risks due to the temporary storage of the dredged
       materials
       − Avoidance
         ▪ Only dispose of dredged material at designated storage areas approved by the PMU and

         ▪ Avoid disposal during rest hours, limit construction after 18:00.
       − Minimization
         ▪ Prepare and implement a Dredging and Excavation Management Plan before

         ▪ Implement mortar rubble masonry pavement and grouted rubble toe protection to stabilize

       − Mitigation
         ▪ Before disposal, strip a 30 cm layer of topsoil and store it separately for final rehabilitation
           of the site.
         ▪ Collect and treat all rainwater runoff to remove sediments before discharging into receiving
           water bodies.
         ▪ If the material is contaiminated with acid sulfate soil (pH < 3.5), lime powder will be used
           to to raise the pH to above 5: (i) pH is below 4.5: apply 1 kg of lime powder per 5 m²; (ii)
           pH ranges from 4.6 to 5.5: apply 1 kg of lime per 10 m²; and (iii) pH ranges from 5.5 to
           6.5: apply 1 kg of lime per 20 m².
         ▪ Use lime powder on-site if odor becomes a nuisance.
         ▪ Construct drainage ditches around the disposal area to manage surface runoff, and ensure
           all rainwater is collected and treated to remove sediments before discharge into receiving
           water bodies
         ▪ Appropriate slope protection and surface stabilization measures shall be applied to
           maintain the structural stability of the disposal site.
 4     Mitigation measures for Damage risk on existing infrastructure
       − Avoidance:
         ▪ Avoid dredging or operating machinery within the designated safety buffer zone around
           bridge piers and abutments;
         ▪ Avoid allowing dredged material transport routes to pass over weak or small-capacity
           bridges.
       − Mitigation:
         ▪ Conduct pre-construction structural inspections and photographic documentation of all
           existing bridges and hydraulic structures to establish baseline conditions;
         ▪ Assign trained operators to ensure precise dredging operations and maintain safe distances
           from bridge foundations;
         ▪ Implement speed and operation limits for barges and dredging vessels when passing under
           or near bridges;
         ▪ Coordinate dredging schedules with local authorities to minimize navigation congestion
           near critical infrastructure points.
