         ▪ In case of minor damage (e.g., to surface coating, minor cracks), conduct immediate repair
           using approved materials and techniques under the supervision of qualified engineers;


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     Page           204
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

         ▪ If post-construction inspection identifies any structural damage caused by construction
           activities, implement repair or strengthening works according to engineering standards and
           under the approval of local authorities.
         ▪ Restore the surrounding areas, including bridge embankments, access roads, and canal
           edges, to their original or improved condition.
 5     Mitigation measures for Impacts on the waterway
       − Avoidance
         ▪ Consultation and preparation of safety and emergency response plans before construction:
              ➢ Develop a    Waterway Traffic Management Plan and submit it to the competent
                authorities for approval.
              ➢ Coordinate in advance with local healthcare facilities to ensure readiness to respond in
                case of incidents.
              ➢ Connect with the meteorological station and local authorities to update weather
                conditions and proactively adjust construction during storms, squalls, or rough seas.
              ➢ Fully equip rescue devices: lifeboats, lifebuoys, signal lights, communication, fire-
                fighting equipment and first-aid equipment on all construction vessels.
              ➢ Assign clear responsibilities and conduct regular training for workers on emergency
                response procedures for waterway traffic incidents.
         ▪ Publicly announce construction information and safety warnings through mass media.
         ▪ Suspend construction during thunderstorms, squalls, fog, or rough seas to ensure safety.
       − Minimization
         ▪ Strict compliance with regulations on dredging boats and inland waterway traffic:
              ➢ Boats must be registered, have license plates, and valid operation permits in
                accordance with regulations.
              ➢ Operators must hold the legally required navigation certificates.
         ▪ Install a warning system and delimit the construction area:
              ➢ Provide buoys, signal lights, and warning signs at dredging areas.
              ➢ Apply a phased dredging approach to reduce the canal surface area occupied at each
                stage.
         ▪ Relocate dredging plants to safe positions during adverse weather conditions or storms.
       − Restoration
         ▪ Clean up and reinstate the site: After each dredging, dismantle equipment and clear the
           canal.
         ▪ Reinstall warning signs to ensure safe waterway traffic after dredging.


5.1.3.7. Site-specific mitigation measures during construction phase of Component 2.3:
         Installation of 4 water monitoring stations

465. Specific impacts of the installation of water level monitoring stations in waterways
include localized obstructions to navigation channels during installation. Mitigation measures
for the specific impacts are as follows:
− Minimization

   ▪ Schedule construction activities during low-flow or calm weather conditions where
     feasible, to reduce navigation disturbance and risks to workers.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Install clear warning buoys, lights, and signboards around the construction zone to alert
     nearby waterway users during pile-driving and construction works.
   ▪ Coordinate with local waterway management authorities to ensure vessel navigation routes
     are maintained safely during construction.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   Page          205
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

−   Remediation (if any)
     ▪ In case of accidental obstruction to navigation (e.g., material drop, partial structure
       collapse), immediately remove obstructions and restore safe passage.

5.1.4. Measures for the Operation Phase
5.1.4.1. Mitigation Measures during Operation of Rach Goc Fishing Port (component 1)

a) Mitigation Measures for Dust and Exhausted Gases

− Avoidance
   ▪ Designate traffic flow and parking areas in advance to reduce vehicle idling and
     congestion.
   ▪ Prohibit entry of vehicles and boats that do not meet emission standards.
− Minimization

   ▪ Require boats docking at the port and vehicles transporting fish to turn off their engines
     while waiting for loading/unloading.
   ▪ Limit unnecessary movement of trucks and cargo-handling equipment.
   ▪ Enforce the speed limit of 10 km/h inside the port to minimize dust resuspension.
− Mitigation

   ▪ All vehicles, loading/unloading machinery, and equipment must have Registry Department
     certification before operation.
   ▪ Ensure periodic inspection and maintenance of trucks, forklifts, and cranes to keep exhaust
     emissions within permissible standards.
   ▪ Provide designated parking and cargo handling zones with paved surfaces to limit dust.
− Remediation (if any)

   ▪ In case of excessive dust or emission levels, carry out immediate cleaning of port surfaces
     and surrounding areas.

b) Mitigation Measures for Noise

− Technical measures for equipment and vehicles:
   ▪ All vehicles operating within the fishing port must undergo registration as required by
     regulations; conduct periodic inspections and maintenance of equipment, vehicles, and
     machinery to ensure that their operation does not generate noise levels exceeding the
     permissible standards.
   ▪ Encourage ship owners and transport units to equip vessels and trucks with silencers,
     especially for large-capacity vehicles frequently operating at the port.
   ▪ Require that engines be switched off when vehicles are parked or vessels are moored at the
     port.
   ▪ Prioritize the use of low-noise equipment, install noise barriers, or situate noisy facilities
     far from sensitive areas.
− Operational management measures:

   ▪ Restrict operations during rest hours (midday and night).
   ▪ Require engines to be turned off when vessels dock and during cargo handling, waiting
     times.
   ▪ Install traffic signs in the port to limit speed to 10 km/h and provide noise-reduction
     operational guidance.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         206
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

−    Training and awareness raising:
      ▪ Organize regular training sessions for fishermen, drivers, and port staff on noise impacts
        and mitigation measures.

c) Mitigation Measures for Wastewater Generation

− Domestic wastewater
   ▪ Increase the awareness of people working at the port on water saving.
   ▪ Collect and treat domestic wastewater at the port’ wastewater treatment plant.
− Wastewater from fish sorting and preliminary processing

   ▪ Check the wastewater treatment plant and fix any issues before the upgradation of the port
     is completed to ensure proper treatment of all wastewater.
   ▪ Increase the awareness of people working at the port on water saving.
   ▪ Collect and treat the wastewater at the port’ wastewater treatment plant.
− Rainwater runoff

   ▪ Clean the port ground before raining
   ▪ Regularly monitor the performance of the rainwater
− Ballast water

   ▪ The ballast water of these vessels must be discharged at a distance far from shore, in areas
     where the sea has good self-cleaning capacity, great depth, and small impact on the
     ecosystem, under the current regulations of Viet Nam (Circular No. 41/2017/TT-BGTVT
     dated November 14, 2017, issued by the Ministry of Transport (now Ministry of
     Construction), regulating the management, collection, and treatment of waste from ships
     in seaport waters).
   ▪ The port strictly prohibits the discharge of waste oil and lubricants from ship machinery
     directly into the environment (while the ship is docked at the port).
   ▪ Waste oil generated by fishing vessels must be collected by ship owners into separate
     barrels for sale or transferred to oil recycling units for proper treatment.
   ▪ Engine room wastewater containing high levels of oil and grease must be manually
     collected and transported for treatment in accordance with regulations.

d) Mitigation Measures for Solid Waste Generation

−    Avoidance
      ▪ On vessels
         ➢ Establish a system for collecting and handling solid waste generated from fishing
            vessels during operation and anchorage, in accordance with national regulations.
         ➢ Food-related waste transported to the port must be managed under local regulations to
            protect human and animal health15.
         ➢ Strictly prohibit the discharge of solid waste from vessels while anchored at the port,
            in compliance with MARPOL and national regulations.
      ▪ On land
         ➢ The Management Boards of the fishing ports must strictly manage and minimize waste
            generated during sorting and receiving of fish, improving the environment at sorting
            houses and across the port.

15
  Countries have specific regulatory requirements for the handling of food waste originating from international
voyages. The goal of most of these regulations is to prevent the cross-border spread of infectious diseases.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                        Page            207
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

      ➢ Port operators must provide adequate facilities for wastewater and solid waste
         reception and management to meet the needs of the port and the vessels it serves.
      ➢ The provision of waste reception facilities must be coordinated with local authorities,
         in line with commitments under the MARPOL Convention by port states.
      ➢ Port operators must prepare environmental incident prevention, control, and response
         plans. Fuel suppliers must develop emergency response plans for oil spills to prevent
         large-scale dispersion that could cause significant harm to the natural environment and
         human health.
− Mitigation (Minimization)

  ▪ Contract with licensed units with full competence to collect, transport, and treat all daily
    domestic solid waste at the port, in compliance with the Law on Environmental Protection
    and relevant guiding documents.
  ▪ The Port Management Board must designate a solid waste collection area with at least one
    day’s storage capacity, ensuring compliance with technical requirements for waste storage
    facilities.
  ▪ Domestic solid waste at the fishing ports (including from staff at the operation area and
    from fishermen/traders/visitors in public areas) shall be collected into 3 covered bins of
    120 liters each, with mandatory segregation: food waste, recyclable/reusable waste, and
    other domestic waste.
  ▪ Sludge from wastewater treatment processes at the WWTP must be collected, transported,
    and treated by licensed contractors in compliance with regulations.
  ▪ Sludge from the drainage and rainwater systems at the port must be dredged, collected, and
    treated by licensed contractors every 6 months.
  ▪ Recyclable and reusable solid waste must be collected at designated places and handled
    once per week in accordance with regulations.
  ▪ Fishery by-products from the sorting process must be collected into 02 bins of 500 liters
    each. Traders must collect them daily and transfer them to processing units for animal feed
    production.

e) Mitigation Measures for Deterioration of the Quality of Water Resources and Aquatic
   Life

e.1). Mitigation Measures for Impacts on Water Quality
− Avoidance:

   ▪ Require all fishing vessels to install oil–sludge separation equipment prior to ballast water
     discharge to ensure no direct release of pollutants into the receiving waters;
   ▪ Design and establish ballast water reception and treatment facilities at the port, ensuring
     sufficient capacity for collection from all docking vessels;
   ▪ Include ballast water and oily waste management requirements in port operational
     regulations and environmental management plans, in line with the MARPOL Convention
     and Decree No. 58/2017/ND-CP;
   ▪ Prohibit ballast water discharge in sensitive or restricted water areas such as near
     mangroves, aquaculture zones, and water intakes;
   ▪ Ensure newly built or rehabilitated port infrastructure incorporates closed drainage systems
     to prevent direct runoff of contaminated water.
   ▪ Require fishing vessel owners and operators to comply with Ballast Water Management
     (BWM) Convention principles, especially for inter-provincial or high-capacity vessels;


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page         208
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Implement solid and hazardous waste management controls as outlined in 5.1.4.1d to
     prevent debris, sludge, and oil residues from entering the aquatic environment;
   ▪ Equip fish receiving and sorting areas with oil–grease traps and trash screens to retain
     suspended solids and organic matter before any runoff reaches Rach Goc River and Mot
     Canal;
− Mitigation:

   ▪ Before the upgraded port is put into operation, the Port Management Board must inspect
     the wastewater treatment plant and repair any damages (if any) to ensure that the plant
     operates properly when the upgraded port becomes operational.
   ▪ Operate the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) effectively, ensuring treated effluent
     meets QCVN 40:2023/BTNMT or equivalent standards before discharge into Rach Goc
     River and Mot Canal;
   ▪ Contract licensed environmental service providers for the collection, transportation, and
     treatment of ballast water and oily sludge in compliance with Circular No. 41/2017/TT-
     BGTVT;
   ▪ Implement training and awareness programs for fishermen, vessel operators, and port staff
     on ballast water management, pollution prevention, and emergency response procedures;
   ▪ Develop and apply monitoring protocols for oil film detection, water quality sampling, and
     record-keeping at discharge points;
   ▪ Enforce compliance monitoring through periodic environmental audits and inspections by
     port authorities and environmental agencies.
− Remediation:

   ▪ In case of accidental release or contamination, immediately contain, collect, and treat
     affected ballast water or oily residues before further discharge;
   ▪ Implement oil spill contingency and response plans, including the use of absorbent booms
     and skimmers to remove surface oil;
   ▪ Clean and restore affected drainage channels, sediment traps, and nearby water areas
     following any pollution event;
   ▪ Review the incident, identify root causes, and strengthen port management procedures and
     vessel maintenance requirements to prevent recurrence.
   ▪ Provide capacity building for port staff and vessel owners on post-incident reporting,
     remediation, and environmental restoration.
e.2). Measures for Impacts on Aquatic Life
During the operational process, if port management authorities and fishermen implement
effective environmental protection measures regarding waste, wastewater, and compliance with
fishing regulations, both coastal and offshore ecosystems will be safeguarded. The impact of
port activities on the environment will be minimized. To achieve this, the following actions are
necessary:
− Avoidance:

   ▪ Conduct awareness campaigns and guide fishermen to ensure they follow environmental
     protection measures properly.
   ▪ Regularly organize waste collection and environmental sanitation activities in port areas.
− Mitigation:

   ▪ Limit fishing port operations to the designated water area in front of the wharf, avoiding
     widespread impacts on the ecosystems of Rach Goc river and Mot canal.
   ▪ Prohibit direct wastewater discharge into Rach Goc river and Mot canal; all wastewater
     must be treated to meet standards before release into Rach Goc river and Mot canal.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page          209
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Control chemical use in preservation and cleaning activities to avoid impacts on aquatic
     organisms.
   ▪ Conduct awareness campaigns for fishermen and port staff on protecting water quality and
     aquatic ecosystems.
   ▪ Collect packaging and food leftovers from operations; prohibit indiscriminate littering into
     surrounding areas or rivers/seas.
   ▪ Raise awareness among residents on vessel maintenance to prevent oil leakage into
     rivers/seas.
   ▪ Comply with the fishery development planning and sustainable aquatic resource
     management of Ca Mau province.

f) Mitigation Measures for Traffic Impacts and Risks

f.1). Waterway Traffic Accidents
− Avoidance

   ▪ Provide formal training and issue professional certificates for all crew members and vessel
     operators, including rescue, emergency response, and safe navigation skills.
   ▪ Provide safety instructions for crew members on emergencies such as fire, explosion,
     sinking, or storms.
   ▪ Install waterway traffic signs and signals in areas with high vessel density or complex
     hydrological conditions.
   ▪ Establish coordination and traffic routing procedures at fishing ports and storm shelter
     anchorages to avoid congestion and collisions.
   ▪ Regularly maintain and inspect watercraft (fishing vessels, canoes, boats), especially
     engines, electrical systems, and safety equipment such as life buoys, life jackets, and
     firefighting tools.
   ▪ Equip vessels with modern communication systems such as VHF, radar, and GPS devices
     to monitor movement and avoid collisions at sea.
   ▪ When a waterway traffic accident occurs at the fishing ports, a prompt and accurate
     response is essential to ensure safety and minimize damage. The following steps should be
     taken to mitigate and address such accidents:
− Mitigation

   ▪ Determine the number of injured, missing, or drowning-risk persons. Inspect the accident
     vessel to assess its condition and damage.
   ▪ Establish a safety zone around the accident site to prevent additional hazards from passing
     vessels.
   ▪ Use rescue boats or canoes to bring injured persons safely ashore. Ensure sufficient life
     buoys, life jackets, and rescue equipment are available.
   ▪ Apply immediate measures such as artificial respiration, bleeding control, wound
     bandaging, and transfer to medical facilities if necessary.
   ▪ Notify relevant authorities immediately: contact rescue agencies, Port Authority, border
     guard forces, and other related bodies for timely assistance.
− Remediation

   ▪ Record the situation: provide detailed accident information to investigators, including time,
     location, vessel, initial cause, and number of people affected.
   ▪ Identify accident causes: authorities investigate whether accidents stemmed from technical
     failures, human error, or adverse weather.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          210
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Correct the incident: if the vessel is damaged (engine failure, hull damage), take temporary
     measures to tow it to a repair site.
   ▪ Evaluate the accident and improve procedures: review operational processes, safety
     systems, and crew training to prevent recurrence.
f.2). Mitigation Measures for Risk of Falling into the River and Drowning for Vessel
Operators/Fishermen on Board
− Avoidance

   ▪ Vessels must have high stability: ensure vessels are designed for stability, especially when
     sailing in rough seas or high waves. Anti-capsize systems must be regularly inspected and
     maintained.
   ▪ Design vessels with clear and accessible emergency exits.
   ▪ Install handrails and guardrails: provide sturdy handrails or guardrails in hazardous areas
     to help fishermen move safely and prevent slips.
   ▪ Ensure dry, non-slippery decks by using anti-slip coating or rough surfaces to reduce fall
     risks during rain or waves.
   ▪ Provide spacious, unobstructed working areas on vessels, docks, and storm shelters for
     safe, easy movement.
   ▪ Establish pre-departure safety checks, including lifesaving equipment, communications,
     weather conditions, and crew health.
   ▪ Require voyage and safety logs to monitor unusual situations and improve operations after
     each trip.
   ▪ Assign tasks clearly and supervise closely, especially during bad weather or offshore trips.
   ▪ Update and monitor weather conditions, particularly storm seasons, to prevent sailing in
     dangerous situations.
   ▪ Provide regular training for fishermen and vessel operators on safety skills, vessel stability,
     and basic rescue techniques. Raise awareness on drowning risks and personal protective
     equipment.
   ▪ Equip vessels with early-warning systems for instability, such as horns, warning lights, or
     electronic devices suited to the vessel type.
− Minimization

   ▪ Life jackets: all crew, vessel operators, and fishermen must wear life jackets at all times
     while working on board, especially near ports or in bad weather.
   ▪ Safety harnesses: provide harnesses for crew working in hazardous conditions with strong
     waves or winds.
   ▪ Safety shoes and gloves: provide anti-slip footwear and gloves for safe movement and
     reduced fall risk.
   ▪ Adequate lighting: ensure full lighting in work areas on vessels and around ports at night
     to reduce slip and collision risks.
   ▪ Emergency communications: radios or satellite phones for contacting rescue agencies in
     emergencies.
   ▪ First aid/rescue stations near ports: maintain nearby stations or teams at fishing ports and
     storm shelters for quick response.
   ▪ Emergency transport: provide ambulances, lifeboats, and necessary medical supplies for
     injuries or drowning cases.
   ▪ Personal locator beacons (PLBs): supply for crew working alone or in dangerous
     conditions, especially on offshore vessels.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         211
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Individual first-aid kits: equip each crew member, especially offshore, with basic supplies
     for small injuries.
   ▪ Encourage installation of man-overboard alarms on large fishing vessels or enclosed cabins
     if technically and financially feasible.
− Mitigation

Emergency measures to reduce harm when falling overboard/drowning occurs:
   ▪ Conduct regular rescue drills for fishermen and vessel operators, including rescue
     techniques, lifesaving equipment use, and basic first aid.
   ▪ Rescue victims: if someone falls overboard, use rescue boats/canoes or throw flotation
     devices for support.
   ▪ Establish rapid response procedures, including assigning rescuers, deploying flotation
     equipment, and contacting rescue forces.
− Remediation

   ▪ Record and report incidents promptly for investigation and procedural improvement.
   ▪ Hold debriefings with the crew after each incident to update safety protocols.
   ▪ Conduct periodic risk assessments and update safety management and protective
     equipment accordingly.
   ▪ Provide psychological support and health recovery for crew after serious accidents to
     restore working capacity and reduce long-term mental impacts.

f.3). Mitigation Measures for Vessel Docking Beyond the Port’s Design Capacity
− Avoidance

   ▪ The technical infrastructure and environmental treatment facilities of the fishing port shall
     be designed to accommodate the maximum number of vessels on peak days in accordance
     with the approved design.
   ▪ The breakwaters shall be designed in segments based on the vessel capacity, ensuring
     minimization of vessel congestion when berthing.
   ▪ Develop a vessel traffic management plan based on fishing seasons and catch volumes, in
     order to forecast peak periods and adjust port operations accordingly.
   ▪ Designate additional temporary mooring areas or buffer zones outside the port to regulate
     vessels exceeding the design capacity.
− Minimization

   ▪ The port management authority shall assign staff to guide vessels in and out during peak
     days, ensuring order and limiting delays or security issues.
   ▪ Establish a pre-registration system for vessels before docking, particularly during peak
     seasons, to control traffic flow and allocate berthing positions appropriately.
   ▪ Apply information technology (e.g., port management software) to monitor vessel arrivals
     in real time, supporting efficient coordination.
   ▪ Enhance communication and early warnings for fishermen through loudspeakers, SMS, or
     electronic boards at the port to prevent congestion.
   ▪ Organize vessel traffic by size, type of activity, and berthing time to optimize port space
     and usage.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Deploy a rapid response team at the port, including security staff, coordinators, and
     technical support teams to handle emerging situations.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          212
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Arrange for vessel rotation or temporary mooring outside the port, combined with
     announcements of estimated docking times to reduce immediate pressure.
− Remediation

   ▪ Review and periodically update the vessel traffic management plan based on actual
     operations and catch growth trends.
   ▪ Collect and analyze data after each peak period to adjust designs, operational procedures,
     and vessel reception capacity in the future.

g) Mitigation Measures for Occupational Risks

− Avoidance

   ▪ Develop a fishing port management regulation that enhances capacity on occupational
     health, safety, and community protection for the port authority, fishermen, service
     providers, and traders.
   ▪ The port’s operation and maintenance system must include functions to respond to
     potential impacts on occupational health and community safety caused by air pollution,
     noise, fire, explosion, traffic accidents, port security, structural collapse, landslides, or
     subsidence due to extreme weather.
   ▪ Design a reasonable port layout, with clear separation between mechanized equipment
     operation zones and pedestrian zones to avoid collisions.
   ▪ Establish a pre-shift safety inspection system, including checking equipment, work areas,
     and worker health conditions.
   ▪ Provide clearly marked emergency exits, adequate safety signage, and arrange first-aid
     stations at strategic points within the port.
   ▪ Conduct regular occupational safety training, first-aid, and emergency response drills for
     all workers and port staff.
− Minimization

   ▪ Minimization measures for accidents caused by mechanized equipment, such as cranes,
     forklifts, etc.)
      ➢ Ensure all workers operating mechanized equipment are properly trained and certified
         for safe operation.
      ➢ Conduct periodic inspection and maintenance of mechanized equipment.
      ➢ Use personal protective equipment (PPE): Operators must wear helmets, safety shoes,
         gloves, etc.
      ➢ Apply standard operating procedures (SOP): Workers must follow prescribed steps to
         avoid mistakes during operations.
      ➢ Install warning systems when mechanized equipment operates near pedestrian areas.
   ▪ Minimization measures for accidents caused due to unsafe working conditions:
      ➢ Ensure safe working conditions: Work areas must be designed, constructed, and
         maintained safely, with adequate lighting, non-slippery floors, and no hazardous
         obstructions.
      ➢ Conduct periodic risk assessments to promptly detect and address potential hazards.
      ➢ Provide safety information and guidance: All workers must receive clear instructions
         on safe working procedures and safety signage at work sites.
      ➢ Establish a rapid feedback system when unsafe conditions are detected.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          213
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Minimization (for occupational health and disease risks)
      ➢ Conduct regular health check-ups for all workers to detect early occupational diseases
         and ensure workers are fit for duty.
      ➢ Provide adequate PPE suited to the port environment, including dust masks, gloves,
         anti-slip boots, waterproof and chemical-resistant clothing to protect workers from
         hazards such as dust, humidity, chemicals, and pathogens, thereby preventing diseases
         like pneumonia, poisoning, or dermatitis.
      ➢ Health education: Organize training sessions on health protection, disease prevention,
         and proper nutrition for workers.
   ▪ Minimization (for environmental accident risks at the port)
      ➢ Maintain   a clean, well-ventilated work environment, free of dust and harmful
         substances.
      ➢ Provide fire and explosion prevention equipment: Ensure that areas with risks (such as
         fuel or chemical storage) are equipped with adequate firefighting systems and are
         inspected regularly.
      ➢ Disaster prediction and response: Establish measures such as drainage systems,
         equipment protection, and evacuation plans in case of storms, high waves, or other
         disasters.
      ➢ Establish and implement a hazardous chemical and material management procedure at
         the fishing port, including classification, storage, transportation, and safe use of
         common substances such as: Diesel, FO/DO fuel oils, and lubricants for engines and
         mechanized equipment; Industrial detergents and disinfectants such as chlorine and
         formalin for cleaning ship holds and processing areas; Refrigerants such as ammonia
         or glycol used in cold storage systems; Industrial solvents such as acetone and xylene
         from ship repair activities; Flammable/explosive chemicals such as LPG and gasoline
         stored at fuel depots.
      ➢ The procedure must ensure clear warning signs, safe isolation zones, adequate
         firefighting equipment, and regular staff training on chemical spill response and
         emergency handling.
− Remediation

   ▪ Conduct regular rescue drills, including rescue techniques, use of life-saving equipment,
     and on-site first aid.
   ▪ Protect victims: use rescue boats, flotation devices, and mobilize local forces.
   ▪ Establish a rapid response team at the port to handle occupational accidents.
   ▪ Provide an internal emergency communication system for quick incident notification.

h) Mitigation Measures for Environmental Risks and Incidents

h.1). Oil spills
Oil spill response in fishing vessel operations must comply with Decision No. 12/2021/QD-
TTg dated March 24, 2021 of the Prime Minister, supplemented with practical measures and
international guidelines.
− Avoidance

   ▪ Prohibit the discharge of oily wastewater or oil-contaminated materials into rivers or
     receiving waters.
   ▪ Train fishermen in safe vessel operation to prevent oil leaks from engines and fuel systems.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          214
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Conduct periodic inspection of fuel systems, engines, and oil tanks on fishing vessels to
     detect leakage risks.
   ▪ Prohibit the use of old vessels that fail to meet fuel safety and environmental standards.
− Minimization

   ▪ Develop, submit, and obtain approval of an oil spill response plan, and implement it
     effectively once issued.
   ▪ Invest in equipment and materials to gradually improve the self-response capacity of the
     port and waterborne transport.
   ▪ Organize annual training and at least biannual drills for staff and response forces.
   ▪ Sign agreements/contracts with competent agencies/units for oil spill response support.
   ▪ Equip each fishing vessel with a basic oil spill response kit (booms, absorbent materials,
     buckets, etc.). Equipment should be adapted by vessel type (e.g., offshore vessels with
     absorbents; nearshore vessels sharing equipment via fishing teams or the port).
   ▪ Organize oil spill response training specifically for fishermen, suited to their vessel
     conditions.
   ▪ Map coastal ecologically sensitive areas for priority protection during oil spills.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Proactively deploy response activities, mobilizing resources promptly and effectively when
     spills occur.
   ▪ Immediately notify authorities of spill location, scale, and cause, and begin on-site
     response:
      ➢ Ensure site safety; mobilize all available resources to contain oil; deploy booms to
         prevent spread.
      ➢ Use available means such as absorbents, buckets, etc., to collect oil.
      ➢ Mobilize local communities to assist in response.
      ➢ Pump remaining fuel in the affected vessel to another storage vessel to ensure safety
         during the response process. During the transfer, measures must be implemented to
         prevent any oil spillage.
− Compensation

   ▪ If it is determined that the port response is ineffective or the oil spill incident may cause
     more serious consequences, promptly report to the PPC for direct response guidance.
   ▪ Coordinate with relevant local authorities to monitor and assess the effectiveness of oil
     spill remediation efforts.
   ▪ Temporarily store and safeguard the collected oil for reuse or to ensure compensation for
     damages caused by the spill. The handover of collected oil should be based on the agreed
     compensation amount with the vessel owner.
   ▪ Upon completion of remediation activities, report to the competent authorities on the
     situation and results of the incident response.
   ▪ Assess environmental damage after the incident in accordance with the guidelines of the
     MAE.
   ▪ Implement compensation for damages in accordance with the provisions of the Law on
     Environmental Protection.
h.2). Ship collision incidents
− Avoidance




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          215
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Install navigation aids (buoys, lights, signal boards) and ensure compliance with
     international and national maritime regulations.
   ▪ Develop and enforce port traffic management plans, including designated berthing and
     maneuvering zones.
− Minimization

   ▪ Enforce speed limits for vessels within the port area.
   ▪ Provide training and awareness programs for boat owners, captains, and crews on collision
     prevention and safe navigation practices.
   ▪ Monitor and regulate vessel density during peak fishing seasons to minimize congestion.
   ▪ Maintain continuous communication systems (VHF radio, port control center) to guide
     vessel entry, berthing, and departure.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Establish an emergency response plan for ship collision incidents, including oil spill
     response, firefighting, and search-and-rescue measures.
   ▪ Equip the port with emergency response equipment such as oil booms, skimmers,
     firefighting systems, and rescue boats.
   ▪ Train port staff and vessel crews in emergency drills to ensure readiness in case of collision.
− Remediation (if any)

   ▪ In case of oil spills or hazardous substance release, immediately deploy containment and
     cleanup operations, followed by water quality monitoring.
   ▪ Repair damaged port infrastructure (berths, navigation aids) and vessels in accordance with
     safety regulations.
h.3). Fires and explosions
Prevention and response to fire/explosion incidents in the fishing port and vessel operations
must comply with current laws, particularly the Law on Fire Prevention and Fighting, Decree
136/2020/ND-CP, and national technical standards.
− Avoidance

   ▪ Train fishermen and port staff in fire prevention and firefighting skills.
   ▪ Comply with national fire safety standards at fishing ports, landing sites, anchorage areas,
     and vessels.
   ▪ Equip port warehouses, offices, and logistics facilities with fire/explosion prevention
     systems in compliance with national standards (fire extinguishers, fire alarms, hydrants,
     firefighting beats/canoes, etc.).
   ▪ Provide portable fire extinguishers suitable for fishing vessels (e.g., ABC powder, CO₂),
     ensuring capacity to handle initial incidents.
   ▪ Clearly designate no-fire, no-smoking, no-welding areas at the port and on vessels.
   ▪ Conduct periodic inspections of electrical, fuel, and flammable systems on vessels and at
     the port.
− Minimization

   ▪ The fire water supply system includes both indoor and outdoor firefighting water systems
     (at offices and storage warehouses).
   ▪ For buildings within the fish port, fish wharf, or mooring areas with a volume > 5,000 m³,
     two fire hydrants must be installed in easily visible and accessible locations, with a water
     flow rate of 2.5 liters/second per hydrant. Additionally, warehouses should be equipped
     with foam fire extinguishers.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         216
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Establish emergency response procedures in case of fire or explosion on vessels or at the
     port.
   ▪ Mobilize on-site personnel and coordinate with the local fire prevention and firefighting
     police.
− Compensation

   ▪ Assess environmental damage after fire/explosion.
   ▪ Compensate damages as required by the Law on Environmental Protection.
   ▪ Restore sites and manage waste generated after the incident.
h.4). Failure of the wastewater treatment system of the port
− Avoidance

   ▪ Develop a maintenance plan: There should be a plan for regular maintenance and
     inspection of the wastewater treatment system to promptly detect signs of damage or
     system degradation.
   ▪ Inspect system structure: Ensure that joints, valves, and other system components are not
     loose, damaged, or corroded.
   ▪ Comply with all design, construction, operation, maintenance, and servicing requirements
     of the wastewater treatment system.
   ▪ Train wastewater treatment operators on the system’s operation and maintenance program.
− Minimization

   ▪ Regularly monitor the operation of machinery and the condition of treatment tanks; prepare
     backup equipment for machinery prone to failure, such as wastewater pumps, blowers,
     sludge pumps, etc.
   ▪ Arrange motors/pumps in all modules with one active unit and one standby unit (operating
     alternately) to ensure the system remains functional during incidents.
   ▪ Provide a backup power supply for the centralized wastewater treatment station.
   ▪ Assign personnel to operate and supervise the wastewater treatment system according to
     the trained procedures.
   ▪ Install leak or abnormal pressure warning systems on main pipeline sections.
Mitigation
   ▪ Design and arrange an emergency tank of 250m3 with appropriate capacity at the
     wastewater treatment station to temporarily store wastewater in case of emergencies.
   ▪ In case the wastewater treatment station experiences machinery or equipment failure and
     cannot continue operation, immediately close the discharge valves. Wastewater should be
     stored in the emergency tank to identify the cause and location of the incident. Only
     discharge treated wastewater that meets standards into the environment.
   ▪ When an incident occurs, wastewater treatment station operators must notify the
     management department, isolate the affected module, and ensure that wastewater from the
     faulty module is not discharged into the environment. Wastewater generated from project
     activities should be stored in the emergency tank, while treatment continues normally in
     the remaining modules.
   ▪ After resolving the incident, pump all wastewater from the tanks in the affected module
     back to the equalization tank for continued treatment to meet standards before discharge
     into the environment.
   ▪ Keep emergency supplies ready, such as shut-off valves, replacement pipes, and water
     pumps, for rapid on-site response.
   ▪ Establish a rapid response team at the fish port to handle environmental incidents related
     to wastewater.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page         217
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

Remediation
  ▪ Conduct rapid environmental impact assessments after incidents and report to authorities.
  ▪ Restore affected systems and areas, including sludge removal, disinfection, and water
    quality testing.
h.5). Risk of natural hazards at the port
− Avoidance

   ▪ Ensure full compliance with the Law on Natural Disaster Prevention and Control No.
     33/2013/QH13 and Decree No. 66/2014/ND-CP, as well as related national guidelines.
   ▪ Design and operate port facilities in accordance with disaster-resilient standards (e.g.,
     reinforced docking areas, secure anchorage zones).
   ▪ Allocate designated safe anchorage areas that are outside the main navigation channel and
     protected against waves and strong currents.
   ▪ Strong structural design: Build the fish port, mooring areas, and technical infrastructure
     with durable, corrosion-resistant materials suited for river and marine environments,
     ensuring they can withstand strong winds, high waves, and heavy rain.
   ▪ Design mooring areas to safeguard vessels during extreme weather.
   ▪ Install early warning systems: Track weather changes and water levels to provide timely
     alerts.
   ▪ Develop a disaster prevention plan: Prepare a detailed plan for the fish port and mooring
     areas, including concrete measures to reduce risks to people and property during natural
     disasters, and update it regularly.
− Minimization

   ▪ The Port Management Board must develop annual disaster prevention and response plans,
     taking into account local hydrometeorological conditions.
   ▪ Coordinate closely with the Provincial Command for Disaster Prevention and Search and
     Rescue to ensure readiness in case of storms, floods, and oil spills.
   ▪ Ensure that all vessels entering the port are equipped with basic safety equipment and
     communication systems.
   ▪ Require captains and crew to strictly comply with directives during storms or tropical
     depressions.
   ▪ Establish an emergency communication system between the fish port and relevant
     authorities to coordinate response efforts.
   ▪ Regularly assess and update risk levels: Conduct studies and update information on the
     impacts of natural disasters.
   ▪ Monitor information from specialized forecasting agencies to implement early response
     plans.
   ▪ Provide skills for evacuation, asset protection, and basic rescue during storms, floods, or
     high tides.
   ▪ Ensure vessels are securely and safely moored when warnings are issued.
   ▪ Establish and maintain stations equipped for evacuation and rescue in case of incidents.
     Evacuation points should be clearly marked and easily accessible.
   ▪ Ensure fishing boats, rescue boats, and transport equipment function properly in severe
     weather. Provide all necessary lifesaving equipment, life jackets, and protective gear.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Establish an on-site emergency response team trained in disaster response, rescue
     operations, and oil spill containment.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page          218
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Regularly organize drills with fishing boat operators, port staff, and local authorities on
     storm/flood evacuation and oil spill responses.
   ▪ Provide clear communication channels (radio, public announcement systems) for timely
     updates during emergencies.
   ▪ Monitor and inspect anchorage conditions, ensuring boats are properly moored and
     secured.
− Remediation (if any)

   ▪ In case of storm or flood damage, promptly repair port infrastructure (wharves, anchorage
     facilities, navigation signs).
   ▪ Provide emergency relief (food, medical support, temporary shelter) for crew members
     affected by disasters.
   ▪ In the event of an oil spill or hazardous release, implement immediate containment,
     cleanup, and environmental remediation measures in coordination with relevant agencies.
   ▪ Conduct post-disaster assessments to identify weaknesses in response systems and
     improve future prevention plans.

5.1.4.2. Mitigation measures during operation of the embankments (Component 2.1)

466. The impacts and risks during operation include: (i) Safety risks and emissions, (ii) Risks
of inundation due to embankment effect; (iii) Restricted access from the roads to houses; and
(iv) Risk of embankment collapse. The mitigation for these impacts are presented below:

a) Mitigation Measures for Safety Risks and Emissions

a.1). Emissions
− Avoidance
   ▪ Prohibit the operation of vehicles that are not registered or do not meet emission standards
     on the embankment roads.
   ▪ Avoid scheduling intensive heavy truck traffic during peak community activity hours (e.g.,
     school, market times).
− Minimization

   ▪ Require all vehicles to be covered when carrying soil, sand, or materials to prevent dust
     from dropping along the embankment.
   ▪ Maintain low operating speeds on rural roads (≤ 30 km/h for trucks, ≤ 20 km/h in
     residential stretches) to reduce dust generation.
   ▪ Encourage shared transport or optimized routing to minimize traffic volume.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Regularly sweep and clean sand/soil from the embankment and road surface.
   ▪ Plant and maintain trees or green belts on both embankment sides to capture dust and
     improve air quality.
   ▪ Periodically check vehicles’ emission conditions and enforce repairs if standards are
     exceeded.
− Remediation (if any)

   ▪ If significant dust deposition affects households along the embankment, organize water
     spraying or temporary dust suppression.
   ▪ For oil or hazardous waste spills, implement immediate containment, cleanup, and
     soil/water remediation measures.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          219
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

a.2). Measures for Traffic Risk Safety
− Avoidance

   ▪ Design the embankment-road system in line with national traffic safety standards, ensuring
     adequate width, curvature, and slope to prevent accidents.
   ▪ Place clear traffic signs and speed limit boards along embankments, especially in
     residential and crowded areas, and intersections
   ▪ Build turnout points (U-turns) along the embankments with a surface width of below 3m
     to facilitate smooth vehicle movement and avoid congestion.
− Minimization

   ▪ Raise awareness among road users and local communities on traffic safety regulations and
     good practices for using the embankments.
   ▪ Monitor and enforce compliance with speed limits, loading limits, and safe driving
     behaviors.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Regularly maintain road surfaces, guardrails, and signage to reduce the risks of accidents
     due to poor infrastructure conditions.
   ▪ Install reflective markers at critical sections to enhance visibility during nighttime or
     adverse weather conditions.
− Remediation (if any)

   ▪ In case of accidents, promptly provide first aid and coordinate with local health services
     for emergency response.
   ▪ Repair damaged sections of embankments and roads to restore safety and prevent
     recurrence.

b) Mitigation Measures for Inundation due to New Embankments

− Minimization

   ▪ Notify households early about the approved crest elevation so they can align any planned
     floor upgrades to reduce inundation risk.
   ▪ Engage households to clean up drainage ditches and open the culverts when heavy rains.

c) Mitigation Measures for Restricted Access from the Roads to Houses

− Avoidance

   ▪ Incorporate access ramps in the design phase to ensure household connectivity once the
     embankment is operational.
− Minimization

   ▪ Provide designated access corridors or side paths for pedestrians, motorbikes, and
     vulnerable group (e.g. children, elderly) at household clusters.
   ▪ Coordinate with local authorities and communities to align household floor elevation
     upgrades with embankment road height to maintain seamless access.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Construct permanent ramps connecting the embankment road to household entrances to
     ensure safe and continuous access (if needed).
− Remediation

   ▪ For households still encountering access difficulties after project completion, implement
     corrective works such as ramp adjustments, elevation filling, or access widening.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page         220
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Offer technical support for households that need to raise their entrance level or adapt their
     private access in response to the new embankment height.
   ▪ Local authorities should have a plan for periodic monitoring and maintenance of ramps,
     access paths during the operational phase to prevent deterioration that could hinder access.

d) Mitigation Measures for Risk of Embankment Collapse

− Avoidance

   ▪ Design the embankment with a stability factor, and climate change resilience to avoid
     collapse risks during operation.
− Minimization

   ▪ Implement a scheduled inspection and maintenance program.
   ▪ Install monitoring markers to track settlement, deformation, seepage, and erosion early
   ▪ Prohibit activities that compromise embankment integrity, such as vehicle overloading or
     pond excavation near the embankment toe.
Mitigation
   ▪ Establish early-warning systems and assign local management units to conduct continuous
     monitoring during high tide, storm events, or heavy rainfall.
   ▪ Prepare an emergency response plan for rapid containment and traffic rerouting.
− Remediation

   ▪ Implement immediate repair in case of erosion, scouring, settlement, or cracking, including
     backfilling, slope regrading, and reinforcing with geotechnical materials.
   ▪ Activate emergency response plans in case of embankment breach, including temporary
     closure, evacuation support, and rapid containment measures to protect shrimp ponds and
     residential areas.
   ▪ Provide restoration and compensation for affected aquaculture and livelihoods if damage
     occurs due to embankment failure during operation.

5.1.4.3. Mitigation measures during operation for the dredged canals (component 2.2)

467. The risk and impacts during the operation of dredged canals include (i) Risk of erosion;
and (ii) Risk of canal pollution due to siltation and accumulated waste. The mitigation measures
for these risks are presented below:

a) Mitigation Measures for Canal Bank Erosion Risk

− Avoidance

   ▪ Prohibit overloading boats or placing excessive weight along canal banks to prevent
     collapse or erosion.
   ▪ Establish and protect the right of way (ROW) to prevent encroachment, illegal
     construction, or activities that could weaken the banks.
− Minimization

   ▪ Allocate adequate budget and human resources for routine inspections and maintenance to
     minimize risks early
   ▪ Conduct periodic canal bank maintenance periodically, especially prior to the rainy season,
     to minimize erosion risks.
   ▪ Regularly monitor high-risk areas such as soft soil sections, steep embankments, and areas
     prone to landslide or subsidence.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          221
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   ▪ Provide awareness raising for local communities on safe canal use and bank protection
     practices
Mitigation
   ▪ Prepare and implement emergency response plans for canal erosion, including coordination
     with adjacent communities for prompt action.
   ▪ Carry out corrective works immediately when early signs of erosion, cracks, or instability
     are detected during inspections.
   ▪ Reinforce embankments using appropriate reinforcement methods (e.g., vegetation cover,
     bioengineering, revetments) where erosion risks are identified.
   ▪ Coordinate closely with local authorities and communities in protecting embankments and
     preventing illegal activities that exacerbate erosion.
− Remediation (if any)

   ▪ In the event of severe erosion or collapse, repair and restore the damaged canal bank using
     engineered stabilization measures (e.g., gabions, riprap, reinforced earth).
   ▪ Relocate or support affected households if erosion impacts their properties or livelihoods.
   ▪ Rehabilitate the canal’s drainage capacity to restore its function after erosion incidents.

b) Mitigation Measures for Risk of Canal Pollution Due To Siltation and Accumulated
   Waste

− Avoidance

   ▪ Prohibit direct discharge of untreated domestic wastewater, aquaculture effluents, and solid
     waste into the canal.
− Minimization

   ▪ Implement regular canal maintenance and desiltation schedules to minimize gradual
     sediment buildup.
   ▪ Promote the use of settling ponds or filtration systems for aquaculture discharge before
     water enters the canal.
   ▪ Require surrounding households and farms to comply with waste management regulations
     and use designated waste collection systems.
− Mitigation

   ▪ Raise awareness among communities on responsible waste disposal
   ▪ Provide training on best management practices for aquaculture water discharge.
− Remediation

   ▪ Remove accumulated silt and organic sludge through mechanical dredging if water quality
     drops below acceptable standards.
   ▪ Restore canal water flow capacity by clearing blockages, repairing eroded banks, and
     reestablishing vegetation for bank stabilization.

5.1.5. Measures for cumulative impacts
 − The management of cumulative impacts within the project, including air pollution, traffic
   safety, traffic congestion, generation of excavated/dredged materials, domestic solid waste,
   hazardous waste, and domestic wastewater, must adhere to the specified mitigation measures
   in Section 5.1.3.4 to 5.1.3.7. These waste types should not be stored on-site for extended
   periods and must be regularly transported to designated disposal sites.
 − Furthermore, during the construction phase, contractors would need to share information
   about construction plans with each other and with other projects in order to mitigate the

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          222
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   cumulative impacts of conflicts in the use of traffic infrastructure, electricity and water
   supply, etc. Good information sharing and cooperation would assist them in adjusting their
   construction schedules correctly and limiting disputes caused by excessive use of
   transportation roads and services.

5.1.6. Measures for induced impacts

− For the embankments and the port

   ▪ Regularly inspect the embankment sections, and propagate and educate local authorities
     and people about their responsibility to protect the embankments.
   ▪ Correctly implement the province’s policies on sustainable development.
   ▪ Under the province’s socio-economic development planning.
   ▪ Comply with the Law on Environmental Protection, Forestry, Biodiversity, Water
     Resources, and other relevant legal regulations.
   ▪ Strictly handle illegal construction in embankment safety corridors.
− For dredged canals

   ▪ Establish a demonstration livelihood model of multi-species farming by applying the
     modern technology transferred by the project.
   ▪ Conduct a workshop to share the successful model of shrimp farming.
   ▪ Establish an interest group who are capable of investing in the required infrastructure and
     application of modern technology for shrimp farming.

5.1.7. Measures for the Impacts of Climate Change
− Measures for impacts of climate change on the project include:

   ▪ Taking into account the scenario of climate change in Ca Mau in 2050 in the project design
   ▪ Using salt-tolerant materials for the upgradation of Rach Goc fish port
− Measures for the impacts of the project on climate change:

   ▪ GHG emission during construction:
      ➢ Encourage contractors to use fuel-efficient equipment, machinery, and vehicles.
      ➢ Encourage contractors to use biofuels during construction
   ▪ GHG emission during operation:
      ➢ Encourage road users to use biofuels for their vehicles
      ➢ Encourage road users to use fuel-efficient vehicles

     ESMP

implementation of the environmental protection plan are summarised in

  included in the bidding documents and costs will be included in the construction bidding
  packages.
− CSC is responsible for day-to-day monitoring of the implementation of mitigation measures,
  and the costs that will be included in the service contract with CSC.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page          223
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

− The Independent Social and Environment Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) is responsible for
 periodic monitoring of the social environment, including assisting PMU in environmental
 monitoring and monitoring, training on environmental and social safety implementation, and
 reporting on the implementation of environmental protection plans through monitoring
 reports.

5.2.2. Environmental and Social Compliance Framework
5.2.2.1. Environmental and Social Duties of the PMU/Detailed Design Consultants

 − When preparing TORs for consulting services and construction bidding documents, the
   PMU will work closely with consultants/contractors to ensure: (i) contract packaging and
   cost estimations, including ESMP implementation, the services of independent E&S
   monitoring, environmental sampling/monitoring and compliance supervision, reporting,
   etc.; (ii) ESCOPs, as well as site-specific mitigation measures, are incorporated into the
   bidding documents; (iii) environmental supervision and training are included in the scope of
   works assigned to the CSC.
 − In the FS/detailed design stage, the PMU shall work closely with the FS consultant and the
   detailed design engineers to ensure that the greening/landscaping, environmentally friendly
   solutions, and relevant mitigation measures proposed in the ESIA/ESMP are considered and
   incorporated into the detailed engineering design as appropriate.
 − During the construction phase, the PMU shall work closely with the CSC to monitor the
   compliance of contractors and report to the relevant authorities and the WB. The PMU will

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  Page           226
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   also direct the CSC and contractors on the actions to be undertaken in the case when issues,
   incidents, or accidents arising. Besides, PMU will be responsible for ensuring the effective
   implementation of the LMP during construction.
 − Throughout project implementation, the PMU will appoint at least one qualified individual
   as EO and one qualified individual as SO, in which:
     ▪ The EO will oversee environmental issues and monitor the standards compliance of the
        project. The EO will be supported by the IMC, the EOs of the CSC, as well as contractors.
     ▪ The SO will oversee and coordinate the social/resettlement aspects of the project.

5.2.2.2. Environmental and Social Duties of the Contractor

473. The contractor is responsible for implementing measures to control and mitigate adverse
impacts and risks arising from construction activities. The mitigation measures specified in the
ESMP must be fully applied to prevent impacts on the local community and the environment
throughout the construction phase.
474. Remedial actions that cannot be effectively carried out during construction should be
carried out on completion of the works (and before issuance of the acceptance of completion of
works).
475. The duties of the Contractor include, but not limited to:
  − Compliance with relevant legislative requirements governing the environment, public
    health, and safety;
  − Work within the scope of contractual requirements and other tender conditions;

  − Organize representatives of the construction team to participate in the joint site inspections
    undertaken by the Environmental Supervisor (ES) of the CSC;
  − Carry out any corrective actions instructed by the Environmental Officer (EO) of the PMU
    and ES of the CSC; In case of non-compliance/discrepancies, carry out an investigation and
    submit proposals on mitigation measures, and implement remedial measures to reduce
    environmental impact.
  − Stop construction activities, which generate adverse impacts upon receiving instructions
    from the EO and ES. Propose and carry out corrective actions and implement alternative
    construction methods, if required, to minimize the environmental impacts; Non-compliance
    by the Contractor will be cause for suspension of works and other penalties until the non-
    compliance has been resolved to the satisfaction of the EO and ES.

5.2.2.3. Contractor's Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP)

476. After signing the contract, the contractor will be required to prepare and submit a
Contractor’s Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) for each contract package
and submit it to the CSC and PMU for review and approval.
477. The C-ESMP aims to provide information for environmental and social management
during the project construction on-site to ensure that the contractor and any sub-constructors
have a small environmental impact. It specifies how the contractor will reduce impacts caused
by the construction of the project and documents the contractor’s response to supervision,
monitoring, verification, internal review, and correction or improvement of E&S performance.
It must be site-specific and include information on the control measures that will be applied on-
site to mitigate any potential E&S impacts of the proposed works/activities.
478. If the proposed works/activities outlined in the C-ESMP are changed or modified over
the term of the contract, the contractor will be expected to update the C-ESMP to reflect these
changes or modifications. The C-ESMP should comprise the following sections:


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          227
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

 −A     statement of policy, defining the contractor’s E&S policy and an indication of a
    commitment to the execution of its C-ESMP.
  − A brief of the document, including the date of issue, revision status, and a list of people with
    their signatures.
  − Applicable laws and regulations associated with the requirements in the ESMP and
    identification of the contractor licenses, permits, and approvals associated with the CESMP.
  − Details on how the E&S impacts and risks identified in the ESIA will be managed on-site,
    including (i) the site-specific measures to mitigate impacts during construction;
    (ii) ESCOPs; (iii) the C-ESMP to be developed after the contractor is selected and before
    construction starts; and (iv) the contractor’s dredging and material management plan and
    other sub-plans that the contractor is required to develop.
  − Details on E&S training for all on-site staff of the contractor (including subcontractors) are
    required. As a minimum, all contractor staff working at the project sites must: (i) be familiar
    with and understand the C-ESMP for their works; (ii) be aware of their environmental and
    social responsibilities and legal obligations on-site; and (iii) receive OHS and emergency
    response training.
  − Specific capabilities, support mechanisms, and resources necessary to satisfactorily
    implement the C-ESMP. Detailed environmental and social responsibilities of all contractor
    staff, including subcontractors working on-site, with appropriate knowledge, skills, and
    training for specific tasks shall be identified.
479. The contractor shall be responsible for preparing monthly environmental reports as part
of the progress reports required in the bidding document, including any accident and incident
reporting, for submission to the project owner. These reports may contain the following
information: (i) Implementation of the C-ESMP per the agreed program; (ii) Any difficulties
encountered during C-ESMP implementation, as well as recommendations for future solutions;
(iii) The number and type of non-compliance, as well as suggested corrective actions; (iv)
Reports from subcontractors involved in the C-ESMP implementation, including minutes of
meetings and discussions undertaken by the contractor; (v) Minutes of meetings and discussions
held by the project owner regarding C-ESMP implementation; and (vi) Implementation of the
workers’ CoC and OHS management plan, including community safety.

5.2.2.4. Contractor’s Safety and Environment Officer (C-ES)

480. The contractor shall be required to appoint a competent individual as the Contractor’s on-
site safety and environment officer (C-ES). The C-ES must be appropriately trained in
environmental management and must possess the skills necessary to transfer environmental
management knowledge to all personnel involved in the contract. The C-ES will be responsible
for monitoring the contractor’s compliance with the ESMP requirements and the environmental
specifications. The duties of the C-ES shall include but not be limited to the following:
  − Carry out environmental site inspections to assess and audit the contractors' site practice,
    equipment, and work methodologies concerning pollution control and adequacy of
    environmental mitigation measures implemented;
  − Monitor compliance with environmental protection measures, pollution prevention and
    control measures, and contractual requirements;
  − Monitor the implementation of environmental mitigation measures;

  − Prepare audit reports for the environmental monitoring data and site environmental
    conditions.
  − Investigate complaints and recommend any required corrective measures.

  − Advise the contractor on environmental improvement, awareness, and proactive pollution
    prevention measures.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         228
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

 − Recommend suitable mitigation measures to the contractor in the case of non-compliance.
   Carry out additional monitoring of noncompliance as instructed by the PMU’s EO and
   CSC’ES.
 − Inform environmental issues to the contractor, ESO and ES (from PMU and CSC),
   submitting the contractor’s ESMP implementation reports to the PMU’s ESO, CSC’s ES,
   and other relevant agencies as required.
 − Keep detailed records of all site activities that may relate to the environment.

 − Utilizing or appointing qualified staff to implement required actions and measures to ensure
   labor-related issues are addressed, such as occupational health and safety (OHS) specialists
   to manage OHS matters.
 − Preparing the Contractor’s Labor Management Procedures (LMP) and Contractor’s ESMP
   (C-ESMP), including OHS provisions, applicable to contracted workers under the project.
 − Submitting the contractor’s LMP and C-ESMP to the PMU/CSC for review and approval
   before mobilizing staff to the project site.
 − Supervising subcontractors’works, including their compliance with the contractor’s LMP
   and C-ESMP.
 − Keeping employment records for contracted workers (including subcontractors), including
   documentation verifying minimum working age as stipulated in the LMP.
 − Providing regular training for contracted workers on topics including, but not limited to,
   occupational health and safety, social risks such as GBV, SEA/SH, and the Code of Conduct
   to maintain good relations with local communities.
 − Requiring primary suppliers to identify and address risks related to child labor, forced labor,
   GBV, SEA/SH, and OHS for their workers.
 − Establishing and implementing a grievance mechanism for contracted workers based on the
   GRM outlined in this LMP, ensuring that worker complaints are addressed promptly, and
   reporting the status of grievances and resolutions to the CSC and PMU. This grievance
   mechanism shall be part of the contractor’s LMP.
 − Ensuring that all contractors and subcontractor workers understand and sign the Code of
   Conduct before commencing work.
 − Taking all necessary measures to address risks of sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA) and
   sexual harassment (SH) as stipulated in the contractor’s LMP/C-ESMP and ensuring full
   compliance with these measures.
 − Reporting incidents at the construction site and labor/OHS implementation status to the
   PMU in a timely manner.
481. Incident Reporting: Contractors shall notify the Construction Supervision Consultant
and the Project Owner of any incidents listed below within the agreed timeframe:
 − Any violation of national laws, regulations, or international agreements.

 − Any serious accidents or fatalities.

 − Significant impacts resulting in personal property damage, such as traffic accidents, damage
   to houses/roads, or other incidents.
 − Severe surface water/groundwater pollution.

 − Failures of dikes/embankments at temporary disposal sites causing serious environmental
   pollution.
 − Any fire incident linked to worker behavior or labor influx.

 − Any complaints related to sexual harassment, child abuse, or other child-related incidents.

 − Complaints received regarding pollution or damage




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page         229
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

5.2.2.5. Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (ISEMC)

482. Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) will be
contracted by the PMU to provide several environmental protection training courses for
relevant parties and to carry out environmental inspections. The IESMC will perform
monitoring tasks, including:
  − Providing support to the PMU in establishing and operating the environmental and social
    management system (EMS), coordinating with the CSC team to train Contractors on the
    Project’s ESHS management requirements.
  − Organizing training courses and implementing capacity building activities for the PMU,
    CSC, Contractors, and relevant stakeholders on ESHS during Project implementation. This
    includes conducting training sessions to raise awareness on Gender/Labor Influx issues for
    Contractor staff, CSC staff, and PMU officers.
  − Carrying out periodical field inspections each 6 months to certify compliance or propose
    timely corrective/improvement actions.
  − Assessing environmental quality in areas affected by construction activities (including site
    observations, reviewing environmental quality data provided by the CSC, reviewing other
    available documents, and conducting additional sampling if necessary)
  − Reviewing the Contractor’s environmental compliance, including the implementation of
    mitigation measures and related documentation
  − Reviewing the compliance of the PMU and CSC with the ESMP

  − The IESMC will also provide guidance and technical support to the PMU and EO regarding
    environmental and social issues.

5.2.2.6. Environmental Supervision during Construction

483. During the construction stage, a qualified CSC reporting to the PMU shall carry out the
environmental supervision. The CSC is responsible for inspecting and supervising all
construction activities to ensure that mitigation measures adopted in the ESMP are properly
implemented and that the negative environmental impacts of the Project are minimized. The
CSC shall engage a sufficient number of Environmental Supervision Engineers with adequate
knowledge of environmental protection and construction project management to perform the
required duties and to supervise the Contractor’s performance. Specifically, ES will:
  − Review and assess, on behalf of the PMU, whether the construction design meets the
    requirements of mitigation measures and impact management under the ESMP
  − Review and approve C’ESMPs

  − Coordinate with the PMU’s Environmental Officer (EO) in reviewing environmental
    compliance at proposed new material and quarry sites, and advise the PMU on whether these
    extraction sites qualify for Project use
  − Verify and confirm with the PMU’s environmental monitoring procedures, including
    parameters, monitoring locations, equipment, and monitoring results
  − Supervise the Contractor’s implementation of the C-ESMP, including enforcement,
    proposing timely additional measures to improve mitigation efforts, and providing corrective
    guidance.
  − Prepare monthly reports on the Contractor’s ESHS compliance status.

  − Review payment requests related to environmental mitigation costs, if any




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page          230
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

5.2.2.7. Compliance with Legal and Contractual Requirements

 − The construction activities shall comply not only with contractual environmental protection
   and pollution control requirements but also with the environmental protection and pollution
   control laws of Viet Nam.
 − All the work method statements submitted by the Contractor to the CSC for approval shall
   also be sent to the ES to see whether sufficient environmental protection and pollution
   control measures have been included.
 − The ES shall also review the progress and program of the works to check that relevant
   environmental laws have not been violated, and that any potential for violating the laws can
   be prevented.
 − The Contractor shall copy relevant documents to the EO/ESO of PMU and the ES of CSC.
   The document shall at least include the updated work progress report, the updated work
   measure, and the application letters for different licenses/permits under the environmental
   protection laws, and all the valid licenses/permits. The ESO/EO and the ES shall also have
   access, upon request, to the Site Log-Book.
 − After reviewing the documents, the ES shall advise the C-ES and the contractor of any non-
   compliance with the contractual and legislative requirements on environmental protection
   and pollution control for them to take follow-up actions. If the ESOES concludes that the
   status of the license/permit application and any environmental protection and pollution
   control preparation works may not comply with the work measure or may result in a potential
   violation of environmental protection and pollution control requirements, they shall advise
   the Contractor and PMU accordingly

5.2.2.8. Environmental Claims and Penalty System

484. The bidding documents will require the Contractor to arrange an Environmental, Social,
Health, and Safety (ESHS) Performance Security in the amount of 1–3% of the contract value.
In the event of serious non-compliance, environmental pollution, or social issues that the
Contractor fails to remedy within the permitted timeframe, funds from the ESHS Performance
Security will be used to pay a third party mobilized by the PMU to resolve these issues.
485. In the compliance framework, if non-compliance with environmental regulations is
discovered by ESO/CSC/ES/ISEMC during the site supervision, 2% values of the interim
payment of the contractor for this month will be held back. The Contractor will be given a grace
period (determined by CSC/ES) to repair the violation. If the Contractor performs the repairs
within the grace period (confirmed by CSC/ES), no penalty is incurred, and keeping money
will be paid. However, if the Contractor fails to successfully make the necessary repairs within
the grace period, the Contractor will pay the cost for a third party to repair the damages
(deduction from keeping money).
486. In case IESMC/CSC/ES is not detected as non-compliance with the environmental
regulations of the contractor, they will be responsible for payment to repair the violation.

5.3. REPORTING

487. The E&S performance will be included in the project progress reports. The CSC, with
assistance from the CSC/field engineers, will submit the E&S performance of the project to the
PMU monthly. The PMU will prepare an E&S monitoring report twice per year for submission
to the WB describing the project progress and compliance with the ESSs and other requirements.
The reporting requirement is described in Table 87.
