5.4. ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING AND SUPERVISION PROGRAM

5.4.1. Waste Generation Monitoring
 − The  content of waste generation monitoring includes the quantity and composition of
   domestic solid waste and wastewater, construction waste, and wasted oil.
 − Monitoring frequency: Every 3 months.

 − Implementation period: Starts from the site preparation phase until the end of the
   construction phase.
 − This task is directly carried out by the contractors. The project owner and CSC will monitor
   the performance of the contractors and report to the Department of Agriculture and
   Environment after each monitoring period.

5.4.2. Other Monitoring

5.4.2.1. During Construction Phase

 − Surface water monitoring:

    ▪ Frequency: Once every 3 months during construction
    ▪ Monitoring sites: (i) in the construction area of the port: 1 sample; (ii) in the construction
       area of the dredged canals: 6 samples (each sample at each dredged canal); and (iii) in the
       construction area of the embankments: 9 samples.
    ▪ Monitoring parameters: pH, BOD5, COD, TSS, DO, Total N, Total P, Total Coliform,
       and Ecoli.
    ▪ Applied standards: Table 3 of the QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT - National technical
       regulations on surface water quality.
 − Dredged and excavated materials monitoring:

    ▪ Frequency: 1 time before commencement of the dredging and excavation activities.
    ▪ Monitoring sites: (i) in the construction area of the port: 2 samples; (ii) in the construction
       area of the dredged canals: 6 samples (each sample at each dredged canal); and (iii) in the
       construction area of the embankments: 10 samples.
    ▪ Monitoring parameters: pH, Cu, Fe, Mn, Zn, Cd, As, salinity
    ▪ Applied standards: QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT - National technical regulations on sediment
       quality (saltwater and brackish water sediment).
 − Air monitoring:



Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page          232
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

    ▪ Frequency: Once every 3 months during construction
    ▪ Monitoring sites: (i) in the construction area of the port: 1 sample; (ii) in the construction
      area of the embankment: 6 samples (1 sample at embankment location).
    ▪ Monitoring parameters: Noise level, TSP.
    ▪ Applied standards: QCVN 05/2023/BTNMT- National technical regulation on ambient
      air quality; QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT - National technical regulation on noise.
 − Canal bed elevation and erosion monitoring

    ▪ Frequency: 1 time after completion of each dredged canal.
    ▪ Monitoring sites: within ongoing canal dredging and at the Rach Goc port
    ▪ Monitoring parameters: elevation and erosion. Measurements will be carried out using
      standard topographic surveying techniques (e.g., total station or Global Navigation
      Satellite System-based survey) combined with visual inspection of canal banks to
      identify signs of erosion.
    ▪ Comparison requirements: changes over time in the elevation of the canal bed terrain
      and the evolution of the shoreline of the port construction areas.

5.4.2.2. During Operation Phase

488. Embankments: The operating unit will conduct embankment settlement monitoring 4
times a year to take prompt action to prevent damage to project items. Specifically:
  − Frequency: Once every 3 months.

  − Monitoring sites: within the system of embankments.

  − Monitoring parameters: settlement. Measurements will be carried out using leveling
    instrument and total station.
489. Port: The operating unit will conduct a river line change, as follows:
  − Frequency: Once every 3 months.

  − Monitoring sites: within the port.

  − Monitoring parameters: elevation and erosion. Measurements will be carried out using
    standard topographic surveying techniques (e.g., total station or Global Navigation Satellite
    System-based survey) combined with visual inspection of canal banks to identify signs of
    erosion.

5.5. TRAINING  AND  CAPACITY  BUILDING                                     PROGRAM             ON
     ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT

5.5.1. Current Ca Mau PMU’s Capacity for Environmental and Social Management
490. Ca Mau PMU is currently a team with experience in managing and implementing projects
using IBRD funds such as CRSD, WB6, WB9, etc. All of these projects have been implemented
in Ca Mau province and managed by the PMU. They were evaluated by the Ministry of
Agriculture and Environment and donors as well-executed, scheduled, and highly effective
projects. Therefore, it can be assessed that the PMU is capable of organizing and managing the
project implementation under the safeguard policies and international practices.
491. However, the PMU’s capacity needs to be improved because existing staff lack
experience with projects involving the WB’s new ESF. Furthermore, when the project is built,
other environmental and technical personnel will be engaged, some of whom may be unfamiliar
with the WB’s ESSs and ESF. As a result, there is a risk that the resources allocated to
addressing environmental and social issues in this project will be insufficient, negatively
impacting environmental performance/compliance with the ESSs.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page         233
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

492. Also, although the PMU attended many WB-organized trainings, an E&S capability
assessment completed during project preparation highlighted a need for additional capacity
building in specific ESSs throughout project implementation. As part of the ESMP, a capacity-
building program will be implemented to address such gaps.

5.5.2. Environmental and Social Capacity Building Program

493. The training programs on environmental and social management capacity for the project
will be developed and conducted by the IESMC, as follows (Table 88):
 − Trainee groups: PMU management staff and environmental-social officers, Construction
   Supervision Consultant (CSC), construction contractors, PMU procurement officers,
   representatives of stakeholders, and local communities in the project area.
 − Training schedule: Training will be organized at least one month before the implementation
   of the first construction contract. Subsequent training sessions may be adjusted to align with
   the construction schedule of project components.
 − Training frequency: The basic training programs outlined in Table 88 will be delivered every
   6 months, with content updated and tailored to the components under implementation.
   Training for PMU staff is expected to continue during the initial years of the Project. Two-
   day training sessions for CSC and contractors are also expected to be held twice a year for
   at least 2 years.

5.7. GRIEVANCE AND REDRESS MECHANISM (GRM)

5.7.1. Project Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM)

499. Within the Vietnamese legal framework citizen rights to complain are protected. As part
of the overall implementation of the project, a GRM will be established by the PMU. The GRM
will identify procedures, responsible persons, and contact information that will be readily
accessible to the public, and will handle grievances and resolve them at the lowest level as
quickly as possible. The mechanism will provide the framework within which complaints about
environmental and social issues can be handled, grievances can be addressed, and disputes can
be settled quickly. The GRM will be in place before the project construction commences.
500. During construction, the GRM will be managed by the Contractor under the supervision
of the CSC. The Contractor will inform the communities and communes affected by the project
about the GRM in place to handle complaints and concerns about the project. This will be done
via the information disclosure and consultation process under which the Contractor will
communicate with the affected communities and interested authorities regularly: hold meetings
at least quarterly, publish a monthly information brochure, place announcements in local media,
post notices of upcoming planned activities, and so on.
501. All complaints and corresponding actions undertaken by the Contractor will be recorded
in the project safeguard monitoring report. Complaints and claims for damages could be lodged
as follows:
  − Verbally: direct to the CSC and/or the contractor safeguard staff, or representative at the
    project office
  − In writing: by hand-delivering or posting a written complaint to the address specified

  − By telephone, fax, or email: to the CSC, the contractor safeguard staff, or the contractor’s
    representative.
502. On receipt of a complaint, the CSC, contractor safeguard staff, or representative will
register the complaint in the Complaints File and maintain a Log of events about it thereafter
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                      Page            238
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

until its resolution. Immediately after receipt, three copies of the complaint will be made. The
original will be kept in the File, one copy will be used by the contractor’s safeguard staff, one
copy will be forwarded to the CSC, and the third copy to the PMU within 24 hours of the
complaint being made. Information to be recorded in the Complaints Log will include:
  − The date and time of the complaint;

  − The name, address, and contact details of the complainant;

  − A short description of the issue of complaint;

  − Actions taken to address the complaint, including persons contacted and findings of each
    step in the complaint redress process;
  − The dates and times when the complainant is contacted during the redress process;

  − The final resolution of the complaint;

  − The date, time, and manner in which the complainant was informed thereof; and

  − The complainant’s signature when a resolution has been obtained.

503. Small complaints will be dealt with within one week. Within two weeks (and weekly
thereafter), a written reply will be delivered to the complainant (by hand, post, fax, e-mail)
indicating the procedures taken and progress to date.
504. The main objective of the GRM will be to resolve an issue as quickly as possible by the
simplest means involving as few people as possible, at the lowest possible level. Only when an
issue cannot be resolved at the simplest level and/or within 15 days will other authorities
become involved. Such a situation may arise, for example, when damages are claimed and the
amount to be paid cannot be resolved, or the cause of the damages determined.

5.7.2. Grievance Redress Mechanism for Workers

505. As mentioned above, for this project, the project GRM will be in place to address the
concerns of project-affected parties. However, the nature of workers' workplace concerns is
typically different, such as demand for employment opportunities; labor wage rates and
payment delays; disagreement over working conditions; and health and safety concerns in the
workplace. As a result, to meet the requirements of ESS2, a separate GRM will be developed
for project workers.
506. Grievances should be handled objectively, promptly, and in response to the needs and
concerns of the aggrieved workers. It should be possible for employees to submit grievances in
a variety of ways, including in person, via phone, text message, mail, and email. The grievance
should be recorded and acknowledged within a day. Even though the timing for remedy will
depend on the nature of the grievance, health, and safety concerns in the workplace and other
critical matters should be resolved immediately. When a grievance cannot be resolved within a
reasonable timeframe, the worker should be notified in writing so that he or she might consider
filing a claim with the state's labor inspection agency (see below for more details). In addition,
the mechanism will enable anonymous complaints to be made and addressed. Individuals who
submit comments or complaints may request that their identities remain confidential. The
mechanism will incur no cost, ensure no retribution, and will not prevent access to judicial or
administrative remedies.
507. Concerns of direct workers: Periodically, Ca Mau PMU will organize team meetings to
discuss any workplace issues. Direct workers' complaints will be documented along with the
PMU’s remedy actions. The summary of grievance cases will be reported to the WB as part of
progress reports. If an aggrieved direct worker wishes to escalate his issue or raise his concern
anonymously and/or to someone other than his line manager/PMU, he may do so with relevant
provincial authorities (e.g., DOHA).
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          239
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

508. Concerns of contracted workers. At the end of each working day, the site manager and
the OHS officer (or any other appropriate officer) of contractors will hold a team meeting with
all on-site contracted workers to discuss any workplace complaints. Any grievance will be
recorded with the contractor's response. The summary of grievance cases will be included in
the contractor's periodic report to the PMU and CSC. When suitable and available, contracted
workers should be permitted to use a GRM already in place within their contractor. If the
aggrieved workers prefer to escalate their issues or voice their complaints anonymously or to
someone other than their immediate supervisor, they may do so with the PMU and/or the CSC.
Before the start of work, contracted workers will be informed of the labor GRM. Contact
information for the PMU and/or the CSC will be provided to contracted workers.
509. State Inspection on Labor, Sanctions Against Violations of Labour Legislation.
According to the Labour Code, the labor inspector is responsible for receiving and resolving
employee complaints and denunciations of labor law violations as prescribed by law.
510. Grievance Handling Procedure. The section provides different scenarios with
responsibilities of grievances relating to the complaint handling linked to labor issues. The key
purpose of this exercise is to present the GRM process in an effective and user-friendly manner
(Table 93).

511. Grievances related to Gender Based Violence (GBV), SEA/SH. To avoid the risk of
stigmatization, exacerbation of mental/psychological harm, and potential reprisal, the GRM
shall have a different and sensitive approach to GBV/SEA/SH-related cases. The GRM equally
applies to workers who experience GBV/SEA/SH. Where such a case is reported to the GRM,
it should immediately be referred to the appropriate service providers, such as medical and
psychological support, emergency accommodation, and any other necessary services. It should
also be reported to the safeguard staff of the PMU, who can advise on relevant service providers.
Data on GBV/SEA/SH cases should not be collected through the GRM unless operators have
been trained on the empathetic, non-judgmental, and confidential collection of these complaints.
Only the nature of the complaint (what the complainant says in her/his own words) and
additional demographic data, such as age and gender, can be collected as usual. More details
are referred to the project LMP.

5.7.3. World Bank’s Grievance Redress Service (GRS)
512. Communities and individuals who believe that they are adversely affected by a WB-
supported project may submit complaints to the existing GRM of the project or the WB’s
Grievance Redress Service (GRS). The GRS ensures that complaints received are promptly
reviewed to address project-related concerns. Project-affected communities and individuals
may submit their complaints to the WB’s independent Inspection Panel, which determines
whether harms occurred, or could occur, as a result of WB's non-compliance with its policies
and procedures. Complaints may be submitted at any time after concerns have been brought
directly to the WB’s attention, and Bank Management has been allowed to respond. For
information on how to submit complaints to the WB’s corporate GRS, please visit
www.worldbank.org/grs. For information on how to submit complaints to the World Bank
Inspection Panel, please visit www.inspectionpanel.org




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    Page           241
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

CHAPTER 6.             PUBLIC CONSULTATION AND INFORMATION
                       DISCLOSURE
6.1. PUBLIC CONSULTATION
513. The WB’s ESS5 and ESS10 require consultation and notification to affected people and
local authorities on social and environmental issues throughout project implementation.
514. Public consultation for the ESIA report must also adhere to the requirements outlined in
Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP of the Government dated 10 January 2022 and Circular No.
02/2022/TT-BTNMT of the MONRE dated the same date, concerning environmental impact
assessment and the environmental protection plan.
515. The purpose of public consultation is to: (i) share information about the project's items
and activities with the community and stakeholders in the project area; and (ii) collect
comments and concerns about characteristics and environmentally and socially sensitive issues
in the project area from the government and local communities. On that basis, public concerns
will be included in the ESIA report and project mitigation measures.
516. Project stakeholders are defined as individuals, groups, or other entities who: (i) are
impacted or likely to be impacted directly or indirectly, positively or adversely, by the project
(also known as “affected parties”); and (ii) have an interest in the project (“interested parties”).
They include individuals or groups whose interests may be affected by the project and who
have the potential to influence the project outcomes in any way.
517. To conduct consultations for the project, open meetings, workshops, focus group
discussions, and interviews are utilized in the 3 stages of the project, including the preparation
stage (before project appraisal); project implementation stage (pre-construction and
construction), and operation stage. The public consultation strategy, which includes targeted
groups/attendees, subjects, formats, timeline, and responsibilities, is shown in Table 118.

6.1.1. During Preparation Phase

6.1.1.1. Public Consultation

a) Public Consultation Process

a.1). Consultations through Posting on the Website (tbc)
518. The project owner sent Official Letter No.196/ODA dated 13 June 2025 to the Ca Mau
DAE, requesting the publication of the project’s ESIA report on the DAE’s website.
 − Managing Agency of the Website: DAE.

 − Link to the Consultation Content: https://www.camau.gov.vn/tham-van/tham-van-bao-cao-
   danh-gia-tac-dong-moi-truong-du-an-phat-trien-thuy-san-ben-vung-tinh-ca-mau-284758
 − Date of consultation: 13 June 2025.

 − Duration of consultation: 15 days.

a.2). Consultations through Meetings
519. After completing the draft ESIA report, the project owner sent Document No. 07/ODA-
TSBV dated January 07, 2025, along with the draft report, to 5 communes of Tan Duc, Ta An
Khuong Dong, Dong Thoi, Phu Tan, and Phu My, and Rach Goc town, requesting coordination
to organize community consultation meetings on January 13-15, 2025.
520. The consultation results are summarized in Table 95. The list of participants and meeting
minutes is included in Appendix 7.
a.3). Consultations through Documents
521. In addition to the consultation meetings, the Project Owner also sent documents to the
People's Committee and Fatherland Front Committee of 6 project communes requesting their
opinions on the ESIA report. All communes, upon receiving the documents, provided their
feedback in writing to the project owner. The responses from the communes are attached in
Appendix 7.
522. Furthermore, in coordination with the project communes, the project owner carried out
written consultations with all affected households in February 2025. Every household expressed
strong support for the project and a desire for its swift implementation, as they have been
anticipating it for a long time.

6.1.1.2. Consultations with experts, Scientists, and Professional Organizations

524. The project does not fall under the category requiring consultation with experts, scientists,
and professional organizations.

6.1.2. During Implementation
525. Project phase. At this stage, the details of proposed project components and specific
project-affected stakeholders will be determined. The consultation will focus more specifically
on people who are affected by the project, especially those adversely impacted, to address their
concerns through the implementation of project-specific E&S plans. In addition, the project
will ensure that the GRM is disseminated and active to promptly address any grievances.
Special attention will be paid to vulnerable/disadvantaged groups to ensure inclusion, non-
discrimination, and access to project benefits. Consultations will also focus on job
opportunities, specific action plans, etc. (see Table 94).
526. Operation phase. At this stage, the consultation will focus on feedback from
stakeholders, particularly affected ones, on the performance of E&S impact and risk
mitigations, results of grievance resolution conducted during the implementation stage as well
as general project operations. The consultation on employment opportunities for operation and
maintenance (O&M) of the project works will be also conducted. Also, areas for improvement
and lessons learned should be identified for the project. Furthermore, dissemination and
reporting back to stakeholders on the outcome of the monitoring and evaluation of the project
will be also arranged (see Table 94).

6.2. INFORMATION DISCLOSURE
To comply with ESS10, draft E&S documents for the project (Resettlement Plan (RP), Ethnic
Minority Development Plan (EMDP), Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP),
Labor Management Procedure (LMP), and Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP)) in Vietnamese
were disclosed at Ca Mau PMU’s website on 24 August 2025. These documents in English
were disclosed on the WB’s external website on 24 August 2025. In addition, the project’s draft
ESIA will be disclosed locally at Ca Mau PPM’s website, the project sites, and the WB’s
external website on 05 December 2025. The final project safeguards instruments will be
disclosed locally at Ca Mau PMU’s website, the project sites, and the WB’s external website
on 15 December 2025 as well.




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          253
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

CONCLUSIONS, RECOMMENDATIONS, AND COMMITMENTS

1.    CONCLUSIONS
527. The fisheries sector is a key driver of Ca Mau province’s economic development.
Therefore, implementing this project to invest in infrastructure for aquaculture and fishing,
aligned with planning, is essential to promote the province’s and the region’s socio-economic
development, adapt to climate change, protect the environment, and gradually move toward
sustainable, high-quality, and diversified fisheries development.
528. The project serve the concentrated aquaculture areas along the coast of Ca Mau province.
It is of great importance, with significant spillover effects across districts, consistent with
regional and provincial plans, as well as Decision No. 1489/QD-TTg on attracting, managing,
and utilizing loan capital. In addition, the project aims to improve infrastructure and provide
equipment to ethnic minority communities in coastal areas, contributing to improved
livelihoods and living standards.
529. ESIA has identified both the potential negative and positive impacts and risks of the
project during 3 phases (preparation, construction and operation):
  − During preparation phase, the negative potential impacts and risks include (i) impacts due to
    land acquisition. About 88.20 ha of land will be used for the project, but only 35.39 ha will
    be acquired from 1,396 households, in which no household will be relocated; (iii) impacts
    due to UXOs. These impacts and risks can be mitigated by applying the approved
    resettlement plan (RP) of the project, especially voluntary land donation producures and the
    project owner will contract with relevant unit to clear UXOs before commencing
    construction activities, and must closely monitor and consult with local people about the
    land acquisition and clearance and UXO clearance.
  − During the construction phase, the project will generate a range of environmental and social
    impacts and risks. General impacts include dust, exhaust emissions, noise, and vibration
    from vehicles and machinery; generation of construction and domestic wastewater; solid and
    hazardous waste; excavated soil and dredged materials; and risks of surface water
    degradation. There will also be impacts on aquatic ecosystems and mangroves, disruption of
    local traffic, risks of localized flooding, land subsidence, and bank erosion during heavy
    rains, as well as pressures from raw material supply. Social issues may arise such as
    temporary loss of livelihoods, disturbance of community activities, risks from labor influx
    including gender-based violence (GBV), sexual exploitation and abuse/sexual harassment
    (SEA/SH), and poor working conditions. Additional risks involve community and worker
    health and safety, including accidents, communicable diseases, and fire or explosion
    hazards, along with potential impacts on physical cultural resources. Site-specific impacts
    include risks from temporary storage of dredged sludge, disruption of Rach Goc Fishing Port
    operations, impacts on water supply, disturbance to business activities along embankments,
    potential damage to current infracstructure (water intake culverts for shrimp pond, local
    houses, bridges) and localized effects at sensitive receptors. These impacts are rated as low
    to moderate and can be mitigated through the Environmental and Social Code of Practices
    (ESCOPs), Code of Conduct (CoC), mitigation measures for site-specific impacts and risks,
    other subplans (Dredging and Material Management Plan (DMMP), Traffic Management
    Plan, Community Health and Safety Plan), etc. Under the ESCOPs, contractors must control
    dust and exhaust emissions by water spraying, truck covering, and regular equipment
    maintenance. Noise and vibration will be mitigated by limiting pile-driving to daytime,
    installing barriers near sensitive receptors, and avoiding construction during school hours or
    religious events. Domestic wastewater from worker camps will be treated by septic tanks,
    while construction wastewater and equipment washing water will be directed to temporary
    sedimentation ponds. Solid and hazardous waste will be segregated, safely stored, and

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          254
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   transferred to licensed facilities. Worker camps must be provided with adequate sanitation,
   safe drinking water, and proper waste collection. Worker and community health and safety
   will be ensured by fencing and lighting at construction sites, provision of PPE, first-aid, and
   regular safety training. Importantly, all contractors are required to enforce a Code of Conduct
   (CoC) to prevent gender-based violence (GBV), sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA), and
   sexual harassment (SH), ensuring respectful behavior of workers towards local communities.
   In addition to ESCOPs, several specialized plans will be implemented. The DMMP will
   govern excavation, transport, storage, and reuse of dredged sludge, ensuring lined and
   drained temporary ponds and prioritizing reuse for embankments. The Traffic Management
   Plan will regulate transport routes, install signs and barriers, and ensure safe crossings for
   local residents. The Community Health and Safety Plan will provide procedures for accident
   prevention, communicable disease control, fire and explosion safety, and emergency
   response. For site-specific issues, such as disruptions at Rach Goc Port or damage to shrimp
   pond culverts, contractors must consult local authorities, arrange construction schedules to
   minimize disruption, promptly reinstate affected facilities, and compensate households when
   necessary.
 − During the operation phase, the project may cause dust, exhaust gases, noise, and vibration
   from traffic along embankments and vessel activities at the port, as well as wastewater and
   solid waste from port operations, fish handling, and local communities, leading to risks of
   surface water pollution and impacts on aquatic ecosystems. Specific risks include canal bank
   erosion and localized flooding due to altered drainage, potential damage to water intake
   culverts affecting shrimp aquaculture, and safety hazards such as road traffic accidents,
   vessel collisions, and fire or explosion incidents at the port. Mitigation during operation
   focuses on managing long-term environmental impacts and ensuring community safety.
   Dust, exhaust gases, noise, and vibration will be minimized by imposing vehicle speed
   limits, restricting heavy truck access at night, and planting trees along embankments.
   Wastewater generated at the port will be treated through an on-site system, while solid waste
   will be collected and transferred through designated services, prohibiting dumping into
   canals. To protect aquaculture and surface water quality, four monitoring stations will
   provide early warning, combined with awareness programs for local communities and
   farmers on pollution prevention. Localized flooding and erosion risks will be managed by
   maintaining and upgrading drainage culverts, inspecting embankments before the rainy
   season, and reinforcing canal banks with vegetation or gabions. Safety measures will include
   annual maintenance of port and embankment facilities, emergency response planning for
   vessel collisions, fires, and explosions, and improved traffic safety for farmers crossing
   roads.

2.    RECOMMENDATIONS
530. The Project Owner respectfully requests:
 − The relevant departments and agencies of Ca Mau province to assist in the project
   implementation.
 − Local environmental management authorities support providing capacity building on
   environmental protection for the project's workers and local communities.
 − All levels of the province support the project owner and contractors in workers management
   (if applicable) and responses in case of safety and environmental incidents.
 − Local authorities and security units support the project owner in ensuring security and traffic
   safety in the area.
 − The local authorities responsible for natural resources and the environment regularly inspect
   and provide specific guidance on environmental monitoring in the project area and the
   implementation of environmental commitments.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          255
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

3.     COMMITMENTS
 − The  project owner commits to complying with the current environmental protection
   regulations in Viet Nam: the Environmental Protection Law of 2020, related laws, and
   regulations (Government Decree 05/2025/ND-CP dated 06 January 2025, Decree No.
   08/2022/ND-CP dated January 10, 2022, Circular No. 07/2025/TT-BTNMT dated 28
   February 2025, Circular No. 02/2022/TT-BTNMT dated January 10, 2022), as well as other
   provisions during the project implementation.
 − The project owner commits to strictly implementing proposed mitigation measures outlined
   in the report and adhering to relevant national technical regulations during project
   implementation. Some key aspects include:
     ▪ Commitment to collect, transport, and treat all types of solid waste, hazardous waste, and
       household waste generated during the project implementation to ensure compliance with
       safety and environmental sanitation requirements stipulated in Decree 05/2025/ND-CP
       dated 06 January 2025, Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP dated January 10, 2022, Circular No.
       07/2025/TT-BTNMT dated 28 February 2025, Circular No. 02/2022/TT-BTNMT dated
       January 10, 2022.
     ▪ Commitment to implement construction organization measures and appropriate technical
       solutions to minimize dust, noise, water turbidity, and rainwater runoff during the project
       construction.
     ▪ Commitment to apply technical and management measures to ensure waste disposal
       meets environmental safety and sanitation requirements; and restore the environmental
       landscape of temporary land acquisition areas during project construction.
     ▪ Commitment to cooperate with relevant agencies to develop plans for preventing and
       responding to environmental incidents caused by project activities; implement awareness-
       raising measures for project workers regarding environmental protection.
     ▪ Commitment to implement an environmental management and monitoring program as
       outlined in the approved ESIA report and maintain data for state environmental protection
       management agencies to conduct inspections when necessary.
     ▪ Adherence to regulations on traffic safety, environmental sanitation, fire and explosion
       prevention, and labor safety during project implementation according to current legal
       provisions.
     ▪ Ensuring funding for environmental protection activities, and environmental monitoring
       programs, to fulfill commitments as outlined in the ESIA report.
 − Commitment to the accuracy and honesty of the information, data, and documents provided
   in the ESIA report.
 − Commitment that if any incidents occur during the implementation of the project that affect
   the environment, structures, and the health of workers and nearby residents, the project
   owner will provide compensation and remedy the consequences.
 − Commitment to ensure the feasibility of fulfilling the responsibilities of the project owner
   after the ESIA report is approved by the competent authority following legal regulations.
 − Adherence to regulations on traffic safety, environmental sanitation, fire prevention,
   explosion prevention, and labor safety during project implementation according to current
   legal provisions.
 − Ensuring funding for environmental protection activities, environmental monitoring, and
   surveillance programs, to fulfill commitments as outlined in the ESIA report.

                                           APPENDICE

APPENDIX 1: DUE DILIGENCE ASSESSMENT FOR RACH GOC
            FISHING PORT
The Rach Goc Fishing Port is located at the embankment of the Rach Goc River, in Rach Goc
Town of Ngoc Hien district in Ca Mau province. The Rach Goc Fishing Port was constructed
in 2009, completed in 2015, and put into operation in 2016. The Port has a land area of 2.4
hectares, a wharf length of 100 m, capable of receiving and unloading aquatic products for
approximately 4 vessels at once, 100 vessel arrivals/day, aquatic output 18,250 tons/year. It had
been designated as a Type II Port according to Decision No. 1912/QD-UBND dated October
12, 2020 by the Ca Mau PPC and designated as a fishing port under Decision No. 4407/QD-
BNN dated November 4, 2020 by MARD.
1. Accessibility
The Port is accessible through roadway and waterway. By roadway, the road to the port is 6m
wide, and there are 2 bridges on the road with a limited load of 8 tons (Figure PL.1). This leads
to limiting the number of trucks that can access the port. The local authority had a plan to
improve the road.




                   Figure PL. 1: Location of the Fishing Port and access road
2. Land Acquisition
In 2016, Ca Mau Provincial People’s Committee issued an official decision handing over more
than 24,623m² of land (equivalent to roughly 2.46 ha) at Quarter 6, Rach Goc Town, Ngoc Hien
District, for the port and storm shelter development. The acquired land was mainly from state
organizations and a few small business entities. The process involved small physical
resettlement, as most land was public or previously under government control. All acquisition
activities followed national legislation regarding land recovery for public benefit and socio-
economic development. To date, compensation and site clearance have been completed, and no
outstanding complaints related to land acquisition have been recorded as of this report.
3. UXO Clearance
The clearance of UXO had been fully completed for the entire onshore port area (2.466
hectares) and the water area in front of the port (24.158 hectares). A record of UXO clearance
is provided below.




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          258
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




                                Figure PL.2: Record of UXO clearance at the Port
4. Facilities at the Port and Staffing
Currently, the Port has 7 staff, including a manager, 5 professional staff, and a cleaner. Existing
facilities at the port include an office building, guardhouse, public restroom, warehouse,
receiving and sorting house, service land area, lighting system, water supply, wastewater
treatment system, internal roads, gate and fence, fire protection equipment, in:
  − A Sorting house: 270 m², solidly built, roofed, with electricity and water, divided into 4 units.

  − Reserve land: 6,631 m²; Production land (2,062 m²).

  − Some land is being leased (1,077 m² by Kim Son Petroleum Co., under construction), and
    A warehouse of 335 m²: Leased by Ly Minh Su private enterprise for seafood business. The
    port has sufficient functional warehouses, sanitation facilities, hand washing, and personal
    hygiene areas for workers to ensure food safety requirements.
  − The port facilities included a wastewater treatment system capacity: 250 m³/day. The
    treatment process comprises sedimentation, filtration, and biological treatment units,
    ensuring treated water meets local environmental standards if properly operated and
    maintained. However, since the port began operations, the volume of wastewater collected
    through the drainage system and sent to the treatment plant has been very low, only about
    0.3-1 m3/day. The reasons are: (i) the drainage system was not designed in a synchronized
    manner, resulting in limited wastewater being conveyed to the treatment plant (Figure PL.3);
    and (ii) the number of vessels loading and unloading at the port is very small, only around
    1–2 vessels per day. Therefore, when the port is upgraded and the number of vessels

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page          259
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   increases, the current capacity of the treatment plant will still be sufficient to meet the
   demand, and there will be no need to expand its capacity. However, it is necessary to improve
   the drainage system and conduct trial operations to ensure the plant functions properly before
   full operation or to identify any required repairs or upgrades to meet environmental
   requirements in the port area.
 − Clean water supply: The clean water supply at the port area comes from two sources: piped
   water and groundwater (with a total capacity of 200 m³/day). This water supply is sufficient
   to ensure stable port operations after the upgrade.




          Figure PL.3: Current wastewater drainage and collection system in the port
The number of vessels docking at the port is limited. From 2020 to February 2022, a total of
806 vessels docked at the port, with seafood throughput reaching 6,225 tons. The number of
vessels calling at the port remains low, mainly due to the access road having a load capacity
restriction of 8 tons with two narrow bridges, and vessels with an engine capacity over 400
horsepower (HP) cannot dock due to insufficient port capacity.
About 40-50 boats are moving on the river daily, with a width of the boats of 4-5m. However,
only about 20–25 boats dock at or surrounding the fishing port, while the rest are local vessels
traveling along the river (Figure PL.2). Among the boats that do dock, most come to complete
customs procedures, which typically take 25–40 minutes per vessel. After that, they usually
continue to private fish landing sites where trucks over 8 tons can access to offload and sell
their catch. Only about 1–2 boats conduct fish trading directly at the port (Figure PL.3). After
docking at the alternative site, boat owners can visit the port to complete customs clearance
procedures.




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          260
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




                        Figure PL. 4: Boats moving on the Rach Goc river




                         Figure PL. 5: Fish trading at the Rach Goc port
5. Anchorage area and Storm Shelter
There is an anchorage area spanning along both banks of the river at Rach Goc with 180
mooring posts distributed over a 4-kilometer stretch (Figure PL.4). Specifically, 83 posts are
located on the Rach Goc Town side, and 97 posts are on the Tan An side. Currently, 16 of these
mooring posts are reported as damaged. Therefore, these posts and 3 big private docks along
the Rach Goc River can be used for temporary boat parking during construction phase (Figure
PL.5).




                        Figure PL. 6: Mooring posts in the Rach Goc port




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page        261
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




                    Figure PL. 7: Alternative docks along the Rach Goc River
6. Waste and Wastewater Generation, Treatment, and Management
  − Total daily wastewater generated: 5.67m³/day (0.4 m³ from port staff and 5 m³ from 1 versel
    per day. This volume is significantly below the wastewater treatment system’s design
    capacity of 250m³/day, indicating that the facility is more than adequate for current
    operational needs. Solid Waste Generation: about 33.5kg/day, including
     • Staff domestic waste: Approximately 0.5kg/person/day × 7 staff = 3.5kg/day.
     • Processing waste: 1 vessel × 30kg/vessel/day = 30kg/day.
There is are existing combined drainage system at the port; however, water from boat parking
at the port is being discharged directly into the river.
The port has a wastewater treatment plant, but it is currently out of service because of the low
wastewater volume. The Port Management Board plans to assess the plant and rehabilitate it to
ensure it operates effectively prior to the completion of port upgrading.




            Figure PL. 8: Wastewater treatment station/plant at the Rach Goc Port
7. Recommendations
Therefore, to enhance the port's operational efficiency after upgradation, the following
recommendations are made:
  − UXO clearance is not required during the port upgrade process.

  − Improve the stormwater and wastewater collection system in the port area after the upgrade.

    Check the wastewater treatment system and repair any damages if necessary to ensure proper
    operation once the fishing port upgrade is completed.




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page          262
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT



APPENDIX 2: DREDGING AND MATERIAL MANAGEMENT PLAN
            (DMMP)
This DMMP is prepared in the stage of Feasibility Study (FS). It will be updated by the Detailed
Design Consultant, then integrated into the bidding documents and the contracts for dredging
at the Rach Goc Port and canals, along with ESCOP, worker code of conduct, and other
mitigation requirements stated in ESMP. After signing the contract, the Contractor will prepare
the Contractor’s DMMP based on the DMMP in the Contract and submit it to the Project
Owner/Supervising Consultant for approval before implementation. Besides, this DMMP
proposed biological/water quality mitigation measures through the selection of dredging
method and processes; mitigation measures and environmental quality monitoring program
covered biological monitoring.
1.    Introduction
The Ca Mau project covers the dredging and excavation activities. There will be negative
environmental and social impacts during the construction phase. The project will also support
the upgrading of 76.72 km of existing embankment, which has been combined as rural roads.
Therefore, the DMMP is prepared to control all impacts and risks caused during the project’s
dredging. This plan is to focus on dredging and embankment activities, and figure out solutions
to the dredged materials generated from the remaining items of this project.
The DMMP covers the following contents:
   1. Introduction
   2. Scope and Method of Dredging and Excavation
   3. Current Status of the Rach Goc River and Canals to Be Dredged
   4. Characteristics of Materials Generated
   5. Proposed Material Storage Sites
   6. Summary about Potential Environmental and Social Impacts And Risks
   7. Impact and Risk Mitigation Plan
   8. Environmental Monitoring and Safety Plan
2.    Scope and Method of Dredging and Excavation
2.1. Scope of Dredging
The Project would support:
 − The dredging of 10,466 m2 of water area front at the Rach Goc Port
 − Dredging 14.81 km of 6 in-field canals with bottom width B=10 m to the depth of -2 to 2.5m,
   including Moi-Dong Tren canal route from Dong
   Tren Bridge to Tam Bo intersection, Xom La
   canal route from Mieu intersection to Ong Rum
   intersection, Tu Hang canal route, Ba Lai canal
   route, Lung Mon Bo Cang route and Mera Bo
   Cang Bridge route.
2.2. Methods of Dredging and Excavation
 −   Dredging the Rach Goc fishing port: the Rach Goc
     River will be dredged by using a hydraulic grab dredger mounted on a 500-ton pontoon.
     Mark the dredging boundaries with buoys. The dredged sediment will be loaded onto a 400-
     ton barge and transported to the designated disposal site located approximately 300m away.
     From there, onboard pumps will convey the material to the disposal yard over a distance of

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           Page          263
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

    300 meters. This method has been selected with careful consideration of potential impacts
    and risks to water quality and usage.
  − Dredging 6 canal routes: Dredging will be carried out using a combination of excavators
    (bucket capacity 0.5 m³, 0.65 m³, and 0.8 m³) to take the mud from the canal bed and place
    it directly onto the canal embankment
      ▪ Excavators will also be used to excavate the top soil layer on the side of residential land
          to create an excavated material containment area. This area will have a top width of 0.5
          m and a height up to the designed crest elevation of the embankment, preventing the
          material from overflowing into
          residents’ farmland or gardens. Every
          200 meters, a drainage channel 0.5 m
          wide will be installed to direct runoff
          back into the canal during rainfall,
          thereby avoiding overflow into fruit
          gardens or shrimp ponds.
      ▪ Dredgers with bucket capacities of
          0.5m³, 0.65 m³, and 0.8 m³ will be used
          to dredge the canal and deposit the
          dredged material onto the project
          embankments. Initially, the material
          will be placed along the canal slopes to
          allow excess water to drain back into
          the canal. Once partially dewatered,
          another excavator will relocate the
          material to a containment area approximately 2–3 m away. At this point, the material is
          mostly dry. During placement, the contractor will ensure the fill height is increased
          gradually to avoid localized overloading, which could lead to ground subsidence or
          slope failure.
It is anticipated that the following machines and equipment will be mobilized for dredging and
excavation (Table PL 1):
                Table PL 1: Machines, Equipment for Dredging and Excavation

                                       (pcs)                                         (pcs)

3.   Current Status of the Rach Goc River and Canals to Be Dredged
The Rach Goc River (35–40 km long, 80–150 m wide, 4–8 m deep) flows into the East Sea via
the Rach Goc Estuary and is a key transport and drainage route. About 40–50 boats travel the
river daily, with 1–2 vessels berthing at the port for customs. Nearby sensitive receptors include
a coffee shop, a grocery store, an aquaculture seedling facility, and a local house. The dredging
area within the port is shown in Figure PL 1.




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         264
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




              Figure PL 1: Water area to be dredged in front of the Rach Goc Port
The project will dredge 10–15 m wide canals supplying water and drainage for local
aquaculture (Figure PL 2). These rural canals have sparse populations, with houses 10–20 m
from the banks. The Shrine of the Goddess is the only sensitive receptor identified.




                                Figure PL 2: Canals to Be Dredged
4.   Characteristics of Materials Generated
4.1. Volume of Excavation and Dredged Materials

About 75,077 m³ of excavated materials from the embankments and 569,416 m³ of dredged
material from the Rach Goc port and canals will be generated.
4.2. Quality
Sediment and soil samples were taken in December 2024 in the course of ESIA preparation to
analyze pH, salinity, organic pollutants, and heavy metals. Sampling positions are shown in
Figure PL 3.

The analysis results show that soil quality (Table PL 2) meets the requirements of QCVN

       (Pb) concentrations ranged from 18.0 mg/kg to 42.3 mg/kg, all well below the allowable
       limit for agricultural soils (70 mg/kg for Pb) under QCVN 03:2023/BTNMT.

5.    Proposed Material Storage Sites
For the Rach Goc Fishing Port, the dredged material will be temporarily stored in an 8,763m²
designated area within the port and in the back-up dead-end ditch for future use. For the canal
dredging, the material will be transported 5–7 km by barges to the embankment upgrading site,
where it will be temporarily stored before being reused for backfilling.
6.    Summary about Potential Environmental and Social Impacts And Risks
6.1. Overall
Chapter 4 of the Environmental and Social Impact Assessment analyzes environmental and
social impacts from dredging, excavation, and embankment, including:
 − Dredging activities and dredged material transportation:

    ▪ Generate dust, gas emission, wastewater, and about 644,493m3 of excavated and
        dredged materials.
    ▪ Disturb water bodies, increase turbidity, affect aquatic species, and water used for
        aquaculture, interrupt or reduce the yield of aquaculture.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          268
                     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

      ▪ Interrupt waterway traffic and increase waterway safety risk.
     ▪ Limit people’s access to the river and canals.
     ▪ Risks posed to safety and health for workers and local communities.
     ▪ Risks of accidents and incidents.
  − Disposal activities:

     ▪ Risks of material spill.
     ▪ Risks of inundation in the surrounding area due to water drainage from the material
        storage site.
     ▪ Safety risk for the community
In addition, specific impacts and risks are also posed by each construction position. The
following table describes some typical impacts and risks of material dredging, transportation,
and gathering at the material storage site.

6.3. Impacts and risks in the material storage sites
In general, the dredged and excavated materials generated are 644,493m3. At the single material
storage sites, there will be the following main potential socio-environmental impacts and risks:
  − Spill and Erosion risks: When the disposal area is significantly elevated above the
    surrounding ground, dredged material may spill downslope into adjacent areas, potentially
    filling vacant or productive land, covering vegetation or infrastructure, and affecting local
    livelihoods and agricultural activities. In addition, exposure of barren soils to air and wind
    can increase the potential for erosion.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page           270
                      ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

     − Localised flooding risks. Materials disposed of at the material storage site may interrupt the
       existing drainage pattern. Rainwater will overflow on top of the materials, then run
       downslope to the surroundings and may cause localised flooding. On the other hand, after
       the wet material is pumped into the material storage site, leakage water will be drained out.
       It may contain high turbidity, thus it would cause water quality reduction of the receiving
       water bodies, affecting the growth of aquatic species.
     − Water leakage generation and impacts. After being pumped from the barges to a material
       storage site, the wet dredged materials will settle down gradually, and water will be drained
       from the material. If not controlled properly, leakage water may cause localised flooding in
       the surroundings of the material storage sites. This leakage water may contain relatively high
       turbidity, causing sedimentation in the drains and receiving water bodies. In addition,
       although the results of soil and sediment quality analyses in the dredging area show no
       evidence of acid sulfate soils, the area itself is known to be prone to such conditions.
       Therefore, acid sulfate may be exposed due to excavation and dredging activities. If this
       occurs, the water leakage containing acid may be discharged into the environment, which
       could degrade water quality and adversely affect aquatic life as well as local shrimp farming
       operations that take water from the dredged rivers and canals. In addition, heavy metals
       contained in the sediment and soil may be mobilized into the water column during dredging
       and excavation. Nevertheless, substantial impacts are not expected because the
       concentrations of these pollutants are well below applicable standards.
     − Overflow/spreading of dredged materials to the surroundings. Each material storage site will
       have a specified design height and containment area. However, when materials are dumped
       at the edge or top of the disposal area, they may overflow beyond the site boundaries. In
       addition, slopes formed at the dump site may fail under load, causing materials to spill
       outside designated areas, potentially covering vegetation, obstructing existing infrastructure,
       or even posing accidents to people.
     − Safety risks to local communities. Because the barges will be stationed at some distance
       from the final material storage sites, pipelines will be installed to transport materials to these
       locations. Both the pipelines themselves and the wet materials discharged from them may
       pose safety risks to local communities, including potential collisions with the pipes or
       accidents resulting from pipe failures. Furthermore, after the materials are deposited, it will
       take some time for the ground at the material storage site to stabilize as excess moisture
       drains from the dredged material. Entering these unstable disposal areas could lead to
       accidents such as sinking or even drowning.
               Table PL 6: Site-specific Impacts and Risks at the Material Storage Sites

                                                              canal and Rach Goc river.

7.     Impact and Risk Mitigation Plan
In the course of construction, Contractor will comply with requirements and follow
environmental and social impact mitigation measures stated in the Environmental and Social
Code of Practice (ESCOP), Worker Code of Conduct, which are specified in Chapter 5. The
dredging and embankment Contractor will comply with measures and requirements prescribed
in this DMMP, including the following requirements:
7.1.    Personnel arrangements
In the bidding documents, the Contractor shall be required to allocate at least 1 officer who has
a degree, certificates, and experience to take charge of safety, environment, and health for the
Package, which is under the Contractor’s responsibility. These officers must be mobilized and
work during the contract period to conduct the monitoring, prepare reports for their assigned
tasks. The Contractor shall create favorable conditions for these officers to participate in
training courses that are beneficial for their tasks held by the Project.
7.2. Design of Material Storage Sites
 −   Design of material storage for the port: The disposal area is designed with three
     compartments surrounded by embankments of +3m, including drainage channels to collect
     water and discharge it into the Rach Goc River after settling. Details are in Table PL 4.




                  Figure PL 4: Design of the material storage area for the port
 −   Design of material storage for the canals: The dredged and excavated soil will be deposited
     into designated compartments located alongside the canals. The embankment is 0.5 meters
     wide, 2.5 meters high, and 200 meters long. They are separated from adjacent lands by
     embankments and have a ditch 0.5m wide between them to drain water from the soil (see
     Figure PL 5 and Figure PL 6).

7.3. Mitigation Measures to be Implemented by Dredging Contractors
To manage the potential socio-environmental impacts and health and safety risks associated
with the dredging process, including the transportation and disposal of materials, the Contractor
is required to implement and/or comply with the following requirements during the execution
of the dredging contracts:
 − The mitigation measures specified in the detailed design and technical specifications of the
   bidding documents, including the dredging methods, transportation processes, and material
   storage sites, must be strictly implemented. Contractors are not permitted to propose
   alternative dredging methods, particularly the use of suction pumps to discharge liquid mud
   directly from the riverbed to the material storage sites, as such practices could increase
   potential adverse environmental and social impacts and risk.
 − All Environmental, Social, Health and Safety (ESHS) terms and conditions specified in the
   bidding documents and contracts, including the ESCOP, and the Workers’ Code of Conduct.
 − Assign staff responsible for managing ESHS aspects, including the operation of the
   Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM).
 − Plan dredging schedules to avoid the rainy season and periods when local shrimp ponds take
   in water.
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          273
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

 − In coordination with the PMU and CSC, consult with local communities and authorities—
   especially people engaged in aquaculture, or waterway transport—when preparing the
   contractor’s detailed construction schedules. Reach agreements with affected households on
   water intake and stocking times for aquaculture ponds. The contractor may need to adjust or
   temporarily suspend dredging at certain times of the day to allow residents to draw river
   water if needed.
 − Coordinate with the PMU to notify local communities and authorities along each river/canal
   section of the dredging schedule at least two weeks before commencement.
 − Prepare the Contractor’s DMMP and submit it to the CSC for review and approval. Update
   the C-DMMP every six months or whenever new issues arise.
 − Develop the Contractor’s Emergency Preparedness Plan to address potential waterway
   traffic incidents or accidents.
 − Register transportation routes, equipment, and construction timelines with the relevant
   authorities to provide a basis for inspection and monitoring of dredging, transportation, and
   disposal activities.
 − Ensure that operators of dredgers, excavators, and barges are properly trained and hold valid
   certificates, which will be verified by the CSC.
 − Provide all workers with appropriate PPE and strictly enforce its use—especially life vests
   for those working over or near water. Assign safety monitors for each shift to ensure a timely
   response in case of emergencies.
 − Conduct ESHS training for workers before the commencement of any physical works and
   provide weekly ESHS briefings thereafter.
 − Implement all measures outlined in the approved Waterway Traffic Control Plan prior to
   dredging. Install and maintain warning signs, including buoys on the water surface and signs
   along river/canal banks, in accordance with the approved plans.
 − Equip barges with signal lights for safe navigation.

 − Conduct daily monitoring of water quality at dredging locations, including turbidity
   measurements. Establish acceptable turbidity thresholds with the CSC, and suspend
   dredging activities when thresholds are exceeded or when harm to aquatic species is
   observed, resuming only after turbidity returns to acceptable levels. Limit dredging during
   peak tides to minimize the dredged material and pollutant dispersion.
 − Store only minimal quantities of oil and lubricants on barges in watertight, sealed containers
   within covered and bunded areas to prevent spills into river water. Collect and store oil-
   contaminated materials in secure containers for disposal by licensed hazardous waste
   contractors.
 − Inspect and maintain all equipment and machinery regularly to ensure proper functioning
   and renew registrations before their expiry dates.
 − Major maintenance or repair of excavators is prohibited at the dredging/construction site;
   such work must be carried out at designated maintenance workshops.
 − Overloading of dredged materials on barges, storage areas, or any transport means is strictly
   prohibited.
 − Move dredgers, excavators, and barges to safe shelters before the flood season, and cover or
   secure equipment to prevent fuel leakage or damage from storm winds.
 − Safely remove all equipment and materials from the water and riverbanks upon completion
   of dredging works.
 − Provide riverside accommodations with adequate clean water, electricity, sanitation
   facilities, and basic amenities to ensure worker health and safety. Register all workers with
   local authorities.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          274
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

 −  Facilitate inspections by the IESMC and relevant authorities.
  − Maintain contractor’s logbooks on ESHS performance and submit monthly ESHS
    implementation reports.
In addition, the contractor should:
  − Prepare specific construction methods in each river/canal section that fit the current status
    of the river/canal in terms of water flow, sediment levels, production activities, impact of
    tides and flooding flow, etc.
  − Set up a construction schedule for each location with consideration into: (i) water level, flow
    level, weather conditions; (ii) recommendations of local people and authorities on their
    demand of using water and surface water for aquaculture; and (iii) construction schedule for
    other packages which share the same construction site, inclusive of transportation route and
    material storage site.
  − Mitigation of safety risks for workers and construction equipment to avoid adverse natural
    conditions such as storms, floods, and natural disasters.
  − Coordinate with the Project Owner and supervising consultant to maintain ongoing
    communication with local authorities and communities during both construction planning
    and implementation phases.
  − The Contractor shall be fully responsible for repairing and compensating, at its own cost,
    any damage caused by construction activities.
  − Disturbance to the ground and on the riverbed/canal bed is kept to a minimum.

  − When storms are forecasted, limit dredging and embankment lining activities, implement
    site protection measures, and suspend all construction during stormy weather.
  − Conduct dredging at a low, acceptable speed and in intervals to allow sediment to settle.

  − Install clear warning signs at hazardous areas, such as underwater flows, areas with high
    erosion potential, or deep excavation zones.
  − Excavate and preserve existing vegetation for later use to cover the surface of dredged
    materials at the material storage sites upon completion.
Mitigation measures for the impacts on aquatic ecosystems:
 − Prepare and implement an appropriate dredging and excavation process to minimize
   disturbance to the ecosystem of the dredging areas.
 − Carry out dredging and excavation strictly within the approved design locations and follow
   a consecutive construction method to avoid prolonged disturbance and turbidity that could
   affect the aquatic ecosystems of the Rach Goc River and canals.
 − Request owners of dredging and excavation machinery/equipment to manage, collect, and
   have proper treatment for wastewater in accordance with the existing regulations on
   discharge
 − Request owners of ships and barges to equip oil separators before discharging the wastewater
   into the environment, or to collect wastewater in confined tanks and contract with a
   functional unit to collect and treat when ships/barges are docked.
 − Request the owner of navigation means not to clean the decks of ships and barges in the
   dredging area to avoid discharging wastewater into the river/canals, leading to an increase
   in water pollution, which can affect the lives of aquatic organisms.
 − These requirements will be specified in the bidding documents between the project owner
   and the construction contractor; if the contractor violates them, there will be sanctions to
   handle according to regulations.
 − During the dredging process, attention should be paid to factors affected by extreme weather
   such as high winds, storms, currents, water levels, etc., so that the dredging can be

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         275
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

     temporarily stopped to minimize damage and impacts on water quality and aquatic life in
     the river and canal.

Mitigation measures for aquacultural production:
 − For aquaculture farming, PMU will work closely with contractors, local authorities, and
   potentially affected households to optimize the dredging operations – in a manner that
   balances technical requirements for dredging, meanwhile minimizing the adverse impact on
   the aquaculture of local people. At the same time, safety measures will be taken by the
   contractors, such as the use of zoning buoys to delineate construction areas to prevent
   potential risks of collision of passing vessels with dredging equipment operational on the
   river and canals.
 − For aquaculture households, the project agrees with local people on the time when water is
   allowed into their aquaculture farm, as well as the time when shrimp are stocked into ponds.
   Farming season is arranged with local households to ensure construction activities will not
   take in a distance that adversely affects the shrimp farming activities of local households by
   ensuring that the river water to be taken in aquaculture farming is not affected by turbidity
   due to dredging, for instance. National technical standards required for brackish water
   shrimp farming (TCVN 13656:2023) on conditions of veterinary hygiene, environmental
   protection, and food safety will be used to monitor this arrangement.
 − During dredging construction, contractors will adopt relevant construction methods, such as
   dredging on a rolling basis, using a minimum of dredging equipment to minimize any
   potential risks and impacts to aquaculture and vessel traffic. Consultation will also be
   conducted with affected people based on the final technical design for dredging activities.
7.4. Preparation of C-DMMP by Contractor
After contract award, the Contractor must prepare and submit the Contractor’s DMMP (C-
DMMP) together with construction schedules and technical plans. The C-DMMP should follow
the structure of this annex and provide detailed descriptions of the measures the Contractor will
implement to manage ESHS aspects in line with the principles outlined in the ESMP and
bidding documents. Special attention should be given to:
 − Clearly differentiate between dredging the river/canal bed and canal side excavation,
   specifying the equipment and labor to be used for each activity.
 − Provide detailed procedures for dredging and excavation operations, the transport of dredged
   and excavated materials, and their disposal at designated sites.
 − Allocation of dredged and excavated materials to each storage site will be determined based
   on updated information on the sediment quality of the river and canals and soil conditions at
   the disposal sites, which will become available during detailed design.
7.5. Requirements for Storage Activities and Material Storage Site Management
 −   Storage Process:
      ▪ The Contractor shall detail in the C-DMMP all arrangements for connecting barges,
        which may be required to park at a distance, to the material storage sites.
      ▪ If pipelines are installed to connect the barges to the Material Storage Sites for pumping
        liquid mud, the C-DMMP shall include mitigation measures, such as installing warning
        signs along the pipeline route.
      ▪ All approved mitigation measures for the material storage sites—including
        embankments, outlets, drainage ditches, warning signs, and fences (if required)—shall
        be fully implemented before any disposal activities commence.
      ▪ Pumping of materials into cells and the drainage of any leakage water shall follow the
        procedures and sequence outlined in the C-DMMP and/or construction plan.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page          276
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

        ▪   Regular monitoring shall be conducted at the Material Storage Sites. Any leakage or
            overflow must be addressed immediately before disposal activities can continue.
   −   Material Storage Site Management: The Project will comply with the regulations stated
       in Article 8: Regulations on Dredged Material Management under Decree No. 57/2024/ND-
       CP, dated May 20, 2024, issued by the Government of Viet Nam. This Decree governs the
       management of dredging activities in seaport waters and inland waterway areas, including
       the handling, transportation, and disposal of dredged materials.
   −   Incident control:
        ▪ The design of the material storage sites shall account for potential risks such as erosion,
          flooding, subsidence, natural disasters, and storms.
        ▪ A comprehensive incident control plan for the subproject shall be established, covering
          incidents related to dredging, transportation, and disposal at the designated material
          storage sites.
        ▪ An incident response team of at least three personnel shall be formed and kept ready to
          respond promptly to any incident.
        ▪ Hotlines or other communication channels shall be established to ensure rapid reporting
          of incidents, accessible to both project staff and local communities.

8.2. Compliance Monitoring Plan and Reporting Requirements
The Contractor shall be responsible for complying with all requirements and implementing
mitigation measures outlined in the ESIA, ESMP, and DMMP, and for preparing monthly
reports. ESHS compliance reports shall be prepared using the Project’s prescribed forms during
implementation, in accordance with the requirements of the bidding documents, and submitted
to the Project Owner/Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) for review and approval. The
reports shall cover:
  − Description of the package’s working scope for the reporting period.

  − Progress on the C-ESMP, C-DMMP, and other environmental-related documents submitted
    to the Project Owner.
  − Implementation status of the construction schedule, including construction sites and work
    volumes completed during the month.
  − Mitigation actions implemented during the reporting period.

  − Existing or newly arisen environmental, social, safety, or health issues.

  − ESHS training sessions conducted.

Stakeholders’ inspecting and monitoring activities: The Construction supervising consultant
shall be responsible for daily monitoring of the Contractor’s compliance and preparing a
monthly monitoring report. An Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant
will conduct quarterly monitoring during the construction phase.
