APPENDIX 5: OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH AND SAFETY (OHS) PLAN
1. Objectives
The objectives of this Annex OHS plan are to guide the contractors on:
    a. Risk Mitigation. To identify and control hazards to ensure a safe working environment.
    b. Risk control. To provide risk control measures based on the hierarchy of controls
    c. Regulatory Compliance. To assess and plan for compliance with all relevant OHS
         regulations and standards, including World Bank Environmental Safety Health
         Guidelines (ESHGs) and Good Industrial International Practice (GIIP)
    d. Monitoring and Emergency Preparedness. To assess and plan for continuous OHS
         monitoring, reporting, and emergency response plans.
     e. Health and Community Safety. To assess and plan for worker health and welfare, and
         mitigate impacts on community safety.
2. Roles and responsibilities
The following roles and responsibilities will be defined for the implementation of the OHS
plan:
 − Project Manager (PMU): Overall responsibility for OHS implementation and compliance.

 − OHS Supervisor (CSCs): Responsible for monitoring and reporting OHS compliance.

 − Contractors: Ensure all work is carried out according to the OHS plan and risk assessments.
   Sub-contractors: Must comply with the OHS requirements and participate in training and
   safety meetings. The contractors will undertake the following responsibilities:
    • Employ or appoint qualified staff to undertake necessary actions and measures to ensure
       labor-related issues, such as occupational safety and health expert(s), to managed OHS
       issues.
    • Prepare a labor management procedure (Contractor’s OHS plan) – is a partly C-ESMP
       (Contractor’s ESMP) including OHS regulations16 , which will apply to their contracted
       workers who work on the projects.
    • Submit the Contractors’ OHS plan and C-ESMP to PMU/CSC for review and approval
       prior to commencing staff mobilization to the project site for the awarded assignments.
    • Supervise subcontractors’ construction works, including their implementation of the
       Contractor’s OHS plan, included in the C-ESMP.
    • Maintain recruitment and employment records for contracted workers (including
       subcontractors), including documentation that verifies minimum labor age as outlined in
       this OHS plan.
    • Provide regular training to contracted workers on issues, but not limited to, such as
       occupational safety and health, and other social risks, such as SEA/SH, code of conduct
       to maintain good relationships with the local community, etc.
    • Require the primary supplier to identify and address risks of child labor, forced labor,
       SEA/SH, and occupational safety and health for primary supply workers.
    • Develop and implement the grievance mechanism based on the GRM set forth in this
       OHS plan for contracted workers, including ensuring that grievances received from their
       contracted workers are resolved promptly, and reporting the status of grievances and
       resolutions to CSC and PMU. This grievance mechanism will be part of the Contractor’s
       OHS plan.
    • Ensure that all contractor and subcontractor workers understand and sign the Code of
       Conduct prior to the commencement of works,




16
     The Contractor’s LMP and the Contractor’s ESMP may be prepared as separate documents or integrated into a single document.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                             Page                 290
                            ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
       • Implement all necessary measures to address the risks of sexual exploitation and abuse
         (SEA)/sexual harassment (SH) as specified in the contractor’s OHS plan/C-ESMP and
         ensure full implementation of these measures.
       • Report to PMU any accident that happens in their construction workplace and on the
         performance of labor and occupational safety and health.
  3. Regulatory compliance and WB requirements
  Compliance with Vietnamese OHS laws is mandatory. Additionally, all contractors and
  subcontractors must adhere to the World Bank’s Environmental Safety Health Guidelines
  (ESHGs) and Good Industrial International Practice (GIIP). These guidelines ensure that
  international best practices are followed in health and safety management.
  a. Compliance with Vietnamese OHS laws:
  Occupational health and Safety (OHS): Pursuant to the relevant provisions of the Labor Code
  (Chapter IX on Labor Safety, Labor Sanitation Articles 132-134), Law on Occupational Health
  and Safety, ESS2 (including WBG General Environmental, Health and Safety Guidelines
  (EHSGs17)), the Project’s ESMP and WB standard procurement documents18, the contractor
  shall manage all construction sites in such a way that the workers and the community are
  properly protected against possible OHS risks. Key elements of OHS measures should include
  (a) identification of potential hazards to workers; (b) provision of preventive and protective
  measures; (c) training of workers and maintenance of training records; (d) documentation and
  reporting of occupational accidents and incidents; (e) emergency preparedness.
  Fatality and serious incidents. In the event of an occupational fatality or serious injury, the
  PMU shall report to the Bank within 48 hours of such an incident occurring, and inform the
  government authorities (where available) in accordance with national reporting requirements
  (Law on Occupational Health and Safety, Chapter III, section 1). The PMU or, where relevant,
  the contractor, will be required to conduct a cause analysis for designing and implementing
  further corrective actions.
  b. Compliance with WB’s ESF and Guidelines on OHS:
    − ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions

    − The World Bank’s Environmental Safety Health Guidelines (ESHGs) and Good Industrial
      International Practice (GIIP).

4.   Risk assessment, hazard identification, risk analysis, and risk control

 a. The contractor is legally required to protect employees and others from harm by assessing
    the likelihood and severity of identified hazards and determining appropriate control
    measures. While it is impossible to eliminate all risks, it is crucial to manage the main risks
    effectively, balancing the level of risk against the measures needed in terms of money, time,
    or effort. Risk assessments should be regularly reviewed, at least every three months, or
    when new equipment, improvements, unidentified hazards, accidents, incidents, near
    misses, or new substances and procedures are introduced. The risk assessment process
    involves identifying potential hazards, evaluating the likelihood and severity of harm, and
    taking action to eliminate or control the risks.
 b. Risk controls. Risk controls should be considered using the OHS hierarchy of controls.
    Elimination involves physically removing the hazard to prevent exposure. Substitution
    entails replacing the hazard with safer equipment or tools. Isolation separates the hazard
    from people by marking off areas or installing barriers. Engineering controls use machinery
    or devices to remove the hazard, such as mechanical aids, guards, safety switches,
    production line adjustments, or sound-dampening measures. Administrative controls
    involve changing processes or task methods, relying on workers to follow safety
    procedures. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is the last line of defense, providing
    protection but often being forgotten, ill-fitting, or inadequate.
 c. Before starting any high-risk activities, arrangements must be documented in writing,
    typically through a method statement generated from a risk assessment. This statement
    addresses identified risks and communicates the safe system of work, especially for
    complex or unusual tasks (e.g., using hazardous substances, operating near overhead lines,
    blasting, confined spaces). It outlines the job's execution in a logical sequence, ensuring
    health and safety by including all control measures and necessary resources. While similar
    operations may use similar statements, significant changes (e.g., in blasting) require
    revisions. Method statements effectively inform employees about work execution and
    safety precautions. Risk assessments should be reviewed regularly, incorporating
    information from previous tasks, accident records, and investigations to ensure adequate
    precautions.

In general, risks related to occupational accidents at the sites are low or medium and mitigable
by suitable solutions such as training on labor safety before and during the construction process
and providing adequate labor equipment for workers. Below are the OHS risks and mitigation
measures for each sub-project construction item.

5. OHS targets. To maintain momentum in health and safety, set measurable objectives and
targets, and outline actions, responsibilities, and dates. Continuously strive to achieve these
targets and measure performance against them. Implement new initiatives, such as rewards for
good safety actions or setting a maximum number of days between accidents, to maintain high
standards. Use risk control measures to achieve targets like minimal or zero accidents and
incidents. Track accidents and incidents using site data, designate responsible persons, and set
specific dates to ensure accountability and progress.
6. Other considerations
a. The work permit system supports method statement actions and serves as a risk control
   measure for activities like hot works, excavations, and confined space work.
b. Management of subcontractors' OHS involves detailing how to achieve compliance with risk
   assessments and supervision of sub-tasks.
c. Health and Safety management at the project site includes managing hazardous materials by
   listing and assessing risks, identifying control measures, and allocating responsible persons.
   Access and site security require risk assessments for encroachment and equipment security.
   Site safety rules should be based on risk assessments and included in the annex. Workplace
   inspections need to specify what, when, how, and who will inspect, with checklists in the
   annex. First aid provisions should address minor and major injuries. Worker welfare facilities
   must include sanitation, washing facilities, shaded areas, and drinking water. Site induction
   and training should be based on a training gap analysis and documented. Emergency and
   incident management should refer to the C-ESMP plan. Road safety and traffic management
   plans should be based on risk assessments and identified deficiencies.
d. Communication and information sharing involve establishing HS meetings/committees,
   conducting inspections, and using reports, toolbox talks, signage, and notice boards to
   review and improve OHS performance. Internal GRM and external consultations with
   media, WB, RDA, road safety authorities, and the local population are essential. Workforce
   consultation should address employee representatives' questions and concerns on HS.
   Accident, incident, and near-miss reporting and investigation procedures should include
   templates in the annex, with data analysis to prevent future incidents and improve safety
   culture.
e. Incidents to be reported include HS hazards, near misses, injuries, illnesses, diseases,
   environmental incidents, equipment damage or loss, community complaints, corporate
   image impacts, and non-compliance issues. Incident reports should include the time, date,
   location, and name of the person who identified the incident; a description and investigation
   of the incident; the cause; actual and potential impacts; and corrective actions to prevent
   recurrence. Identifying and eliminating unsafe acts, and reporting near misses and minor
   injuries, are crucial to preventing lost-time injuries and fatalities.
f. Revisions of the OHS Plan. Regularly update the OHS plan with the date of revision, the
   person responsible, and details of the revisions made.




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                Page          294
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

APPENDIX 6: TRAFFIC MANAGEMENT PLAN
The contractor of the Port will be required to prepare a Waterway Traffic Management Plan
and submit it to the relevant authorities for review and approval prior to construction
commencement. The Traffic Management Plan aims to organize vehicle movements and
prevent accidents caused by congestion or improper traffic flow. It includes the following:
 − Designated vehicle routes, traffic control measures (e.g., signs, barriers), and safe pedestrian
   crossings.
 − Speed limits and zones of restricted access for certain vehicles or workers.

 − Considerations for emergency vehicle access.

 − The Road Safety Plan ensures proper signage and road markings, including areas with heavy
   construction equipment or where the road may be temporarily closed.

Site induction brief

Site Induction Brief is essential for ensuring all new workers and visitors are made aware of
potential hazards before entering the worksite. It includes:

 − General Safety Information: Overview of the project, location-specific risks, and emergency
   procedures.
 − Worksite Specific Hazards: Potential risks like machinery, high-exposure work areas (e.g.,
   near water, excavation), and chemical hazards.
 − Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): Clear instructions on the mandatory PPE required for
   specific tasks (e.g., gloves, safety helmets, goggles).
 − Emergency Evacuation: Escape routes, assembly points, and emergency exits.


Permits to work (e.g., for excavations, hot works)

The Permits to Work system ensures that high-risk activities are carried out safely. It includes:

 − Hot Work Permit: For activities like welding or cutting, ensure adequate ventilation and fire
   safety measures.
 − Excavation Permit: Ensures safe digging practices, checks for underground utilities, and
   assesses soil stability.
 − Confined Space Entry Permit: Covers safety checks for workers entering confined spaces,
   ensuring proper ventilation and emergency retrieval measures.
 − Electrical Work Permit: Ensures safe procedures for working with electricity, including
   isolation and lockout/tagout.

Driver rules and regulations, covering:

 − Speed Limits: Maximum speeds for vehicles operating on-site, including restricted areas.

 − Parking Regulations: Designated parking areas to prevent traffic disruptions and accidents.

 − Pedestrian and Vehicle Interaction: Guidelines for maintaining safe distances between
   vehicles and workers.

Accident and incident reporting and investigation: Reporting on accidents, incidents, and
near-misses on the worksite includes:

 − Accident Details: Date, time, location, people involved, and description of the incident.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         295
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
 − Immediate   Actions Taken: First-aid administered, emergency response actions, and
   treatment received.
 − Investigation: Root cause analysis and corrective actions to prevent recurrence.


Emergency procedures checklist: Checklist outlines procedures for different types of
emergencies on the site:

 − Fire Safety: Evacuation routes, fire extinguisher locations, and fire safety training.

 − Medical Emergencies: Contact information for emergency medical services and procedures
   for treating injuries.
 − Natural Disasters: Actions to take during floods, earthquakes, or extreme weather conditions.


Signage (e.g., road, safety signs). It includes:

 − Hazard Signs: Clear and visible signs indicating dangerous areas (e.g., wet floors, electrical
   hazards).
 − Traffic Signs: Speed limits, pedestrian crossings, and equipment zones.

 − Emergency Information: Locations of first-aid kits, emergency exits, and evacuation plans.

                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

APPENDIX 9: EMERGENCY REPONSE PLAN (ERP)
1.   Introduction

The Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) is the primary document for
identifying, assessing, and managing the environmental and social risks and impacts associated
with the project. A principal output of the ESIA is the Environmental and Social Management
Plan (ESMP), which sets out high-level mitigation measures and actions to manage
environmental and social risks and impacts, together with the project’s Occupational Health
and Safety (OHS) arrangements. Across the pre-construction, construction and operational
phases, an Emergency Response Plan (ERP) must also be established and implemented to
prevent and respond to potential environmental and social incidents. ERPs should be site-
specific, prepared and implemented by the relevant contractors, and must comply with the
requirements of the ESMP. This Annex provides guidance for the preparation and
implementation of all site-specific ERPs.

2.   Project Description
The proposed project investments are briefly described below:
  − Upgrading the Rach Goc fishing port, including: Expanding the existing wharves by a total
    of 150 m (two sections, each 75 m long, 14 m wide, designed with a top elevation at +2.50m,
    bottom elevation of –5.00 m; Dredging 10,446 m² of water area in front of the port with
    dimensions of 25 m wide, 270 m long, and 5 m deep; Installing 2 new roofs at the sorting
    areas of each new wharf section, dimensions of 29 m length, 9 m wide, 7.2 m high, and
    providing a 4.5 m high clearance; and Building a 150m new drainage ditch (cross section 40
    x 40cm) to improve the port’s drainage system.
  − Upgrading 76.72km of embankments combined with rural roads. Design with crest
    elevations ranging from 1.8–2.5m, road surface widths of 3–4.5m, and total roadbed widths
    of 4–6m. Along the embankment routes 940 reinforced concrete panels will be installed at
    the top of the existing water intakes for shrimp ponds. The embankments have 220 expanded
    cross-sections to allow vehicle passing or U-turns. Ancillary equipment (such as pavement
    markings, reflective signs, steel posts, load limit signs, etc.) will also be installed to ensure
    traffic safety during the operation phase of the embankments.
  − Dredging 14.81 km along 6 canal routes designed with a bottom width of 10 m and a bottom
    depth ranging from –2.0 to –2.5 m.
  − Installing 4 water monitoring stations. More details are not available at the time this ESIA
    is prepared.
In addition, the project will also provide technical assistance for technological innovation,
capacity building in marine fishing, and brackish shrimp farming.
3.   Objectives of the Plan
The objective of this plan is to identify, assess and manage potential risks and incidents; to
prioritize protection of human life; to limit the spread and severity of incidents; to ensure legal
compliance; and to facilitate coordinated multi-agency responses. The ERP objectives set out
in this Annex are intended to guide contractors on response times, containment timeframes for
major incidents, protection of sensitive receptors, and measures to limit pollution to receiving
waters, etc.
The project will prepare one Emergency Response Plan (ERP) for each component, with two
versions per component in one for Construction Phase, and one for Operation Phase.

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               Page          302
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
Each ERP will be a single, self-contained document that consolidates all incident-specific
protocols (oil spills, chemical releases, UXO, structural failure, flood/storm, fire/explosion,
traffic incidents, community safety/SEA-SH, public health, sediment pollution from dredging,
etc.) together with common cross-cutting elements: roles and responsibilities, communications
and reporting, resources and equipment, training and drills, remediation and post-incident
monitoring, and recovery/compensation actions. This will make implementation clearer and
faster for PMU, CSCs, contractors and operation units.
4.   Structure of each ERP (recommended Table of Contents)
 a). Purpose, scope and applicability (site, phase, linked documents: ESMP, ESCP, OHS Plan,
     Contractor ESMP)
 b). Incident classification and triggers
 c). Incident categories (list of prioritized incidents)
 d). Command & control (simplified Incident Command System for the site)
 e). Roles & responsibilities (PMU, CSC, Contractor, Port Management, Local Authorities,
     responders)
 f). Immediate actions and safety priorities (life safety → containment → prevent spread →
     evidence)
 g). Consolidated response protocols (for each incident type):
       − Oil spills (containment, recovery, transfer & disposal)

       − Hazardous chemical release (isolate, PPE, neutralization)

       − UXO discovery (stop-work, cordon, notify EOD)

       − Structural failure / embankment breach (evacuate, contain, protect ponds)

       − Fire/explosion (evacuation, firefighting, mutual aid)

       − Vessel collision / sinking (rescue and pollution control)

       − Sediment/turbidity event from dredging (stop works, turbidity barrier, water sampling)

       − Public health incident (disease outbreak response)

       − Community     conflict/SEA-SH allegation (immediate protective measures, referral,
        reporting)
 h). Communications & reporting protocols (internal, authorities, WB/ESCP, community):
     standardized templates and timelines
 i). Containment, cleanup, transport and disposal procedures (detailed for oil, contaminated
     sediments, hazardous waste)
 j). Investigation, corrective & preventive actions (root-cause analysis, corrective and
     prevention actions tracking)
 k). Post-incident monitoring, remediation and livelihood restoration (environmental
     monitoring plan, compensation procedures)
 l). Resources & equipment list (site packing list + contractual arrangements)
 m).    Training, drills and readiness (frequency & evaluation)
 n). Record keeping and documentation (incident file contents, retention)
 o). Annexes: contact lists, incident report templates, SOP checklists, maps, equipment
     suppliers, mutual-aid agreements, insurance details.


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page        303
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

5.   Standardized reporting timelines
      − Immediate verbal notification (within 24 hours) to PMU/CSC and, for serious incidents,
        to the World Bank (via PMU).
      − Initial written report (within 48 hours): facts, immediate actions, impacts, containment
        measures.
      − Detailed investigation and corrective and prevention actions (within 7–10 days): root
        cause, corrective actions, responsible parties, timetable.
      − Closure  and verification report: when remedial works complete and monitoring
        confirms recovery.
These timelines will be included as mandatory contractual requirements.
6.   Consolidated incident protocols - example highlights (what to include in “single”
     protocol)
      − Oil spill protocol: activation thresholds, containment (booms/sorbents), recovery &
        storage, transfer to licensed hazardous-waste handler, shoreline cleanup procedures,
        environmental monitoring schedule, compensation process.
      − Dredging  turbidity event: immediate halt trigger, installation of turbidity curtains,
        upstream/downstream sampling protocol, notification to authorities and adjacent
        aquaculture owners, remedial dredging/cleanout plan.
      − Embankment breach: immediate evacuation, isolation of affected ponds, emergency
        pumps, temporary bunds, repair & geotechnical remediation, livelihood compensation
        if ponds are lost.
      − SEA/SH:   immediate safeguarding measures, confidential reporting and referral
        pathways, medical support and psychosocial counseling; all aligned with project
        SEA/SH action plan.
Each protocol will contain: purpose, triggers, stepwise actions, equipment list, communications
template, responsibilities, post-incident monitoring.
7.   Roles and Responsibilities
The ERP will explicitly state who does what:
      − PMU/Project Director: overall incident governance, liaising with regulators and the
        WB, approving major emergency expenditure, coordinating compensation and public
        communications.
      − CSC:   verify contractor response, lead independent            verification,   supervise
        environmental sampling and technical investigations.
      − Contractor / Site Manager: on-site immediate response, first aid, containment, initial
        reporting, implement corrective and prevention actions.
      − Port Management / Operation Team: operation-phase responses (vessel incidents, oil
        spills), maintain operational response equipment.
      − Local   Authorities and Emergency Services: provide rescue, firefighting, medical
        referrals; contractual mutual-aid arrangements to be documented.
      − Community Liaison Officer: manage community notifications and GRM interface.

8.   Equipment, mutual aid and contractual arrangements




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page         304
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
        − Maintain a minimum equipment inventory on-site (booms, sorbents, pumps, PPE, first-
         aid, spill kits, turbidity curtains).
        − Pre-approve service contracts with hazardous-waste handlers, oil-spill response
         companies, and salvage/rescue providers.
        − Ensureinsurance coverage and financial provision for emergency response and
         compensation. PMU will verify contracts before mobilization.
9.    Integration with ESMP, ESCP and GRM
        − Each ERP will be explicitly cross-referenced to ESMP measures, ESCP reporting
         obligations, and the GRM.
        − Incident report templates will include GRM linkage and SEA/SH confidential reporting
         channels.
        − PMU will retain consolidated incident registry across all components.

10. Implementation steps and timeline (recommended)
        − Prepare CERP templates for each component area (port / embankments / canals) —
         within 4 weeks.
        − Contractor-specific CERP: contractor completes site-specific CERP based on template
         and submit to CSC/PMU for approval before mobilization.
        − Equipment & contractual arrangements: secure mutual-aid and disposal contracts —
         before start of relevant works.
        − Training & first drill: contractor conducts induction and first tabletop drill within 2
         weeks of mobilization.
        − Annual review: PMU-led review and update; lessons from drills/incidents to be
         incorporated.
11. Emergency Response Plan (Erp) template
Component: Rach Goc Fishing Port – Construction Phase
Project: Sustainable Fisheries Development Project (SFDP) – Ca Mau Province
Contractor: ___________________________
Contract Package: _____________________
Date of Submission: _____
Revision No.: _______
a). Purpose and scope
This Emergency Response Plan (ERP) establishes the procedures, responsibilities, resources,
and communication protocols required to respond effectively to emergencies that may occur
during the construction of the Rach Goc Fishing Port.
This ERP covers all incidents that may pose risks to worker and community safety, the
environment, property, or continuity of construction activities. The Plan applies to:
     − The Contractor and all subcontractors

     − Supervision engineers and site management

     − Visitors and third-party service providers




Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page         305
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
This document supplements and aligns with the ESMP, OHS Management Plan, Labor
Management Procedures (LMP), SEA/SH Action Plan, and ESCP.
b). Legal and project references
The ERP shall comply with:
   − Vietnam Law on Environmental Protection (2020)

   − Law on Fire Prevention and Fighting

   − Maritime Navigation Safety Regulations

   − SFDP-ESMP requirements

   − World Bank ESF – ESS1, ESS2, ESS3, ESS4, ESS10

   − Relevant technical regulations (QCVN)

   − (Contractor may add additional legal references here.)

c). Types of emergencies covered
This ERP consolidates all emergency types into one unified response framework. Incident types
include:
   − Environmental Incidents: Oil and fuel spills on land or water; Chemical spills / hazardous
    material leaks; Sediment/turbidity release into waterways from construction; Wastewater
    discharge incident; Air emissions, dust, or smoke incident
   − Health & Safety Incidents:mWorker injury or fatality; Traffic accident involving
    construction vehicles; Fire or explosion; Structural failure (temporary works, formwork,
    scaffolding); Fall from height; Drowning or waterborne accident
   − Physical & Cultural Resource Incidents: Chance finds of cultural artefacts; UXO discovery

   − Community and Social Incidents: Community injury; GBV, SEA/SH allegation; Violence,
    conflict, strike, or security incident; Public health emergency (infectious disease outbreak)
d). Incident classification
To standardize reporting, incidents are classified into three levels:
   − Level 1 – Minor Incident

      ▪ No injury or minor first-aid case
      ▪ Spill <20 liters, fully contained on site
      ▪ No off-site impact
      ▪ Can be managed by contractor EHS staff
   − Level 2 – Serious Incident

      ▪ Injury requiring medical treatment
      ▪ Spill 20–200 liters or minor water contamination
      ▪ Temporary stop-work required
      ▪ Community complaint or minor property damage
   − Level 3 – Major / Critical Incident

      ▪ Fatality or severe injury
      ▪ Spill >200 liters, water pollution, oil spill to river
      ▪ Fire, explosion, structural failure

Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            Page          306
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

       ▪ Major community harm
       ▪ Any incident requiring notification to authorities or the World Bank via PMU

f). Emergency response procedures (unified protocol)
Immediate Actions (for all emergencies):
   − Stop work and secure the area

   − Protect life and prevent injury

   − Initiate alarm and notify Site Manager

   − Contain hazard (spill containment, isolate electrical source, evacuation, etc.)

   − Provide first aid

   − Prevent the spread of pollution

   − Notify PMU and CSC within 1 hour for serious incidents

   − Maintain a log of actions taken

g). Incident-specific protocols
(Contractor must adapt to site conditions; minimum content below)
   − Oil or Fuel Spill (Land or Water)

       ▪ Deploy spill kits, booms, sorbents
       ▪ Stop leak at source
       ▪ Protect water bodies
       ▪ Recover contaminated soil/sorbents for disposal
       ▪ Conduct water quality sampling
       ▪ Record volume and cause of spill
Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              Page         307
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   − Fire or Explosion

      ▪ Raise alarm and evacuate
      ▪ Use appropriate fire extinguishers
      ▪ Contact local Fire Department
      ▪ Protect fuel storage area
      ▪ Prevent contaminated water from entering drains
   − Worker Injury / Fatality

      ▪ Provide first aid; call ambulance
      ▪ Secure scene (no disturbance until investigation)
      ▪ Notify PMU and CSC within 1 hour
      ▪ Record witness statements
   − Water Pollution / Turbidity Event

      ▪ Stop related works immediately
      ▪ Install turbidity curtain
      ▪ Take upstream/downstream water samples
      ▪ Notify affected aquaculture operators
      ▪ Implement corrective measures
   − GBV/SEA/SH Incident

      ▪ Ensure survivor safety and confidentiality
      ▪ Follow GBV/SEA/SH Action Plan
      ▪ Provide immediate referral to GBV service provider
      ▪ Strict non-retaliation
      ▪ Confidential reporting to PMU
h). Communication an Reporting Protocol
   − Internal Notification Sequence

      ▪ Site Manager
      ▪ Safety Officer
      ▪ Environmental Officer
      ▪ Contractor Project Manager
      ▪ CSC
      ▪ PMU Environmental & Social Unit
   − Reporting Timelines

      ▪ Verbal notification: within 24 hours (Level 2–3)
      ▪ Initial written incident report: within 48 hours
      ▪ Detailed investigation & CAPA: within 7–10 days
      ▪ Closure report: upon completion of remediation & monitoring
   − External Notification: PMU will notify:


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                      Page   308
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

      ▪ Department of Agriculture & Environment (DAE)
      ▪ Fisheries and Waterway Authorities
      ▪ Fire Police (if applicable)
      ▪ World Bank (for Level 3 incidents)
i). Equipment and Resource Requirements
(Contractor must list exact quantities)
Minimum Required Equipment On-Site:
   − Oil spill response kit (booms, pads, sorbents)

   − Floating booms for water spill containment

   − Fire extinguishers (A, B, C types)

   − First aid kits

   − Turbidity curtains for in-water works

   − Pumps and hoses

   − Emergency lighting & generators

   − PPE: gloves, respirators, boots

   − Emergency contact board and maps

j). Training, Drills and Readiness
   − Induction training: all workers before entering site

   − Monthly toolbox trainings: spill response, fire safety, rescue

   − Quarterly tabletop drill: simulate incident response

   − Biannual full-scale drills:

   − One oil spill drill

   − One fire/evacuation drill

   − Drill reports must include findings, corrective actions, and lessons learned.

k). Post-Incident Actions
   − Environmental monitoring at affected areas

   − Corrective and preventive actions (CAPA)

   − Stakeholder notification if community impacted

   − Compensation (if required) in coordination with PMU

   − Update the ERP based on lessons learned

l). Documentation and Record Keeping
The contractor must maintain:
   − Incident register

   − Training and drill logs

   − Equipment inspection records

   − Chemical and fuel inventory

   − Communications and reporting files


Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                             Page    309
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   − CAPA implementation records

Retention: minimum 5 years or as required by PMU.
